title: Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye) - Symptom Guide
content_text: Patients with Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye) commonly present with the following symptoms: Redness in the white of the eye; increased tear production; itchy or burning eyes; discharge (watery, mucous, or pus-like); blurred vision; crusting of eyelids. Symptom severity and combination vary by individual and disease stage.
symptoms: Redness in the white of the eye; increased tear production; itchy or burning eyes; discharge (watery, mucous, or pus-like); blurred vision; crusting of eyelids
treatments: Viral conjunctivitis is managed with supportive care (cool compresses, artificial tears). Bacterial conjunctivitis may require antibiotic eye drops. Allergic conjunctivitis is treated with antihistamine eye drops.
risk_factors: Close contact with infected individuals; allergies; contact lens use; exposure to irritants; recent upper respiratory infection
prevention: Frequent handwashing, avoiding touching or rubbing the eyes, not sharing personal items such as towels, and proper contact lens hygiene.

title: Urinary Tract Infection - Clinical Decision Support Summary
content_text: Clinical decision support summary for Urinary Tract Infection (ICD-10: N39). Category: Infectious. Typical severity: Mild to Moderate. Chronicity: typically an acute (short-term) condition. Transmission risk: not considered contagious and does not spread between people. Primary risk factors: Female anatomy; sexual activity; certain types of birth control; menopause; urinary tract abnormalities; catheter use; weakened immune system. Recommended initial management: Short course of antibiotics (e.g., nitrofurantoin, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) tailored to local resistance patterns. Increased fluid intake supports recovery.
symptoms: Strong, persistent urge to urinate; burning sensation during urination; cloudy or strong-smelling urine; pelvic pain in women; frequent, small amounts of urine
treatments: Short course of antibiotics (e.g., nitrofurantoin, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) tailored to local resistance patterns. Increased fluid intake supports recovery.
risk_factors: Female anatomy; sexual activity; certain types of birth control; menopause; urinary tract abnormalities; catheter use; weakened immune system
prevention: Adequate hydration, urinating after intercourse, proper hygiene practices, and avoiding irritating feminine products.
common_age_group: Women of all ages, older adults

title: What are the common symptoms of Major Depressive Disorder?
content_text: Q: What are the common symptoms of Major Depressive Disorder? A: Common symptoms include persistent sad or empty mood; loss of interest in hobbies; fatigue or low energy; difficulty concentrating; changes in appetite or weight; sleep disturbances; thoughts of death or suicide.
symptoms: Persistent sad or empty mood; loss of interest in hobbies; fatigue or low energy; difficulty concentrating; changes in appetite or weight; sleep disturbances; thoughts of death or suicide
treatments: Psychotherapy (cognitive behavioral therapy, interpersonal therapy) and/or antidepressant medications (SSRIs, SNRIs). Severe cases may require combination therapy or, rarely, electroconvulsive therapy.
risk_factors: Family history of depression; history of trauma or abuse; chronic illness; substance use; major life changes or stress; certain medications
prevention: Stress management, strong social support networks, regular physical activity, adequate sleep, and early intervention for at-risk individuals.

title: Is Essential Hypertension contagious?
content_text: Q: Is Essential Hypertension contagious? A: Based on current medical guidance, Essential Hypertension is not contagious and does not spread between people.
symptoms: Often asymptomatic; occasional headaches; shortness of breath; nosebleeds; dizziness in severe cases
treatments: Lifestyle changes (DASH diet, sodium reduction, exercise, weight management) combined with antihypertensive medications such as ACE inhibitors, ARBs, calcium channel blockers, or thiazide diuretics depending on patient profile.
risk_factors: High sodium intake; obesity; sedentary lifestyle; excessive alcohol consumption; chronic stress; family history; aging
prevention: Reducing dietary sodium, maintaining healthy weight, limiting alcohol, regular aerobic exercise, and managing stress.
source_name: American Heart Association - High Blood Pressure

title: What is Psoriasis?
content_text: Q: What is Psoriasis? A: Psoriasis is a chronic autoimmune condition that causes rapid buildup of skin cells, resulting in scaling, inflammation, and thick, silvery, scaly patches, often on the elbows, knees, and scalp.
symptoms: Red patches of skin covered with thick, silvery scales; dry, cracked skin that may bleed; itching, burning, or soreness; thickened or ridged nails; stiff and swollen joints (in psoriatic arthritis)
treatments: Topical treatments (corticosteroids, vitamin D analogs) for mild cases, phototherapy for moderate cases, and systemic agents or biologics for moderate-to-severe disease.
risk_factors: Family history of psoriasis; smoking; obesity; stress; certain infections; certain medications (e.g., lithium, beta-blockers)
prevention: No definitive prevention exists; managing triggers such as stress, smoking, and certain medications may reduce flare frequency.

title: Who is most commonly affected by Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye)?
content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye)? A: Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye) most commonly affects All ages, common in children.
symptoms: Redness in the white of the eye; increased tear production; itchy or burning eyes; discharge (watery, mucous, or pus-like); blurred vision; crusting of eyelids
treatments: Viral conjunctivitis is managed with supportive care (cool compresses, artificial tears). Bacterial conjunctivitis may require antibiotic eye drops. Allergic conjunctivitis is treated with antihistamine eye drops.
risk_factors: Close contact with infected individuals; allergies; contact lens use; exposure to irritants; recent upper respiratory infection
prevention: Frequent handwashing, avoiding touching or rubbing the eyes, not sharing personal items such as towels, and proper contact lens hygiene.

title: What causes or increases the risk of Generalized Anxiety Disorder?
content_text: Q: What causes or increases the risk of Generalized Anxiety Disorder? A: Key risk factors include family history of anxiety disorders; history of trauma; chronic health conditions; personality traits; substance use.
symptoms: Excessive worry; restlessness; fatigue; difficulty concentrating; irritability; muscle tension; sleep disturbances
treatments: Cognitive behavioral therapy is highly effective; medications such as SSRIs, SNRIs, or buspirone may be used. Relaxation techniques and lifestyle changes support management.
risk_factors: Family history of anxiety disorders; history of trauma; chronic health conditions; personality traits; substance use
prevention: Stress management techniques, regular physical activity, limiting caffeine and alcohol, and adequate sleep hygiene.

title: Migraine - Treatment Guideline
content_text: Clinical management of Migraine typically follows a stepwise approach. Acute treatment includes NSAIDs or triptans for moderate to severe attacks. Preventive therapy (beta-blockers, anticonvulsants, CGRP inhibitors) is considered for frequent migraines. Treatment plans should be individualized based on patient comorbidities, preferences, and response to therapy. Clinical management of Migraine typically follows a stepwise approach. Acute treatment includes NSAIDs or triptans for moderate to severe attacks. Preventive therapy (beta-blockers, anticonvulsants, CGRP inhibitors) is considered for frequent migraines. Treatment plans should be individualized based on patient comorbidities, preferences, and response to therapy.
symptoms: Throbbing or pulsing headache, often one-sided; nausea and vomiting; sensitivity to light and sound; visual disturbances (aura); dizziness
treatments: Acute treatment includes NSAIDs or triptans for moderate to severe attacks. Preventive therapy (beta-blockers, anticonvulsants, CGRP inhibitors) is considered for frequent migraines.
risk_factors: Family history of migraine; hormonal changes (especially in women); stress; certain foods or food additives; changes in sleep patterns; sensory stimuli
prevention: Identifying and avoiding personal triggers, maintaining regular sleep and meal schedules, stress reduction techniques, and preventive medication when prescribed.

title: What causes or increases the risk of Coronary Artery Disease?
content_text: Q: What causes or increases the risk of Coronary Artery Disease? A: Key risk factors include high cholesterol; high blood pressure; smoking; diabetes; obesity; family history of heart disease; physical inactivity; age.
symptoms: Chest pain or discomfort (angina); shortness of breath; fatigue; pain in arms or shoulders; cold sweats; nausea
treatments: Lifestyle changes, statins for cholesterol management, antiplatelet agents (aspirin), beta-blockers, and in severe cases, procedures such as angioplasty with stenting or coronary artery bypass grafting.
risk_factors: High cholesterol; high blood pressure; smoking; diabetes; obesity; family history of heart disease; physical inactivity; age
prevention: Heart-healthy diet, regular exercise, smoking cessation, cholesterol and blood pressure management, and routine cardiovascular screening.

title: How is Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis) treated?
content_text: Q: How is Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis) treated? A: Regular moisturizing, topical corticosteroids for flare-ups, antihistamines for itching, and identification/avoidance of triggers. Severe cases may require systemic immunomodulators.
symptoms: Dry, scaly skin; intense itching; red to brownish-gray patches; small raised bumps that may leak fluid when scratched; thickened, cracked skin
treatments: Regular moisturizing, topical corticosteroids for flare-ups, antihistamines for itching, and identification/avoidance of triggers. Severe cases may require systemic immunomodulators.
risk_factors: Family history of eczema, asthma, or hay fever; dry skin; environmental irritants; stress; certain climates
prevention: Regular moisturizing, avoiding harsh soaps and known irritants, using lukewarm water for bathing, and wearing soft, breathable fabrics.
common_age_group: Children, with many cases persisting into adulthood

title: What causes or increases the risk of Malaria?
content_text: Q: What causes or increases the risk of Malaria? A: Key risk factors include living in or traveling to malaria-endemic regions; lack of access to preventive measures (bed nets, prophylaxis); pregnancy; young age.
symptoms: Fever and chills; headache; nausea and vomiting; muscle pain and fatigue; sweating; in severe cases, confusion, seizures, or difficulty breathing
treatments: Artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs) are the standard treatment for uncomplicated malaria. Severe malaria requires hospitalization and intravenous antimalarial drugs.
risk_factors: Living in or traveling to malaria-endemic regions; lack of access to preventive measures (bed nets, prophylaxis); pregnancy; young age
prevention: Use of insecticide-treated bed nets, indoor residual spraying, antimalarial prophylaxis for travelers, and prompt diagnosis and treatment of cases.
common_age_group: Children under 5 in endemic regions; travelers

title: How is Hypothyroidism treated?
content_text: Q: How is Hypothyroidism treated? A: Lifelong thyroid hormone replacement therapy (levothyroxine), with regular monitoring of thyroid hormone levels to adjust dosage.
symptoms: Fatigue; weight gain; cold intolerance; dry skin; constipation; hair thinning; depression; muscle weakness; slowed heart rate
treatments: Lifelong thyroid hormone replacement therapy (levothyroxine), with regular monitoring of thyroid hormone levels to adjust dosage.
risk_factors: Female sex; age over 60; family history of thyroid disease; autoimmune conditions; previous thyroid surgery or radiation treatment
prevention: No specific prevention exists for autoimmune causes; adequate dietary iodine intake helps prevent iodine-deficiency-related hypothyroidism.

title: What is Urinary Tract Infection?
content_text: Q: What is Urinary Tract Infection? A: Urinary Tract Infection is an infection affecting any part of the urinary system, most commonly the bladder and urethra, typically caused by bacteria entering the urinary tract.
symptoms: Strong, persistent urge to urinate; burning sensation during urination; cloudy or strong-smelling urine; pelvic pain in women; frequent, small amounts of urine
treatments: Short course of antibiotics (e.g., nitrofurantoin, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) tailored to local resistance patterns. Increased fluid intake supports recovery.
risk_factors: Female anatomy; sexual activity; certain types of birth control; menopause; urinary tract abnormalities; catheter use; weakened immune system
prevention: Adequate hydration, urinating after intercourse, proper hygiene practices, and avoiding irritating feminine products.
common_age_group: Women of all ages, older adults

title: Asthma - Prevention Guide
content_text: Prevention strategies for Asthma focus on modifiable risk factors. Avoiding known allergens and irritants, controlling indoor air quality, getting recommended vaccinations, and adherence to prescribed controller therapy. Individuals with additional risk factors (Family history of asthma or allergies; exposure to tobacco smoke; air pollution; occupational exposure to irritants; respiratory infections in childhood) should consult a healthcare provider for personalized guidance.
symptoms: Wheezing; shortness of breath; chest tightness; coughing, especially at night or early morning; difficulty sleeping due to breathing problems
treatments: Controller medications (inhaled corticosteroids, long-acting beta-agonists) for long-term control, plus short-acting bronchodilators (e.g., albuterol) for acute symptom relief. Avoidance of known triggers is essential.
risk_factors: Family history of asthma or allergies; exposure to tobacco smoke; air pollution; occupational exposure to irritants; respiratory infections in childhood
prevention: Avoiding known allergens and irritants, controlling indoor air quality, getting recommended vaccinations, and adherence to prescribed controller therapy.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood

title: Allergic Rhinitis - Symptom Guide
content_text: Patients with Allergic Rhinitis commonly present with the following symptoms: Sneezing; runny or stuffy nose; itchy or watery eyes; itchy throat; postnasal drip; fatigue. Symptom severity and combination vary by individual and disease stage.
symptoms: Sneezing; runny or stuffy nose; itchy or watery eyes; itchy throat; postnasal drip; fatigue
treatments: Antihistamines, intranasal corticosteroids, decongestants for short-term relief, and allergen immunotherapy for long-term management of severe cases.
risk_factors: Family history of allergies; other allergic conditions (asthma, eczema); exposure to environmental allergens; early childhood exposure to tobacco smoke
prevention: Avoiding known allergens, using air purifiers, keeping windows closed during high pollen seasons, and regular cleaning to reduce dust mites.
common_age_group: All ages, often begins in childhood or adolescence

title: Peptic Ulcer Disease - Overview
content_text: A condition involving sores that develop on the lining of the stomach, lower esophagus, or small intestine, most commonly caused by Helicobacter pylori infection or long-term use of NSAIDs.
symptoms: Burning stomach pain; bloating; heartburn; nausea; intolerance to fatty foods; in severe cases, dark stools or vomiting blood
treatments: Antibiotic regimens to eradicate H. pylori when present, proton pump inhibitors to reduce stomach acid, and discontinuation of NSAIDs when possible.
risk_factors: H. pylori infection; regular use of NSAIDs; smoking; excessive alcohol consumption; stress (as a contributing factor)
prevention: Limiting NSAID use, avoiding excessive alcohol, not smoking, and prompt treatment of H. pylori infection.

title: Is Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease a chronic condition? A: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease is generally classified as a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management.
symptoms: Heartburn; regurgitation of food or sour liquid; chest pain; difficulty swallowing; chronic cough; sensation of a lump in the throat
treatments: Lifestyle modifications (weight loss, smaller meals, avoiding trigger foods) combined with proton pump inhibitors or H2 receptor blockers. Severe or refractory cases may require surgical intervention.
risk_factors: Obesity; hiatal hernia; pregnancy; smoking; certain medications; large meals or eating late at night
prevention: Avoiding large or fatty meals, not lying down immediately after eating, weight management, and limiting alcohol and caffeine intake.

title: What are the common symptoms of Epilepsy?
content_text: Q: What are the common symptoms of Epilepsy? A: Common symptoms include recurrent seizures (varying from brief staring spells to convulsions); temporary confusion; uncontrollable jerking movements; loss of consciousness; psychic symptoms such as fear or anxiety before a seizure.
symptoms: Recurrent seizures (varying from brief staring spells to convulsions); temporary confusion; uncontrollable jerking movements; loss of consciousness; psychic symptoms such as fear or anxiety before a seizure
treatments: Antiepileptic drugs (e.g., levetiracetam, valproate, lamotrigine) are the mainstay of treatment, with surgery, dietary therapy (ketogenic diet), or neurostimulation devices considered for drug-resistant cases.
risk_factors: Family history of epilepsy; brain injury or trauma; stroke; brain infections; developmental disorders; prenatal injury
prevention: Preventing head injuries, managing cardiovascular risk factors to reduce stroke risk, and prompt treatment of infections that can affect the brain.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood or after age 60

title: Is Iron Deficiency Anemia contagious?
content_text: Q: Is Iron Deficiency Anemia contagious? A: Based on current medical guidance, Iron Deficiency Anemia is not contagious and does not spread between people.
symptoms: Fatigue; pale skin; shortness of breath; dizziness; brittle nails; headaches; cold hands and feet; unusual cravings for non-nutritive substances (pica)
treatments: Oral or intravenous iron supplementation, dietary modification to increase iron-rich foods, and treatment of underlying causes such as bleeding sources.
risk_factors: Poor dietary iron intake; heavy menstrual periods; pregnancy; gastrointestinal bleeding; chronic conditions affecting absorption (e.g., celiac disease)
prevention: Eating iron-rich foods (lean meat, beans, fortified cereals), pairing with vitamin C for absorption, and screening in high-risk groups such as pregnant women.
common_age_group: Women of reproductive age; young children

title: Is Sleep Apnea contagious?
content_text: Q: Is Sleep Apnea contagious? A: Based on current medical guidance, Sleep Apnea is not contagious and does not spread between people.
symptoms: Loud snoring; episodes of stopped breathing observed by others; gasping for air during sleep; excessive daytime sleepiness; morning headaches; difficulty concentrating; irritability
treatments: Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) therapy is the standard treatment. Weight loss, positional therapy, oral appliances, and in select cases, surgery, may also be used.
risk_factors: Obesity; older age; male sex; large neck circumference; family history; smoking; nasal congestion; use of alcohol or sedatives
prevention: Maintaining healthy weight, avoiding alcohol and sedatives before sleep, sleeping on one's side, and treating nasal congestion.

title: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease - Overview
content_text: A digestive disorder in which stomach acid frequently flows back into the esophagus, irritating the lining and causing heartburn and other symptoms, potentially leading to complications if untreated.
symptoms: Heartburn; regurgitation of food or sour liquid; chest pain; difficulty swallowing; chronic cough; sensation of a lump in the throat
treatments: Lifestyle modifications (weight loss, smaller meals, avoiding trigger foods) combined with proton pump inhibitors or H2 receptor blockers. Severe or refractory cases may require surgical intervention.
risk_factors: Obesity; hiatal hernia; pregnancy; smoking; certain medications; large meals or eating late at night
prevention: Avoiding large or fatty meals, not lying down immediately after eating, weight management, and limiting alcohol and caffeine intake.

title: Influenza - Overview
content_text: A contagious respiratory illness caused by influenza viruses that infect the nose, throat, and lungs, ranging from mild to severe illness, and occasionally leading to death, particularly in high-risk groups.
symptoms: Fever or feeling feverish; chills; cough; sore throat; runny or stuffy nose; muscle or body aches; headaches; fatigue
treatments: Supportive care (rest, fluids, antipyretics) for most cases. Antiviral medications such as oseltamivir may be prescribed within 48 hours of symptom onset for high-risk patients.
risk_factors: Age under 5 or over 65; pregnancy; chronic medical conditions (asthma, diabetes, heart disease); weakened immune system; close contact with infected individuals
prevention: Annual influenza vaccination, frequent handwashing, avoiding close contact with sick individuals, and covering coughs and sneezes.

title: Is Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis) a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis) a chronic condition? A: Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis) is generally classified as a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management.
symptoms: Dry, scaly skin; intense itching; red to brownish-gray patches; small raised bumps that may leak fluid when scratched; thickened, cracked skin
treatments: Regular moisturizing, topical corticosteroids for flare-ups, antihistamines for itching, and identification/avoidance of triggers. Severe cases may require systemic immunomodulators.
risk_factors: Family history of eczema, asthma, or hay fever; dry skin; environmental irritants; stress; certain climates
prevention: Regular moisturizing, avoiding harsh soaps and known irritants, using lukewarm water for bathing, and wearing soft, breathable fabrics.
common_age_group: Children, with many cases persisting into adulthood

title: What causes or increases the risk of Tuberculosis?
content_text: Q: What causes or increases the risk of Tuberculosis? A: Key risk factors include close contact with an infected person; weakened immune system (e.g., HIV); living in or traveling to areas with high TB rates; healthcare work; substance use.
symptoms: Persistent cough lasting three weeks or longer, sometimes with blood; chest pain; unintentional weight loss; fatigue; fever; night sweats; loss of appetite
treatments: Standard treatment involves a 6-9 month course of multiple antibiotics (isoniazid, rifampin, ethambutol, pyrazinamide), with directly observed therapy recommended to ensure adherence and prevent drug resistance.
risk_factors: Close contact with an infected person; weakened immune system (e.g., HIV); living in or traveling to areas with high TB rates; healthcare work; substance use
prevention: BCG vaccination in countries with high TB prevalence, prompt identification and treatment of active cases, and infection control measures in healthcare settings.
common_age_group: All ages, higher burden in low-resource settings

title: Can Chickenpox (Varicella) be prevented?
content_text: Q: Can Chickenpox (Varicella) be prevented? A: Varicella vaccination, typically given in two doses during childhood, is highly effective at preventing infection or reducing severity.
symptoms: Itchy rash with fluid-filled blisters; fever; tiredness; loss of appetite; headache; the rash typically appears in waves over several days
treatments: Supportive care including antihistamines for itching, calamine lotion, and antipyretics. Antiviral medication (acyclovir) may be used for high-risk individuals or severe cases.
risk_factors: Lack of vaccination; close contact with an infected person; weakened immune system; age under 12 (unvaccinated)
prevention: Varicella vaccination, typically given in two doses during childhood, is highly effective at preventing infection or reducing severity.

title: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease - Prevention Guide
content_text: Prevention strategies for Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease focus on modifiable risk factors. Avoiding smoking and secondhand smoke, reducing occupational and environmental exposure to lung irritants, and vaccination against respiratory infections. Individuals with additional risk factors (Smoking (primary cause); long-term exposure to air pollutants or occupational dust/chemicals; genetic factors (alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency); history of childhood respiratory infections) should consult a healthcare provider for personalized guidance.
symptoms: Chronic cough with mucus production; shortness of breath, especially during physical activity; wheezing; chest tightness; frequent respiratory infections
treatments: Bronchodilators (short and long-acting), inhaled corticosteroids for frequent exacerbations, pulmonary rehabilitation, oxygen therapy in advanced stages, and smoking cessation as the most critical intervention.
risk_factors: Smoking (primary cause); long-term exposure to air pollutants or occupational dust/chemicals; genetic factors (alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency); history of childhood respiratory infections
prevention: Avoiding smoking and secondhand smoke, reducing occupational and environmental exposure to lung irritants, and vaccination against respiratory infections.

title: Is Influenza contagious?
content_text: Q: Is Influenza contagious? A: Based on current medical guidance, Influenza is contagious and can spread between people.
symptoms: Fever or feeling feverish; chills; cough; sore throat; runny or stuffy nose; muscle or body aches; headaches; fatigue
treatments: Supportive care (rest, fluids, antipyretics) for most cases. Antiviral medications such as oseltamivir may be prescribed within 48 hours of symptom onset for high-risk patients.
risk_factors: Age under 5 or over 65; pregnancy; chronic medical conditions (asthma, diabetes, heart disease); weakened immune system; close contact with infected individuals
prevention: Annual influenza vaccination, frequent handwashing, avoiding close contact with sick individuals, and covering coughs and sneezes.

title: Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident) - Treatment Guideline
content_text: Clinical management of Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident) typically follows a stepwise approach. Acute ischemic stroke may be treated with thrombolytic therapy (tPA) within a narrow time window or mechanical thrombectomy. Long-term management includes antiplatelet/anticoagulant therapy, blood pressure control, and rehabilitation. Treatment plans should be individualized based on patient comorbidities, preferences, and response to therapy.
symptoms: Sudden numbness or weakness of face, arm, or leg, especially on one side; sudden confusion or trouble speaking; sudden vision problems; sudden trouble walking or loss of balance; sudden severe headache
treatments: Acute ischemic stroke may be treated with thrombolytic therapy (tPA) within a narrow time window or mechanical thrombectomy. Long-term management includes antiplatelet/anticoagulant therapy, blood pressure control, and rehabilitation.
risk_factors: High blood pressure; smoking; diabetes; high cholesterol; atrial fibrillation; obesity; family history of stroke; age over 55
prevention: Controlling blood pressure, cholesterol, and blood sugar, smoking cessation, regular exercise, and managing atrial fibrillation when present.

title: What is Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident)?
content_text: Q: What is Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident)? A: Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident) is a medical emergency that occurs when blood supply to part of the brain is interrupted or reduced, depriving brain tissue of oxygen and nutrients, leading to brain cell death within minutes if not treated promptly.
symptoms: Sudden numbness or weakness of face, arm, or leg, especially on one side; sudden confusion or trouble speaking; sudden vision problems; sudden trouble walking or loss of balance; sudden severe headache
treatments: Acute ischemic stroke may be treated with thrombolytic therapy (tPA) within a narrow time window or mechanical thrombectomy. Long-term management includes antiplatelet/anticoagulant therapy, blood pressure control, and rehabilitation.
risk_factors: High blood pressure; smoking; diabetes; high cholesterol; atrial fibrillation; obesity; family history of stroke; age over 55
prevention: Controlling blood pressure, cholesterol, and blood sugar, smoking cessation, regular exercise, and managing atrial fibrillation when present.

title: Is Iron Deficiency Anemia a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Iron Deficiency Anemia a chronic condition? A: Iron Deficiency Anemia is generally classified as typically an acute (short-term) condition.
symptoms: Fatigue; pale skin; shortness of breath; dizziness; brittle nails; headaches; cold hands and feet; unusual cravings for non-nutritive substances (pica)
treatments: Oral or intravenous iron supplementation, dietary modification to increase iron-rich foods, and treatment of underlying causes such as bleeding sources.
risk_factors: Poor dietary iron intake; heavy menstrual periods; pregnancy; gastrointestinal bleeding; chronic conditions affecting absorption (e.g., celiac disease)

common_age_group: Women of reproductive age; young children

title: What are the common symptoms of Hyperlipidemia?
content_text: Q: What are the common symptoms of Hyperlipidemia? A: Common symptoms include usually asymptomatic; in severe cases, fatty deposits around the eyes or joints (xanthomas); occasionally associated with pancreatitis symptoms if triglycerides are very high.
symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; in severe cases, fatty deposits around the eyes or joints (xanthomas); occasionally associated with pancreatitis symptoms if triglycerides are very high
treatments: Lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, weight loss) as first-line therapy, with statins as the primary pharmacologic treatment; additional agents (ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors) for high-risk patients.
risk_factors: Diet high in saturated fats; obesity; physical inactivity; smoking; diabetes; family history of high cholesterol; age
prevention: Heart-healthy diet low in saturated and trans fats, regular physical activity, maintaining healthy weight, and routine lipid screening.

title: Epilepsy - Treatment Guideline

Antiepileptic drugs (e.g., levetiracetam, valproate, lamotrigine) are the mainstay of treatment, with surgery, dietary therapy (ketogenic diet), or neurostimulation devices considered for drug-resistant cases. Treatment plans should be individualized based on patient comorbidities, preferences, and response to therapy.

treatments: Antiepileptic drugs (e.g., levetiracetam, valproate, lamotrigine) are the mainstay of treatment, with surgery, dietary therapy (ketogenic diet), or neurostimulation devices considered for drug-resistant cases.
risk_factors: Family history of epilepsy; brain injury or trauma; stroke; brain infections; developmental disorders; prenatal injury
prevention: Preventing head injuries, managing cardiovascular risk factors to reduce stroke risk, and prompt treatment of infections that can affect the brain.

title: Is Pneumonia a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Pneumonia a chronic condition? A: Pneumonia is generally classified as typically an acute (short-term) condition.
symptoms: Cough with phlegm; fever; chills; shortness of breath; chest pain when breathing or coughing; fatigue; confusion (especially in older adults)
treatments: Bacterial pneumonia is treated with antibiotics; viral pneumonia is managed with supportive care and antivirals when appropriate. Hospitalization may be required for severe cases.
risk_factors: Age under 2 or over 65; chronic diseases (asthma, COPD, heart disease); weakened immune system; smoking; recent respiratory infection; hospitalization
prevention: Vaccination (pneumococcal, influenza), good hygiene, avoiding smoking, and managing underlying chronic conditions.

title: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease - Clinical Decision Support Summary
content_text: Clinical decision support summary for Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (ICD-10: K21). Category: Gastrointestinal. Typical severity: Mild to Moderate. Chronicity: a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management. Transmission risk: not considered contagious and does not spread between people. Primary risk factors: Obesity; hiatal hernia; pregnancy; smoking; certain medications; large meals or eating late at night. Severe or refractory cases may require surgical intervention.

risk_factors: Family history of eczema, asthma, or hay fever; dry skin; environmental irritants; stress; certain climates
prevention: Regular moisturizing, avoiding harsh soaps and known irritants, using lukewarm water for bathing, and wearing soft, breathable fabrics.
common_age_group: Children, with many cases persisting into adulthood

title: Is Rheumatoid Arthritis a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Rheumatoid Arthritis a chronic condition? A: Rheumatoid Arthritis is generally classified as a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management.
symptoms: Joint pain and swelling, often symmetrical; morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes; fatigue; low-grade fever; joint deformity in advanced stages
treatments: Disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs) such as methotrexate, biologic agents, NSAIDs for symptom relief, and physical therapy to maintain joint function.
risk_factors: Female sex; family history of autoimmune disease; smoking; obesity; age between 30 and 60
prevention: No definitive prevention exists, but smoking cessation and maintaining healthy weight may reduce risk; early diagnosis and treatment slow disease progression.

title: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease - Overview
content_text: A progressive lung disease characterized by chronic airflow limitation, primarily caused by long-term exposure to irritants such as cigarette smoke, leading to chronic bronchitis and/or emphysema.
symptoms: Chronic cough with mucus production; shortness of breath, especially during physical activity; wheezing; chest tightness; frequent respiratory infections
treatments: Bronchodilators (short and long-acting), inhaled corticosteroids for frequent exacerbations, pulmonary rehabilitation, oxygen therapy in advanced stages, and smoking cessation as the most critical intervention.
risk_factors: Smoking (primary cause); long-term exposure to air pollutants or occupational dust/chemicals; genetic factors (alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency); history of childhood respiratory infections
prevention: Avoiding smoking and secondhand smoke, reducing occupational and environmental exposure to lung irritants, and vaccination against respiratory infections.

title: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease - Treatment Guideline
content_text: Clinical management of Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease typically follows a stepwise approach. Bronchodilators (short and long-acting), inhaled corticosteroids for frequent exacerbations, pulmonary rehabilitation, oxygen therapy in advanced stages, and smoking cessation as the most critical intervention. Treatment plans should be individualized based on patient comorbidities, preferences, and response to therapy.
symptoms: Chronic cough with mucus production; shortness of breath, especially during physical activity; wheezing; chest tightness; frequent respiratory infections
treatments: Bronchodilators (short and long-acting), inhaled corticosteroids for frequent exacerbations, pulmonary rehabilitation, oxygen therapy in advanced stages, and smoking cessation as the most critical intervention.
risk_factors: Smoking (primary cause); long-term exposure to air pollutants or occupational dust/chemicals; genetic factors (alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency); history of childhood respiratory infections
prevention: Avoiding smoking and secondhand smoke, reducing occupational and environmental exposure to lung irritants, and vaccination against respiratory infections.

title: Hyperlipidemia - Symptom Guide
content_text: Patients with Hyperlipidemia commonly present with the following symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; in severe cases, fatty deposits around the eyes or joints (xanthomas); occasionally associated with pancreatitis symptoms if triglycerides are very high. Symptom severity and combination vary by individual and disease stage.
symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; in severe cases, fatty deposits around the eyes or joints (xanthomas); occasionally associated with pancreatitis symptoms if triglycerides are very high
treatments: Lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, weight loss) as first-line therapy, with statins as the primary pharmacologic treatment; additional agents (ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors) for high-risk patients.
risk_factors: Diet high in saturated fats; obesity; physical inactivity; smoking; diabetes; family history of high cholesterol; age
prevention: Heart-healthy diet low in saturated and trans fats, regular physical activity, maintaining healthy weight, and routine lipid screening.

title: Is Migraine a chronic condition? (Archived Copy)
content_text: Q: Is Migraine a chronic condition? A: Migraine is generally classified as a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management.
symptoms: Throbbing or pulsing headache, often one-sided; nausea and vomiting; sensitivity to light and sound; visual disturbances (aura); dizziness
treatments: Acute treatment includes NSAIDs or triptans for moderate to severe attacks. Preventive therapy (beta-blockers, anticonvulsants, CGRP inhibitors) is considered for frequent migraines.
risk_factors: Family history of migraine; hormonal changes (especially in women); stress; certain foods or food additives; changes in sleep patterns; sensory stimuli
prevention: Identifying and avoiding personal triggers, maintaining regular sleep and meal schedules, stress reduction techniques, and preventive medication when prescribed.

title: Iron Deficiency Anemia - Overview
content_text: A condition resulting from insufficient iron to produce adequate hemoglobin, leading to reduced oxygen-carrying capacity of the blood and a range of systemic symptoms. A condition resulting from insufficient iron to produce adequate hemoglobin, leading to reduced oxygen-carrying capacity of the blood and a range of systemic symptoms.
symptoms: Fatigue; pale skin; shortness of breath; dizziness; brittle nails; headaches; cold hands and feet; unusual cravings for non-nutritive substances (pica)
treatments: Oral or intravenous iron supplementation, dietary modification to increase iron-rich foods, and treatment of underlying causes such as bleeding sources.
risk_factors: Poor dietary iron intake; heavy menstrual periods; pregnancy; gastrointestinal bleeding; chronic conditions affecting absorption (e.g., celiac disease)
prevention: Eating iron-rich foods (lean meat, beans, fortified cereals), pairing with vitamin C for absorption, and screening in high-risk groups such as pregnant women.
common_age_group: Women of reproductive age; young children

title: Who is most commonly affected by Psoriasis?
content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Psoriasis? A: Psoriasis most commonly affects Adults 20-40.
symptoms: Red patches of skin covered with thick, silvery scales; dry, cracked skin that may bleed; itching, burning, or soreness; thickened or ridged nails; stiff and swollen joints (in psoriatic arthritis)
treatments: Topical treatments (corticosteroids, vitamin D analogs) for mild cases, phototherapy for moderate cases, and systemic agents or biologics for moderate-to-severe disease.
risk_factors: Family history of psoriasis; smoking; obesity; stress; certain infections; certain medications (e.g., lithium, beta-blockers)
prevention: No definitive prevention exists; managing triggers such as stress, smoking, and certain medications may reduce flare frequency.

title: Who is most commonly affected by Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease?
content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease? A: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease most commonly affects Adults 30+.
symptoms: Heartburn; regurgitation of food or sour liquid; chest pain; difficulty swallowing; chronic cough; sensation of a lump in the throat
treatments: Lifestyle modifications (weight loss, smaller meals, avoiding trigger foods) combined with proton pump inhibitors or H2 receptor blockers. Severe or refractory cases may require surgical intervention.
risk_factors: Obesity; hiatal hernia; pregnancy; smoking; certain medications; large meals or eating late at night
prevention: Avoiding large or fatty meals, not lying down immediately after eating, weight management, and limiting alcohol and caffeine intake.

title: Essential Hypertension - Prevention Guide
content_text: Prevention strategies for Essential Hypertension focus on modifiable risk factors. Reducing dietary sodium, maintaining healthy weight, limiting alcohol, regular aerobic exercise, and managing stress. Individuals with additional risk factors (High sodium intake; obesity; sedentary lifestyle; excessive alcohol consumption; chronic stress; family history; aging) should consult a healthcare provider for personalized guidance.
symptoms: Often asymptomatic; occasional headaches; shortness of breath; nosebleeds; dizziness in severe cases
treatments: Lifestyle changes (DASH diet, sodium reduction, exercise, weight management) combined with antihypertensive medications such as ACE inhibitors, ARBs, calcium channel blockers, or thiazide diuretics depending on patient profile.
risk_factors: High sodium intake; obesity; sedentary lifestyle; excessive alcohol consumption; chronic stress; family history; aging
prevention: Reducing dietary sodium, maintaining healthy weight, limiting alcohol, regular aerobic exercise, and managing stress.
source_name: American Heart Association - High Blood Pressure

title: What are the common symptoms of Allergic Rhinitis?
content_text: Q: What are the common symptoms of Allergic Rhinitis? A: Common symptoms include sneezing; runny or stuffy nose; itchy or watery eyes; itchy throat; postnasal drip; fatigue.
symptoms: Sneezing; runny or stuffy nose; itchy or watery eyes; itchy throat; postnasal drip; fatigue
treatments: Antihistamines, intranasal corticosteroids, decongestants for short-term relief, and allergen immunotherapy for long-term management of severe cases.
risk_factors: Family history of allergies; other allergic conditions (asthma, eczema); exposure to environmental allergens; early childhood exposure to tobacco smoke
prevention: Avoiding known allergens, using air purifiers, keeping windows closed during high pollen seasons, and regular cleaning to reduce dust mites.
common_age_group: All ages, often begins in childhood or adolescence

title: What are the common symptoms of Tuberculosis?
content_text: Q: What are the common symptoms of Tuberculosis? A: Common symptoms include persistent cough lasting three weeks or longer, sometimes with blood; chest pain; unintentional weight loss; fatigue; fever; night sweats; loss of appetite.
symptoms: Persistent cough lasting three weeks or longer, sometimes with blood; chest pain; unintentional weight loss; fatigue; fever; night sweats; loss of appetite
treatments: Standard treatment involves a 6-9 month course of multiple antibiotics (isoniazid, rifampin, ethambutol, pyrazinamide), with directly observed therapy recommended to ensure adherence and prevent drug resistance.
risk_factors: Close contact with an infected person; weakened immune system (e.g., HIV); living in or traveling to areas with high TB rates; healthcare work; substance use
prevention: BCG vaccination in countries with high TB prevalence, prompt identification and treatment of active cases, and infection control measures in healthcare settings.
common_age_group: All ages, higher burden in low-resource settings

title: Is Hyperlipidemia contagious?
content_text: Q: Is Hyperlipidemia contagious? A: Based on current medical guidance, Hyperlipidemia is not contagious and does not spread between people.
symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; in severe cases, fatty deposits around the eyes or joints (xanthomas); occasionally associated with pancreatitis symptoms if triglycerides are very high
treatments: Lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, weight loss) as first-line therapy, with statins as the primary pharmacologic treatment; additional agents (ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors) for high-risk patients.
risk_factors: Diet high in saturated fats; obesity; physical inactivity; smoking; diabetes; family history of high cholesterol; age
prevention: Heart-healthy diet low in saturated and trans fats, regular physical activity, maintaining healthy weight, and routine lipid screening.

title: Can Asthma be prevented?
content_text: Q: Can Asthma be prevented? A: Avoiding known allergens and irritants, controlling indoor air quality, getting recommended vaccinations, and adherence to prescribed controller therapy.
symptoms: Wheezing; shortness of breath; chest tightness; coughing, especially at night or early morning; difficulty sleeping due to breathing problems
treatments: Controller medications (inhaled corticosteroids, long-acting beta-agonists) for long-term control, plus short-acting bronchodilators (e.g., albuterol) for acute symptom relief. Avoidance of known triggers is essential.
risk_factors: Family history of asthma or allergies; exposure to tobacco smoke; air pollution; occupational exposure to irritants; respiratory infections in childhood
prevention: Avoiding known allergens and irritants, controlling indoor air quality, getting recommended vaccinations, and adherence to prescribed controller therapy.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood

title: Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye) - Overview
content_text: Inflammation or infection of the conjunctiva, the thin tissue covering the inner eyelid and white part of the eye, commonly caused by viruses, bacteria, or allergens.
symptoms: Redness in the white of the eye; increased tear production; itchy or burning eyes; discharge (watery, mucous, or pus-like); blurred vision; crusting of eyelids
treatments: Viral conjunctivitis is managed with supportive care (cool compresses, artificial tears). Bacterial conjunctivitis may require antibiotic eye drops. Allergic conjunctivitis is treated with antihistamine eye drops.
risk_factors: Close contact with infected individuals; allergies; contact lens use; exposure to irritants; recent upper respiratory infection
prevention: Frequent handwashing, avoiding touching or rubbing the eyes, not sharing personal items such as towels, and proper contact lens hygiene.

title: Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident) - Clinical Decision Support Summary
content_text: Clinical decision support summary for Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident) (ICD-10: I63). Category: Neurological. Typical severity: Severe. Chronicity: typically an acute (short-term) condition. Transmission risk: not considered contagious and does not spread between people. Primary risk factors: High blood pressure; smoking; diabetes; high cholesterol; atrial fibrillation; obesity; family history of stroke; age over 55. Recommended initial management: Acute ischemic stroke may be treated with thrombolytic therapy (tPA) within a narrow time window or mechanical thrombectomy. Long-term management includes antiplatelet/anticoagulant therapy, blood pressure control, and rehabilitation.
symptoms: Sudden numbness or weakness of face, arm, or leg, especially on one side; sudden confusion or trouble speaking; sudden vision problems; sudden trouble walking or loss of balance; sudden severe headache
treatments: Acute ischemic stroke may be treated with thrombolytic therapy (tPA) within a narrow time window or mechanical thrombectomy. Long-term management includes antiplatelet/anticoagulant therapy, blood pressure control, and rehabilitation.
risk_factors: High blood pressure; smoking; diabetes; high cholesterol; atrial fibrillation; obesity; family history of stroke; age over 55
prevention: Controlling blood pressure, cholesterol, and blood sugar, smoking cessation, regular exercise, and managing atrial fibrillation when present.

title: Urinary Tract Infection - Treatment Guideline
content_text: Clinical management of Urinary Tract Infection typically follows a stepwise approach. Short course of antibiotics (e.g., nitrofurantoin, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) tailored to local resistance patterns. Increased fluid intake supports recovery. Treatment plans should be individualized based on patient comorbidities, preferences, and response to therapy.
symptoms: Strong, persistent urge to urinate; burning sensation during urination; cloudy or strong-smelling urine; pelvic pain in women; frequent, small amounts of urine
treatments: Short course of antibiotics (e.g., nitrofurantoin, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) tailored to local resistance patterns. Increased fluid intake supports recovery.
risk_factors: Female anatomy; sexual activity; certain types of birth control; menopause; urinary tract abnormalities; catheter use; weakened immune system
prevention: Adequate hydration, urinating after intercourse, proper hygiene practices, and avoiding irritating feminine products.
common_age_group: Women of all ages, older adults

risk_factors: Family history of migraine; hormonal changes (especially in women); stress; certain foods or food additives; changes in sleep patterns; sensory stimuli

title: How is Celiac Disease treated?
content_text: Q: How is Celiac Disease treated? A: The only effective treatment is a strict, lifelong gluten-free diet. Nutritional supplementation may be needed to correct deficiencies (iron, vitamin D, B12, folate).
symptoms: Diarrhea or constipation; bloating and gas; abdominal pain; fatigue; weight loss; anemia; skin rash (dermatitis herpetiformis); in children, failure to thrive
treatments: The only effective treatment is a strict, lifelong gluten-free diet. Nutritional supplementation may be needed to correct deficiencies (iron, vitamin D, B12, folate).
risk_factors: Family history of celiac disease or autoimmune disorders; type 1 diabetes; Down syndrome; autoimmune thyroid disease
prevention: No known prevention; early diagnosis and dietary management prevent complications such as malnutrition and osteoporosis.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood or 20s-30s

title: What causes or increases the risk of Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis)?
content_text: Q: What causes or increases the risk of Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis)? A: Key risk factors include family history of eczema, asthma, or hay fever; dry skin; environmental irritants; stress; certain climates.
symptoms: Dry, scaly skin; intense itching; red to brownish-gray patches; small raised bumps that may leak fluid when scratched; thickened, cracked skin
treatments: Regular moisturizing, topical corticosteroids for flare-ups, antihistamines for itching, and identification/avoidance of triggers. Severe cases may require systemic immunomodulators.
risk_factors: Family history of eczema, asthma, or hay fever; dry skin; environmental irritants; stress; certain climates
prevention: Regular moisturizing, avoiding harsh soaps and known irritants, using lukewarm water for bathing, and wearing soft, breathable fabrics.
common_age_group: Children, with many cases persisting into adulthood

title: Is Major Depressive Disorder a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Major Depressive Disorder a chronic condition? A: Major Depressive Disorder is generally classified as a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management.
symptoms: Persistent sad or empty mood; loss of interest in hobbies; fatigue or low energy; difficulty concentrating; changes in appetite or weight; sleep disturbances; thoughts of death or suicide
treatments: Psychotherapy (cognitive behavioral therapy, interpersonal therapy) and/or antidepressant medications (SSRIs, SNRIs). Severe cases may require combination therapy or, rarely, electroconvulsive therapy.
risk_factors: Family history of depression; history of trauma or abuse; chronic illness; substance use; major life changes or stress; certain medications
prevention: Stress management, strong social support networks, regular physical activity, adequate sleep, and early intervention for at-risk individuals.

title: Who is most commonly affected by Sleep Apnea?
content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Sleep Apnea? A: Sleep Apnea most commonly affects Adults 40+, more common in men.
symptoms: Loud snoring; episodes of stopped breathing observed by others; gasping for air during sleep; excessive daytime sleepiness; morning headaches; difficulty concentrating; irritability
treatments: Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) therapy is the standard treatment. Weight loss, positional therapy, oral appliances, and in select cases, surgery, may also be used.
risk_factors: Obesity; older age; male sex; large neck circumference; family history; smoking; nasal congestion; use of alcohol or sedatives
prevention: Maintaining healthy weight, avoiding alcohol and sedatives before sleep, sleeping on one's side, and treating nasal congestion.

content_text: Patients with Asthma commonly present with the following symptoms: Wheezing; shortness of breath; chest tightness; coughing, especially at night or early morning; difficulty sleeping due to breathing problems. Symptom severity and combination vary by individual and disease stage.
symptoms: Wheezing; shortness of breath; chest tightness; coughing, especially at night or early morning; difficulty sleeping due to breathing problems
treatments: Controller medications (inhaled corticosteroids, long-acting beta-agonists) for long-term control, plus short-acting bronchodilators (e.g., albuterol) for acute symptom relief. Avoidance of known triggers is essential.
risk_factors: Family history of asthma or allergies; exposure to tobacco smoke; air pollution; occupational exposure to irritants; respiratory infections in childhood

title: What are the common symptoms of Coronary Artery Disease?
content_text: Q: What are the common symptoms of Coronary Artery Disease? A: Common symptoms include chest pain or discomfort (angina); shortness of breath; fatigue; pain in arms or shoulders; cold sweats; nausea.
symptoms: Chest pain or discomfort (angina); shortness of breath; fatigue; pain in arms or shoulders; cold sweats; nausea
treatments: Lifestyle changes, statins for cholesterol management, antiplatelet agents (aspirin), beta-blockers, and in severe cases, procedures such as angioplasty with stenting or coronary artery bypass grafting.
risk_factors: High cholesterol; high blood pressure; smoking; diabetes; obesity; family history of heart disease; physical inactivity; age
prevention: Heart-healthy diet, regular exercise, smoking cessation, cholesterol and blood pressure management, and routine cardiovascular screening.

title: Malaria - Treatment Guideline
content_text: Clinical management of Malaria typically follows a stepwise approach. Artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs) are the standard treatment for uncomplicated malaria. Severe malaria requires hospitalization and intravenous antimalarial drugs. Treatment plans should be individualized based on patient comorbidities, preferences, and response to therapy.
symptoms: Fever and chills; headache; nausea and vomiting; muscle pain and fatigue; sweating; in severe cases, confusion, seizures, or difficulty breathing
treatments: Artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs) are the standard treatment for uncomplicated malaria. Severe malaria requires hospitalization and intravenous antimalarial drugs.
risk_factors: Living in or traveling to malaria-endemic regions; lack of access to preventive measures (bed nets, prophylaxis); pregnancy; young age
prevention: Use of insecticide-treated bed nets, indoor residual spraying, antimalarial prophylaxis for travelers, and prompt diagnosis and treatment of cases.
common_age_group: Children under 5 in endemic regions; travelers

title: Who is most commonly affected by Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus?
content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus? A: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus most commonly affects Adults 45+.
symptoms: Increased thirst; frequent urination; fatigue; blurred vision; slow-healing sores; unexplained weight loss; numbness or tingling in hands or feet
treatments: First-line management includes lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, weight loss) and metformin. Additional agents such as SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 receptor agonists, sulfonylureas, or insulin are added based on glycemic targets and comorbidities.
risk_factors: Obesity; family history of diabetes; age over 45; physical inactivity; history of gestational diabetes; high blood pressure
prevention: Maintaining healthy body weight, regular physical activity (150 minutes/week), balanced diet low in refined sugars, and routine screening for at-risk individuals.

title: Influenza - Treatment Guideline
content_text: Clinical management of Influenza typically follows a stepwise approach. Supportive care (rest, fluids, antipyretics) for most cases. Antiviral medications such as oseltamivir may be prescribed within 48 hours of symptom onset for high-risk patients. Treatment plans should be individualized based on patient comorbidities, preferences, and response to therapy.
symptoms: Fever or feeling feverish; chills; cough; sore throat; runny or stuffy nose; muscle or body aches; headaches; fatigue
treatments: Supportive care (rest, fluids, antipyretics) for most cases. Antiviral medications such as oseltamivir may be prescribed within 48 hours of symptom onset for high-risk patients.
risk_factors: Age under 5 or over 65; pregnancy; chronic medical conditions (asthma, diabetes, heart disease); weakened immune system; close contact with infected individuals
prevention: Annual influenza vaccination, frequent handwashing, avoiding close contact with sick individuals, and covering coughs and sneezes.

title: Is Hypothyroidism contagious?
content_text: Q: Is Hypothyroidism contagious? A: Based on current medical guidance, Hypothyroidism is not contagious and does not spread between people.
symptoms: Fatigue; weight gain; cold intolerance; dry skin; constipation; hair thinning; depression; muscle weakness; slowed heart rate
treatments: Lifelong thyroid hormone replacement therapy (levothyroxine), with regular monitoring of thyroid hormone levels to adjust dosage.
risk_factors: Female sex; age over 60; family history of thyroid disease; autoimmune conditions; previous thyroid surgery or radiation treatment
prevention: No specific prevention exists for autoimmune causes; adequate dietary iodine intake helps prevent iodine-deficiency-related hypothyroidism.

title: Who is most commonly affected by Epilepsy?
content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Epilepsy? A: Epilepsy most commonly affects All ages, often diagnosed in childhood or after age 60.
symptoms: Recurrent seizures (varying from brief staring spells to convulsions); temporary confusion; uncontrollable jerking movements; loss of consciousness; psychic symptoms such as fear or anxiety before a seizure
treatments: Antiepileptic drugs (e.g., levetiracetam, valproate, lamotrigine) are the mainstay of treatment, with surgery, dietary therapy (ketogenic diet), or neurostimulation devices considered for drug-resistant cases.
risk_factors: Family history of epilepsy; brain injury or trauma; stroke; brain infections; developmental disorders; prenatal injury
prevention: Preventing head injuries, managing cardiovascular risk factors to reduce stroke risk, and prompt treatment of infections that can affect the brain.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood or after age 60

title: What are the common symptoms of Chickenpox (Varicella)?
content_text: Q: What are the common symptoms of Chickenpox (Varicella)? A: Common symptoms include itchy rash with fluid-filled blisters; fever; tiredness; loss of appetite; headache; the rash typically appears in waves over several days.
symptoms: Itchy rash with fluid-filled blisters; fever; tiredness; loss of appetite; headache; the rash typically appears in waves over several days
treatments: Supportive care including antihistamines for itching, calamine lotion, and antipyretics. Antiviral medication (acyclovir) may be used for high-risk individuals or severe cases.
risk_factors: Lack of vaccination; close contact with an infected person; weakened immune system; age under 12 (unvaccinated)
prevention: Varicella vaccination, typically given in two doses during childhood, is highly effective at preventing infection or reducing severity.

title: Rheumatoid Arthritis - Overview
content_text: A chronic autoimmune disorder primarily affecting the joints, causing inflammation that leads to pain, swelling, stiffness, and progressive joint damage, and which can also affect other body systems.
symptoms: Joint pain and swelling, often symmetrical; morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes; fatigue; low-grade fever; joint deformity in advanced stages
treatments: Disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs) such as methotrexate, biologic agents, NSAIDs for symptom relief, and physical therapy to maintain joint function.
risk_factors: Female sex; family history of autoimmune disease; smoking; obesity; age between 30 and 60
prevention: No definitive prevention exists, but smoking cessation and maintaining healthy weight may reduce risk; early diagnosis and treatment slow disease progression.

Individuals with additional risk factors (Living in or traveling to malaria-endemic regions; lack of access to preventive measures (bed nets, prophylaxis); pregnancy; young age) should consult a healthcare provider for personalized guidance. Use of insecticide-treated bed nets, indoor residual spraying, antimalarial prophylaxis for travelers, and prompt diagnosis and treatment of cases. Individuals with additional risk factors (Living in or traveling to malaria-endemic regions; lack of access to preventive measures (bed nets, prophylaxis); pregnancy; young age) should consult a healthcare provider for personalized guidance.
symptoms: Fever and chills; headache; nausea and vomiting; muscle pain and fatigue; sweating; in severe cases, confusion, seizures, or difficulty breathing
treatments: Artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs) are the standard treatment for uncomplicated malaria. Severe malaria requires hospitalization and intravenous antimalarial drugs.
risk_factors: Living in or traveling to malaria-endemic regions; lack of access to preventive measures (bed nets, prophylaxis); pregnancy; young age
prevention: Use of insecticide-treated bed nets, indoor residual spraying, antimalarial prophylaxis for travelers, and prompt diagnosis and treatment of cases.
common_age_group: Children under 5 in endemic regions; travelers

title: Is Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus a chronic condition? (Archived Copy)
content_text: Q: Is Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus a chronic condition? A: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus is generally classified as a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management.
symptoms: Increased thirst; frequent urination; fatigue; blurred vision; slow-healing sores; unexplained weight loss; numbness or tingling in hands or feet
treatments: First-line management includes lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, weight loss) and metformin. Additional agents such as SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 receptor agonists, sulfonylureas, or insulin are added based on glycemic targets and comorbidities.
risk_factors: Obesity; family history of diabetes; age over 45; physical inactivity; history of gestational diabetes; high blood pressure
prevention: Maintaining healthy body weight, regular physical activity (150 minutes/week), balanced diet low in refined sugars, and routine screening for at-risk individuals.

title: Is Peptic Ulcer Disease a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Peptic Ulcer Disease a chronic condition? A: Peptic Ulcer Disease is generally classified as typically an acute (short-term) condition.
symptoms: Burning stomach pain; bloating; heartburn; nausea; intolerance to fatty foods; in severe cases, dark stools or vomiting blood
treatments: Antibiotic regimens to eradicate H. pylori when present, proton pump inhibitors to reduce stomach acid, and discontinuation of NSAIDs when possible.
risk_factors: H. pylori infection; regular use of NSAIDs; smoking; excessive alcohol consumption; stress (as a contributing factor)
prevention: Limiting NSAID use, avoiding excessive alcohol, not smoking, and prompt treatment of H. pylori infection.

title: What causes or increases the risk of Epilepsy?
content_text: Q: What causes or increases the risk of Epilepsy? A: Key risk factors include family history of epilepsy; brain injury or trauma; stroke; brain infections; developmental disorders; prenatal injury.
symptoms: Recurrent seizures (varying from brief staring spells to convulsions); temporary confusion; uncontrollable jerking movements; loss of consciousness; psychic symptoms such as fear or anxiety before a seizure
treatments: Antiepileptic drugs (e.g., levetiracetam, valproate, lamotrigine) are the mainstay of treatment, with surgery, dietary therapy (ketogenic diet), or neurostimulation devices considered for drug-resistant cases.
risk_factors: Family history of epilepsy; brain injury or trauma; stroke; brain infections; developmental disorders; prenatal injury
prevention: Preventing head injuries, managing cardiovascular risk factors to reduce stroke risk, and prompt treatment of infections that can affect the brain.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood or after age 60

title: How is Tuberculosis treated?
content_text: Q: How is Tuberculosis treated? A: Standard treatment involves a 6-9 month course of multiple antibiotics (isoniazid, rifampin, ethambutol, pyrazinamide), with directly observed therapy recommended to ensure adherence and prevent drug resistance.
symptoms: Persistent cough lasting three weeks or longer, sometimes with blood; chest pain; unintentional weight loss; fatigue; fever; night sweats; loss of appetite
treatments: Standard treatment involves a 6-9 month course of multiple antibiotics (isoniazid, rifampin, ethambutol, pyrazinamide), with directly observed therapy recommended to ensure adherence and prevent drug resistance.
risk_factors: Close contact with an infected person; weakened immune system (e.g., HIV); living in or traveling to areas with high TB rates; healthcare work; substance use
prevention: BCG vaccination in countries with high TB prevalence, prompt identification and treatment of active cases, and infection control measures in healthcare settings.
common_age_group: All ages, higher burden in low-resource settings

title: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus - Treatment Guideline
content_text: Clinical management of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus typically follows a stepwise approach. First-line management includes lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, weight loss) and metformin. Additional agents such as SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 receptor agonists, sulfonylureas, or insulin are added based on glycemic targets and comorbidities. Treatment plans should be individualized based on patient comorbidities, preferences, and response to therapy.
symptoms: Increased thirst; frequent urination; fatigue; blurred vision; slow-healing sores; unexplained weight loss; numbness or tingling in hands or feet
treatments: First-line management includes lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, weight loss) and metformin. Additional agents such as SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 receptor agonists, sulfonylureas, or insulin are added based on glycemic targets and comorbidities.
risk_factors: Obesity; family history of diabetes; age over 45; physical inactivity; history of gestational diabetes; high blood pressure

title: Who is most commonly affected by Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease?
content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease? A: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease most commonly affects Adults 40+.
symptoms: Chronic cough with mucus production; shortness of breath, especially during physical activity; wheezing; chest tightness; frequent respiratory infections
treatments: Bronchodilators (short and long-acting), inhaled corticosteroids for frequent exacerbations, pulmonary rehabilitation, oxygen therapy in advanced stages, and smoking cessation as the most critical intervention.
risk_factors: Smoking (primary cause); long-term exposure to air pollutants or occupational dust/chemicals; genetic factors (alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency); history of childhood respiratory infections
prevention: Avoiding smoking and secondhand smoke, reducing occupational and environmental exposure to lung irritants, and vaccination against respiratory infections.

title: Generalized Anxiety Disorder - Prevention Guide
content_text: Prevention strategies for Generalized Anxiety Disorder focus on modifiable risk factors. symptoms: Excessive worry; restlessness; fatigue; difficulty concentrating; irritability; muscle tension; sleep disturbances
treatments: Cognitive behavioral therapy is highly effective; medications such as SSRIs, SNRIs, or buspirone may be used. Relaxation techniques and lifestyle changes support management.
risk_factors: Family history of anxiety disorders; history of trauma; chronic health conditions; personality traits; substance use
prevention: Stress management techniques, regular physical activity, limiting caffeine and alcohol, and adequate sleep hygiene.

title: How is Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease treated?
content_text: Q: How is Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease treated? A: Lifestyle modifications (weight loss, smaller meals, avoiding trigger foods) combined with proton pump inhibitors or H2 receptor blockers. Severe or refractory cases may require surgical intervention.
symptoms: Heartburn; regurgitation of food or sour liquid; chest pain; difficulty swallowing; chronic cough; sensation of a lump in the throat
treatments: Lifestyle modifications (weight loss, smaller meals, avoiding trigger foods) combined with proton pump inhibitors or H2 receptor blockers. Severe or refractory cases may require surgical intervention.
risk_factors: Obesity; hiatal hernia; pregnancy; smoking; certain medications; large meals or eating late at night
prevention: Avoiding large or fatty meals, not lying down immediately after eating, weight management, and limiting alcohol and caffeine intake.

risk_factors: Smoking (primary cause); long-term exposure to air pollutants or occupational dust/chemicals; genetic factors (alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency); history of childhood respiratory infections

title: What is Chronic Kidney Disease?
content_text: Q: What is Chronic Kidney Disease? A: Chronic Kidney Disease is a progressive condition involving gradual loss of kidney function over time, often resulting from diabetes and hypertension, ultimately leading to kidney failure if not managed appropriately.
symptoms: Fatigue; swelling in legs, ankles, or feet; persistent itching; nausea; loss of appetite; muscle cramps; changes in urination frequency
treatments: Management focuses on slowing progression through blood pressure control (ACE inhibitors/ARBs), blood sugar management, dietary modifications (sodium and protein restriction), and treating underlying causes. Advanced stages may require dialysis or transplantation.
risk_factors: Diabetes; high blood pressure; family history of kidney disease; age over 60; obesity; smoking; cardiovascular disease
prevention: Controlling blood pressure and blood sugar, avoiding NSAIDs when possible, maintaining healthy weight, and regular kidney function monitoring for at-risk individuals.

title: How is Migraine treated?
content_text: Q: How is Migraine treated? A: Acute treatment includes NSAIDs or triptans for moderate to severe attacks. Preventive therapy (beta-blockers, anticonvulsants, CGRP inhibitors) is considered for frequent migraines.
symptoms: Throbbing or pulsing headache, often one-sided; nausea and vomiting; sensitivity to light and sound; visual disturbances (aura); dizziness
treatments: Acute treatment includes NSAIDs or triptans for moderate to severe attacks. Preventive therapy (beta-blockers, anticonvulsants, CGRP inhibitors) is considered for frequent migraines.
risk_factors: Family history of migraine; hormonal changes (especially in women); stress; certain foods or food additives; changes in sleep patterns; sensory stimuli
prevention: Identifying and avoiding personal triggers, maintaining regular sleep and meal schedules, stress reduction techniques, and preventive medication when prescribed.

title: Is Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident) a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident) a chronic condition? A: Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident) is generally classified as typically an acute (short-term) condition.
symptoms: Sudden numbness or weakness of face, arm, or leg, especially on one side; sudden confusion or trouble speaking; sudden vision problems; sudden trouble walking or loss of balance; sudden severe headache
treatments: Acute ischemic stroke may be treated with thrombolytic therapy (tPA) within a narrow time window or mechanical thrombectomy. Long-term management includes antiplatelet/anticoagulant therapy, blood pressure control, and rehabilitation.
risk_factors: High blood pressure; smoking; diabetes; high cholesterol; atrial fibrillation; obesity; family history of stroke; age over 55
prevention: Controlling blood pressure, cholesterol, and blood sugar, smoking cessation, regular exercise, and managing atrial fibrillation when present.

title: Is Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus a chronic condition? A: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus is generally classified as a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management.
symptoms: Increased thirst; frequent urination; fatigue; blurred vision; slow-healing sores; unexplained weight loss; numbness or tingling in hands or feet
treatments: First-line management includes lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, weight loss) and metformin. Additional agents such as SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 receptor agonists, sulfonylureas, or insulin are added based on glycemic targets and comorbidities.
risk_factors: Obesity; family history of diabetes; age over 45; physical inactivity; history of gestational diabetes; high blood pressure
prevention: Maintaining healthy body weight, regular physical activity (150 minutes/week), balanced diet low in refined sugars, and routine screening for at-risk individuals.

title: Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis) - Overview
content_text: A chronic inflammatory skin condition characterized by dry, itchy, and inflamed skin, commonly appearing in childhood and often associated with a personal or family history of allergies or asthma.
symptoms: Dry, scaly skin; intense itching; red to brownish-gray patches; small raised bumps that may leak fluid when scratched; thickened, cracked skin
treatments: Regular moisturizing, topical corticosteroids for flare-ups, antihistamines for itching, and identification/avoidance of triggers. Severe cases may require systemic immunomodulators.
risk_factors: Family history of eczema, asthma, or hay fever; dry skin; environmental irritants; stress; certain climates
prevention: Regular moisturizing, avoiding harsh soaps and known irritants, using lukewarm water for bathing, and wearing soft, breathable fabrics.
common_age_group: Children, with many cases persisting into adulthood

title: Who is most commonly affected by Allergic Rhinitis?
content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Allergic Rhinitis? A: Allergic Rhinitis most commonly affects All ages, often begins in childhood or adolescence.
symptoms: Sneezing; runny or stuffy nose; itchy or watery eyes; itchy throat; postnasal drip; fatigue
treatments: Antihistamines, intranasal corticosteroids, decongestants for short-term relief, and allergen immunotherapy for long-term management of severe cases.
risk_factors: Family history of allergies; other allergic conditions (asthma, eczema); exposure to environmental allergens; early childhood exposure to tobacco smoke
prevention: Avoiding known allergens, using air purifiers, keeping windows closed during high pollen seasons, and regular cleaning to reduce dust mites.
common_age_group: All ages, often begins in childhood or adolescence

title: How is Hyperlipidemia treated?
content_text: Q: How is Hyperlipidemia treated? A: Lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, weight loss) as first-line therapy, with statins as the primary pharmacologic treatment; additional agents (ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors) for high-risk patients.

prevention: Heart-healthy diet low in saturated and trans fats, regular physical activity, maintaining healthy weight, and routine lipid screening.
