title: Obesity - Treatment Guideline
content_text: Clinical management of Obesity typically follows a stepwise approach. Comprehensive lifestyle intervention including dietary changes, increased physical activity, and behavioral therapy. Pharmacotherapy (e.g., GLP-1 agonists) or bariatric surgery may be considered for severe cases. Treatment plans should be individualized based on patient comorbidities, preferences, and response to therapy.
symptoms: Excess body fat; breathlessness on exertion; increased sweating; difficulty with physical activity; joint and back pain; low self-esteem
treatments: Comprehensive lifestyle intervention including dietary changes, increased physical activity, and behavioral therapy. Pharmacotherapy (e.g., GLP-1 agonists) or bariatric surgery may be considered for severe cases.
risk_factors: Poor diet; physical inactivity; genetics; certain medications; insufficient sleep; high stress levels; socioeconomic factors
prevention: Balanced nutrition, regular physical activity, adequate sleep, and community/environmental support for healthy choices.

title: Chickenpox (Varicella) - Overview
content_text: A highly contagious viral infection caused by the varicella-zoster virus, characterized by an itchy, blister-like rash, most common in children but preventable through vaccination.
symptoms: Itchy rash with fluid-filled blisters; fever; tiredness; loss of appetite; headache; the rash typically appears in waves over several days
treatments: Supportive care including antihistamines for itching, calamine lotion, and antipyretics. Antiviral medication (acyclovir) may be used for high-risk individuals or severe cases.
risk_factors: Lack of vaccination; close contact with an infected person; weakened immune system; age under 12 (unvaccinated)
prevention: Varicella vaccination, typically given in two doses during childhood, is highly effective at preventing infection or reducing severity.

title: What are the common symptoms of Rheumatoid Arthritis?
content_text: Q: What are the common symptoms of Rheumatoid Arthritis? A: Common symptoms include joint pain and swelling, often symmetrical; morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes; fatigue; low-grade fever; joint deformity in advanced stages.
symptoms: Joint pain and swelling, often symmetrical; morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes; fatigue; low-grade fever; joint deformity in advanced stages
treatments: Disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs) such as methotrexate, biologic agents, NSAIDs for symptom relief, and physical therapy to maintain joint function.
risk_factors: Female sex; family history of autoimmune disease; smoking; obesity; age between 30 and 60
prevention: No definitive prevention exists, but smoking cessation and maintaining healthy weight may reduce risk; early diagnosis and treatment slow disease progression.

title: Is Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye) a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye) a chronic condition? A: Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye) is generally classified as typically an acute (short-term) condition.
symptoms: Redness in the white of the eye; increased tear production; itchy or burning eyes; discharge (watery, mucous, or pus-like); blurred vision; crusting of eyelids
treatments: Viral conjunctivitis is managed with supportive care (cool compresses, artificial tears). Bacterial conjunctivitis may require antibiotic eye drops. Allergic conjunctivitis is treated with antihistamine eye drops.
risk_factors: Close contact with infected individuals; allergies; contact lens use; exposure to irritants; recent upper respiratory infection
prevention: Frequent handwashing, avoiding touching or rubbing the eyes, not sharing personal items such as towels, and proper contact lens hygiene.

title: Is Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease contagious?
content_text: Q: Is Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease contagious? A: Based on current medical guidance, Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease is not contagious and does not spread between people.
symptoms: Heartburn; regurgitation of food or sour liquid; chest pain; difficulty swallowing; chronic cough; sensation of a lump in the throat
treatments: Lifestyle modifications (weight loss, smaller meals, avoiding trigger foods) combined with proton pump inhibitors or H2 receptor blockers. Severe or refractory cases may require surgical intervention.
risk_factors: Obesity; hiatal hernia; pregnancy; smoking; certain medications; large meals or eating late at night
prevention: Avoiding large or fatty meals, not lying down immediately after eating, weight management, and limiting alcohol and caffeine intake.

treatments: Lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, weight loss) as first-line therapy, with statins as the primary pharmacologic treatment; additional agents (ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors) for high-risk patients.
risk_factors: Diet high in saturated fats; obesity; physical inactivity; smoking; diabetes; family history of high cholesterol; age
prevention: Heart-healthy diet low in saturated and trans fats, regular physical activity, maintaining healthy weight, and routine lipid screening.

title: Who is most commonly affected by Influenza?
content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Influenza? A: Influenza most commonly affects All ages.
symptoms: Fever or feeling feverish; chills; cough; sore throat; runny or stuffy nose; muscle or body aches; headaches; fatigue
treatments: Supportive care (rest, fluids, antipyretics) for most cases. Antiviral medications such as oseltamivir may be prescribed within 48 hours of symptom onset for high-risk patients.
risk_factors: Age under 5 or over 65; pregnancy; chronic medical conditions (asthma, diabetes, heart disease); weakened immune system; close contact with infected individuals
prevention: Annual influenza vaccination, frequent handwashing, avoiding close contact with sick individuals, and covering coughs and sneezes.

title: How is Allergic Rhinitis treated?
content_text: Q: How is Allergic Rhinitis treated? A: Antihistamines, intranasal corticosteroids, decongestants for short-term relief, and allergen immunotherapy for long-term management of severe cases.
symptoms: Sneezing; runny or stuffy nose; itchy or watery eyes; itchy throat; postnasal drip; fatigue
treatments: Antihistamines, intranasal corticosteroids, decongestants for short-term relief, and allergen immunotherapy for long-term management of severe cases.
risk_factors: Family history of allergies; other allergic conditions (asthma, eczema); exposure to environmental allergens; early childhood exposure to tobacco smoke
prevention: Avoiding known allergens, using air purifiers, keeping windows closed during high pollen seasons, and regular cleaning to reduce dust mites.
common_age_group: All ages, often begins in childhood or adolescence

title: What causes or increases the risk of Iron Deficiency Anemia?
content_text: Q: What causes or increases the risk of Iron Deficiency Anemia? A: Key risk factors include poor dietary iron intake; heavy menstrual periods; pregnancy; gastrointestinal bleeding; chronic conditions affecting absorption (e.g., celiac disease).
symptoms: Fatigue; pale skin; shortness of breath; dizziness; brittle nails; headaches; cold hands and feet; unusual cravings for non-nutritive substances (pica)
treatments: Oral or intravenous iron supplementation, dietary modification to increase iron-rich foods, and treatment of underlying causes such as bleeding sources.
risk_factors: Poor dietary iron intake; heavy menstrual periods; pregnancy; gastrointestinal bleeding; chronic conditions affecting absorption (e.g., celiac disease)
prevention: Eating iron-rich foods (lean meat, beans, fortified cereals), pairing with vitamin C for absorption, and screening in high-risk groups such as pregnant women.
common_age_group: Women of reproductive age; young children

title: Coronary Artery Disease - Symptom Guide
content_text: Patients with Coronary Artery Disease commonly present with the following symptoms: Chest pain or discomfort (angina); shortness of breath; fatigue; pain in arms or shoulders; cold sweats; nausea. Symptom severity and combination vary by individual and disease stage.
symptoms: Chest pain or discomfort (angina); shortness of breath; fatigue; pain in arms or shoulders; cold sweats; nausea
treatments: Lifestyle changes, statins for cholesterol management, antiplatelet agents (aspirin), beta-blockers, and in severe cases, procedures such as angioplasty with stenting or coronary artery bypass grafting.
risk_factors: High cholesterol; high blood pressure; smoking; diabetes; obesity; family history of heart disease; physical inactivity; age
prevention: Heart-healthy diet, regular exercise, smoking cessation, cholesterol and blood pressure management, and routine cardiovascular screening.

title: Essential Hypertension - Overview
content_text: A common chronic condition in which blood pressure in the arteries is persistently elevated without an identifiable secondary cause, increasing the risk of heart disease, stroke, and kidney damage over time.
symptoms: Often asymptomatic; occasional headaches; shortness of breath; nosebleeds; dizziness in severe cases
treatments: Lifestyle changes (DASH diet, sodium reduction, exercise, weight management) combined with antihypertensive medications such as ACE inhibitors, ARBs, calcium channel blockers, or thiazide diuretics depending on patient profile.
risk_factors: High sodium intake; obesity; sedentary lifestyle; excessive alcohol consumption; chronic stress; family history; aging
prevention: Reducing dietary sodium, maintaining healthy weight, limiting alcohol, regular aerobic exercise, and managing stress.
source_name: American Heart Association - High Blood Pressure

title: Is Pneumonia contagious?
content_text: Q: Is Pneumonia contagious? A: Based on current medical guidance, Pneumonia is contagious and can spread between people.
symptoms: Cough with phlegm; fever; chills; shortness of breath; chest pain when breathing or coughing; fatigue; confusion (especially in older adults)
treatments: Bacterial pneumonia is treated with antibiotics; viral pneumonia is managed with supportive care and antivirals when appropriate. Hospitalization may be required for severe cases.
risk_factors: Age under 2 or over 65; chronic diseases (asthma, COPD, heart disease); weakened immune system; smoking; recent respiratory infection; hospitalization
prevention: Vaccination (pneumococcal, influenza), good hygiene, avoiding smoking, and managing underlying chronic conditions.

title: Is Psoriasis a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Psoriasis a chronic condition? A: Psoriasis is generally classified as a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management.
symptoms: Red patches of skin covered with thick, silvery scales; dry, cracked skin that may bleed; itching, burning, or soreness; thickened or ridged nails; stiff and swollen joints (in psoriatic arthritis)
treatments: Topical treatments (corticosteroids, vitamin D analogs) for mild cases, phototherapy for moderate cases, and systemic agents or biologics for moderate-to-severe disease.
risk_factors: Family history of psoriasis; smoking; obesity; stress; certain infections; certain medications (e.g., lithium, beta-blockers)
prevention: No definitive prevention exists; managing triggers such as stress, smoking, and certain medications may reduce flare frequency.

title: What is Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis)?
content_text: Q: What is Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis)? A: Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis) is a chronic inflammatory skin condition characterized by dry, itchy, and inflamed skin, commonly appearing in childhood and often associated with a personal or family history of allergies or asthma.
symptoms: Dry, scaly skin; intense itching; red to brownish-gray patches; small raised bumps that may leak fluid when scratched; thickened, cracked skin
treatments: Regular moisturizing, topical corticosteroids for flare-ups, antihistamines for itching, and identification/avoidance of triggers. Severe cases may require systemic immunomodulators.
risk_factors: Family history of eczema, asthma, or hay fever; dry skin; environmental irritants; stress; certain climates
prevention: Regular moisturizing, avoiding harsh soaps and known irritants, using lukewarm water for bathing, and wearing soft, breathable fabrics.
common_age_group: Children, with many cases persisting into adulthood

title: Iron Deficiency Anemia - Symptom Guide
content_text: Patients with Iron Deficiency Anemia commonly present with the following symptoms: Fatigue; pale skin; shortness of breath; dizziness; brittle nails; headaches; cold hands and feet; unusual cravings for non-nutritive substances (pica). Symptom severity and combination vary by individual and disease stage.
symptoms: Fatigue; pale skin; shortness of breath; dizziness; brittle nails; headaches; cold hands and feet; unusual cravings for non-nutritive substances (pica)
treatments: Oral or intravenous iron supplementation, dietary modification to increase iron-rich foods, and treatment of underlying causes such as bleeding sources.
risk_factors: Poor dietary iron intake; heavy menstrual periods; pregnancy; gastrointestinal bleeding; chronic conditions affecting absorption (e.g., celiac disease)
prevention: Eating iron-rich foods (lean meat, beans, fortified cereals), pairing with vitamin C for absorption, and screening in high-risk groups such as pregnant women.
common_age_group: Women of reproductive age; young children

title: Urinary Tract Infection - Prevention Guide
content_text: Prevention strategies for Urinary Tract Infection focus on modifiable risk factors. Adequate hydration, urinating after intercourse, proper hygiene practices, and avoiding irritating feminine products. Individuals with additional risk factors (Female anatomy; sexual activity; certain types of birth control; menopause; urinary tract abnormalities; catheter use; weakened immune system) should consult a healthcare provider for personalized guidance.
symptoms: Strong, persistent urge to urinate; burning sensation during urination; cloudy or strong-smelling urine; pelvic pain in women; frequent, small amounts of urine
treatments: Short course of antibiotics (e.g., nitrofurantoin, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) tailored to local resistance patterns. Increased fluid intake supports recovery.
risk_factors: Female anatomy; sexual activity; certain types of birth control; menopause; urinary tract abnormalities; catheter use; weakened immune system
prevention: Adequate hydration, urinating after intercourse, proper hygiene practices, and avoiding irritating feminine products.
common_age_group: Women of all ages, older adults

title: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus - Symptom Guide
content_text: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus commonly present with the following symptoms: Increased thirst; frequent urination; fatigue; blurred vision; slow-healing sores; unexplained weight loss; numbness or tingling in hands or feet. Symptom severity and combination vary by individual and disease stage.
symptoms: Increased thirst; frequent urination; fatigue; blurred vision; slow-healing sores; unexplained weight loss; numbness or tingling in hands or feet
treatments: First-line management includes lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, weight loss) and metformin. Additional agents such as SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 receptor agonists, sulfonylureas, or insulin are added based on glycemic targets and comorbidities.
risk_factors: Obesity; family history of diabetes; age over 45; physical inactivity; history of gestational diabetes; high blood pressure
prevention: Maintaining healthy body weight, regular physical activity (150 minutes/week), balanced diet low in refined sugars, and routine screening for at-risk individuals.

title: Pneumonia - Symptom Guide (Archived Copy)
content_text: Patients with Pneumonia commonly present with the following symptoms: Cough with phlegm; fever; chills; shortness of breath; chest pain when breathing or coughing; fatigue; confusion (especially in older adults). Symptom severity and combination vary by individual and disease stage.
symptoms: Cough with phlegm; fever; chills; shortness of breath; chest pain when breathing or coughing; fatigue; confusion (especially in older adults)
treatments: Bacterial pneumonia is treated with antibiotics; viral pneumonia is managed with supportive care and antivirals when appropriate. Hospitalization may be required for severe cases.
risk_factors: Age under 2 or over 65; chronic diseases (asthma, COPD, heart disease); weakened immune system; smoking; recent respiratory infection; hospitalization
prevention: Vaccination (pneumococcal, influenza), good hygiene, avoiding smoking, and managing underlying chronic conditions.

title: Influenza - Prevention Guide
content_text: Prevention strategies for Influenza focus on modifiable risk factors. Annual influenza vaccination, frequent handwashing, avoiding close contact with sick individuals, and covering coughs and sneezes. Individuals with additional risk factors (Age under 5 or over 65; pregnancy; chronic medical conditions (asthma, diabetes, heart disease); weakened immune system; close contact with infected individuals) should consult a healthcare provider for personalized guidance.
symptoms: Fever or feeling feverish; chills; cough; sore throat; runny or stuffy nose; muscle or body aches; headaches; fatigue
treatments: Supportive care (rest, fluids, antipyretics) for most cases. Antiviral medications such as oseltamivir may be prescribed within 48 hours of symptom onset for high-risk patients.
risk_factors: Age under 5 or over 65; pregnancy; chronic medical conditions (asthma, diabetes, heart disease); weakened immune system; close contact with infected individuals
prevention: Annual influenza vaccination, frequent handwashing, avoiding close contact with sick individuals, and covering coughs and sneezes.

title: What is Sleep Apnea?
content_text: Q: What is Sleep Apnea? A: Sleep Apnea is a sleep disorder characterized by repeated interruptions in breathing during sleep, most commonly due to relaxation of throat muscles causing airway obstruction (obstructive sleep apnea).
symptoms: Loud snoring; episodes of stopped breathing observed by others; gasping for air during sleep; excessive daytime sleepiness; morning headaches; difficulty concentrating; irritability
treatments: Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) therapy is the standard treatment. Weight loss, positional therapy, oral appliances, and in select cases, surgery, may also be used.
risk_factors: Obesity; older age; male sex; large neck circumference; family history; smoking; nasal congestion; use of alcohol or sedatives
prevention: Maintaining healthy weight, avoiding alcohol and sedatives before sleep, sleeping on one's side, and treating nasal congestion.

title: Sleep Apnea - Treatment Guideline
content_text: Clinical management of Sleep Apnea typically follows a stepwise approach. Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) therapy is the standard treatment. Weight loss, positional therapy, oral appliances, and in select cases, surgery, may also be used. Treatment plans should be individualized based on patient comorbidities, preferences, and response to therapy.
symptoms: Loud snoring; episodes of stopped breathing observed by others; gasping for air during sleep; excessive daytime sleepiness; morning headaches; difficulty concentrating; irritability
treatments: Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) therapy is the standard treatment. Weight loss, positional therapy, oral appliances, and in select cases, surgery, may also be used.
risk_factors: Obesity; older age; male sex; large neck circumference; family history; smoking; nasal congestion; use of alcohol or sedatives
prevention: Maintaining healthy weight, avoiding alcohol and sedatives before sleep, sleeping on one's side, and treating nasal congestion.

title: How is Urinary Tract Infection treated?
content_text: Q: How is Urinary Tract Infection treated? A: Short course of antibiotics (e.g., nitrofurantoin, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) tailored to local resistance patterns. Increased fluid intake supports recovery.
symptoms: Strong, persistent urge to urinate; burning sensation during urination; cloudy or strong-smelling urine; pelvic pain in women; frequent, small amounts of urine
treatments: Short course of antibiotics (e.g., nitrofurantoin, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) tailored to local resistance patterns. Increased fluid intake supports recovery.
risk_factors: Female anatomy; sexual activity; certain types of birth control; menopause; urinary tract abnormalities; catheter use; weakened immune system
prevention: Adequate hydration, urinating after intercourse, proper hygiene practices, and avoiding irritating feminine products.
common_age_group: Women of all ages, older adults

title: What causes or increases the risk of Chronic Kidney Disease?
content_text: Q: What causes or increases the risk of Chronic Kidney Disease? A: Key risk factors include diabetes; high blood pressure; family history of kidney disease; age over 60; obesity; smoking; cardiovascular disease.
symptoms: Fatigue; swelling in legs, ankles, or feet; persistent itching; nausea; loss of appetite; muscle cramps; changes in urination frequency
treatments: Management focuses on slowing progression through blood pressure control (ACE inhibitors/ARBs), blood sugar management, dietary modifications (sodium and protein restriction), and treating underlying causes. Advanced stages may require dialysis or transplantation.
risk_factors: Diabetes; high blood pressure; family history of kidney disease; age over 60; obesity; smoking; cardiovascular disease
prevention: Controlling blood pressure and blood sugar, avoiding NSAIDs when possible, maintaining healthy weight, and regular kidney function monitoring for at-risk individuals.

title: Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident) - Overview
content_text: A medical emergency that occurs when blood supply to part of the brain is interrupted or reduced, depriving brain tissue of oxygen and nutrients, leading to brain cell death within minutes if not treated promptly.
symptoms: Sudden numbness or weakness of face, arm, or leg, especially on one side; sudden confusion or trouble speaking; sudden vision problems; sudden trouble walking or loss of balance; sudden severe headache
treatments: Acute ischemic stroke may be treated with thrombolytic therapy (tPA) within a narrow time window or mechanical thrombectomy. Long-term management includes antiplatelet/anticoagulant therapy, blood pressure control, and rehabilitation.
risk_factors: High blood pressure; smoking; diabetes; high cholesterol; atrial fibrillation; obesity; family history of stroke; age over 55
prevention: Controlling blood pressure, cholesterol, and blood sugar, smoking cessation, regular exercise, and managing atrial fibrillation when present.

title: Is Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye) contagious?
content_text: Q: Is Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye) contagious? A: Based on current medical guidance, Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye) is contagious and can spread between people.
symptoms: Redness in the white of the eye; increased tear production; itchy or burning eyes; discharge (watery, mucous, or pus-like); blurred vision; crusting of eyelids
treatments: Viral conjunctivitis is managed with supportive care (cool compresses, artificial tears). Bacterial conjunctivitis may require antibiotic eye drops. Allergic conjunctivitis is treated with antihistamine eye drops.
risk_factors: Close contact with infected individuals; allergies; contact lens use; exposure to irritants; recent upper respiratory infection
prevention: Frequent handwashing, avoiding touching or rubbing the eyes, not sharing personal items such as towels, and proper contact lens hygiene.

title: What causes or increases the risk of Celiac Disease?
content_text: Q: What causes or increases the risk of Celiac Disease? A: Key risk factors include family history of celiac disease or autoimmune disorders; type 1 diabetes; Down syndrome; autoimmune thyroid disease.
symptoms: Diarrhea or constipation; bloating and gas; abdominal pain; fatigue; weight loss; anemia; skin rash (dermatitis herpetiformis); in children, failure to thrive
treatments: The only effective treatment is a strict, lifelong gluten-free diet. Nutritional supplementation may be needed to correct deficiencies (iron, vitamin D, B12, folate).
risk_factors: Family history of celiac disease or autoimmune disorders; type 1 diabetes; Down syndrome; autoimmune thyroid disease
prevention: No known prevention; early diagnosis and dietary management prevent complications such as malnutrition and osteoporosis.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood or 20s-30s

title: How is Malaria treated?
content_text: Q: How is Malaria treated? A: Artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs) are the standard treatment for uncomplicated malaria. Severe malaria requires hospitalization and intravenous antimalarial drugs.
symptoms: Fever and chills; headache; nausea and vomiting; muscle pain and fatigue; sweating; in severe cases, confusion, seizures, or difficulty breathing
treatments: Artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs) are the standard treatment for uncomplicated malaria. Severe malaria requires hospitalization and intravenous antimalarial drugs.
risk_factors: Living in or traveling to malaria-endemic regions; lack of access to preventive measures (bed nets, prophylaxis); pregnancy; young age
prevention: Use of insecticide-treated bed nets, indoor residual spraying, antimalarial prophylaxis for travelers, and prompt diagnosis and treatment of cases.
common_age_group: Children under 5 in endemic regions; travelers

title: Who is most commonly affected by Chickenpox (Varicella)?
content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Chickenpox (Varicella)? A: Chickenpox (Varicella) most commonly affects Children under 10 (unvaccinated populations).
symptoms: Itchy rash with fluid-filled blisters; fever; tiredness; loss of appetite; headache; the rash typically appears in waves over several days
treatments: Supportive care including antihistamines for itching, calamine lotion, and antipyretics. Antiviral medication (acyclovir) may be used for high-risk individuals or severe cases.
risk_factors: Lack of vaccination; close contact with an infected person; weakened immune system; age under 12 (unvaccinated)
prevention: Varicella vaccination, typically given in two doses during childhood, is highly effective at preventing infection or reducing severity.

title: Tuberculosis - Prevention Guide
content_text: Prevention strategies for Tuberculosis focus on modifiable risk factors. BCG vaccination in countries with high TB prevalence, prompt identification and treatment of active cases, and infection control measures in healthcare settings. Individuals with additional risk factors (Close contact with an infected person; weakened immune system (e.g., HIV); living in or traveling to areas with high TB rates; healthcare work; substance use) should consult a healthcare provider for personalized guidance.
symptoms: Persistent cough lasting three weeks or longer, sometimes with blood; chest pain; unintentional weight loss; fatigue; fever; night sweats; loss of appetite
treatments: Standard treatment involves a 6-9 month course of multiple antibiotics (isoniazid, rifampin, ethambutol, pyrazinamide), with directly observed therapy recommended to ensure adherence and prevent drug resistance.
risk_factors: Close contact with an infected person; weakened immune system (e.g., HIV); living in or traveling to areas with high TB rates; healthcare work; substance use
prevention: BCG vaccination in countries with high TB prevalence, prompt identification and treatment of active cases, and infection control measures in healthcare settings.
common_age_group: All ages, higher burden in low-resource settings

title: What is Peptic Ulcer Disease?
content_text: Q: What is Peptic Ulcer Disease? A: Peptic Ulcer Disease is a condition involving sores that develop on the lining of the stomach, lower esophagus, or small intestine, most commonly caused by Helicobacter pylori infection or long-term use of NSAIDs.
symptoms: Burning stomach pain; bloating; heartburn; nausea; intolerance to fatty foods; in severe cases, dark stools or vomiting blood
treatments: Antibiotic regimens to eradicate H. pylori when present, proton pump inhibitors to reduce stomach acid, and discontinuation of NSAIDs when possible.
risk_factors: H. pylori infection; regular use of NSAIDs; smoking; excessive alcohol consumption; stress (as a contributing factor)
prevention: Limiting NSAID use, avoiding excessive alcohol, not smoking, and prompt treatment of H. pylori infection.

title: Osteoarthritis - Prevention Guide
content_text: Prevention strategies for Osteoarthritis focus on modifiable risk factors. Maintaining healthy weight, regular low-impact exercise, avoiding repetitive joint stress, and proper injury management. Individuals with additional risk factors (Older age; obesity; joint injury; repetitive stress on joints; genetics; female sex) should consult a healthcare provider for personalized guidance.
symptoms: Joint pain that worsens with activity; stiffness, especially after rest; loss of flexibility; grating sensation; bone spurs; swelling around the joint
treatments: Weight management, physical therapy, pain relievers (acetaminophen, NSAIDs), and in advanced cases, joint replacement surgery.
risk_factors: Older age; obesity; joint injury; repetitive stress on joints; genetics; female sex
prevention: Maintaining healthy weight, regular low-impact exercise, avoiding repetitive joint stress, and proper injury management.

title: What are the common symptoms of Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye)?
content_text: Q: What are the common symptoms of Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye)? A: Common symptoms include redness in the white of the eye; increased tear production; itchy or burning eyes; discharge (watery, mucous, or pus-like); blurred vision; crusting of eyelids.

risk_factors: Close contact with infected individuals; allergies; contact lens use; exposure to irritants; recent upper respiratory infection
prevention: Frequent handwashing, avoiding touching or rubbing the eyes, not sharing personal items such as towels, and proper contact lens hygiene.

title: What are the common symptoms of Essential Hypertension?
content_text: Q: What are the common symptoms of Essential Hypertension? A: Common symptoms include often asymptomatic; occasional headaches; shortness of breath; nosebleeds; dizziness in severe cases.
symptoms: Often asymptomatic; occasional headaches; shortness of breath; nosebleeds; dizziness in severe cases
treatments: Lifestyle changes (DASH diet, sodium reduction, exercise, weight management) combined with antihypertensive medications such as ACE inhibitors, ARBs, calcium channel blockers, or thiazide diuretics depending on patient profile.
risk_factors: High sodium intake; obesity; sedentary lifestyle; excessive alcohol consumption; chronic stress; family history; aging
prevention: Reducing dietary sodium, maintaining healthy weight, limiting alcohol, regular aerobic exercise, and managing stress.
source_name: American Heart Association - High Blood Pressure

title: How is Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease treated?
content_text: Q: How is Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease treated? A: Bronchodilators (short and long-acting), inhaled corticosteroids for frequent exacerbations, pulmonary rehabilitation, oxygen therapy in advanced stages, and smoking cessation as the most critical intervention.
symptoms: Chronic cough with mucus production; shortness of breath, especially during physical activity; wheezing; chest tightness; frequent respiratory infections
treatments: Bronchodilators (short and long-acting), inhaled corticosteroids for frequent exacerbations, pulmonary rehabilitation, oxygen therapy in advanced stages, and smoking cessation as the most critical intervention.
risk_factors: Smoking (primary cause); long-term exposure to air pollutants or occupational dust/chemicals; genetic factors (alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency); history of childhood respiratory infections
prevention: Avoiding smoking and secondhand smoke, reducing occupational and environmental exposure to lung irritants, and vaccination against respiratory infections.

title: Celiac Disease - Prevention Guide
content_text: Prevention strategies for Celiac Disease focus on modifiable risk factors. No known prevention; early diagnosis and dietary management prevent complications such as malnutrition and osteoporosis. Individuals with additional risk factors (Family history of celiac disease or autoimmune disorders; type 1 diabetes; Down syndrome; autoimmune thyroid disease) should consult a healthcare provider for personalized guidance.
symptoms: Diarrhea or constipation; bloating and gas; abdominal pain; fatigue; weight loss; anemia; skin rash (dermatitis herpetiformis); in children, failure to thrive
treatments: The only effective treatment is a strict, lifelong gluten-free diet. Nutritional supplementation may be needed to correct deficiencies (iron, vitamin D, B12, folate).
risk_factors: Family history of celiac disease or autoimmune disorders; type 1 diabetes; Down syndrome; autoimmune thyroid disease
prevention: No known prevention; early diagnosis and dietary management prevent complications such as malnutrition and osteoporosis.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood or 20s-30s

title: Asthma - Overview
content_text: A chronic inflammatory disease of the airways causing recurring episodes of wheezing, breathlessness, chest tightness, and coughing due to airway hyperresponsiveness and reversible airflow obstruction.
symptoms: Wheezing; shortness of breath; chest tightness; coughing, especially at night or early morning; difficulty sleeping due to breathing problems
treatments: Controller medications (inhaled corticosteroids, long-acting beta-agonists) for long-term control, plus short-acting bronchodilators (e.g., albuterol) for acute symptom relief. Avoidance of known triggers is essential.
risk_factors: Family history of asthma or allergies; exposure to tobacco smoke; air pollution; occupational exposure to irritants; respiratory infections in childhood
prevention: Avoiding known allergens and irritants, controlling indoor air quality, getting recommended vaccinations, and adherence to prescribed controller therapy.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood

title: Hypothyroidism - Prevention Guide
content_text: Prevention strategies for Hypothyroidism focus on modifiable risk factors. No specific prevention exists for autoimmune causes; adequate dietary iodine intake helps prevent iodine-deficiency-related hypothyroidism. Individuals with additional risk factors (Female sex; age over 60; family history of thyroid disease; autoimmune conditions; previous thyroid surgery or radiation treatment) should consult a healthcare provider for personalized guidance.
symptoms: Fatigue; weight gain; cold intolerance; dry skin; constipation; hair thinning; depression; muscle weakness; slowed heart rate
treatments: Lifelong thyroid hormone replacement therapy (levothyroxine), with regular monitoring of thyroid hormone levels to adjust dosage.
risk_factors: Female sex; age over 60; family history of thyroid disease; autoimmune conditions; previous thyroid surgery or radiation treatment
prevention: No specific prevention exists for autoimmune causes; adequate dietary iodine intake helps prevent iodine-deficiency-related hypothyroidism.

title: What is Osteoarthritis?
content_text: Q: What is Osteoarthritis? A: Osteoarthritis is the most common form of arthritis, caused by the gradual breakdown of cartilage cushioning the ends of bones, resulting in pain, stiffness, and reduced joint mobility, most often affecting hands, knees, hips, and spine.
symptoms: Joint pain that worsens with activity; stiffness, especially after rest; loss of flexibility; grating sensation; bone spurs; swelling around the joint
treatments: Weight management, physical therapy, pain relievers (acetaminophen, NSAIDs), and in advanced cases, joint replacement surgery.
risk_factors: Older age; obesity; joint injury; repetitive stress on joints; genetics; female sex
prevention: Maintaining healthy weight, regular low-impact exercise, avoiding repetitive joint stress, and proper injury management.

title: Can Allergic Rhinitis be prevented?
content_text: Q: Can Allergic Rhinitis be prevented? A: Avoiding known allergens, using air purifiers, keeping windows closed during high pollen seasons, and regular cleaning to reduce dust mites.
symptoms: Sneezing; runny or stuffy nose; itchy or watery eyes; itchy throat; postnasal drip; fatigue
treatments: Antihistamines, intranasal corticosteroids, decongestants for short-term relief, and allergen immunotherapy for long-term management of severe cases.
risk_factors: Family history of allergies; other allergic conditions (asthma, eczema); exposure to environmental allergens; early childhood exposure to tobacco smoke
prevention: Avoiding known allergens, using air purifiers, keeping windows closed during high pollen seasons, and regular cleaning to reduce dust mites.
common_age_group: All ages, often begins in childhood or adolescence

title: How is Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus treated?
content_text: Q: How is Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus treated? A: First-line management includes lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, weight loss) and metformin. Additional agents such as SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 receptor agonists, sulfonylureas, or insulin are added based on glycemic targets and comorbidities.

title: Can Coronary Artery Disease be prevented?
content_text: Q: Can Coronary Artery Disease be prevented? A: Heart-healthy diet, regular exercise, smoking cessation, cholesterol and blood pressure management, and routine cardiovascular screening.
symptoms: Chest pain or discomfort (angina); shortness of breath; fatigue; pain in arms or shoulders; cold sweats; nausea
treatments: Lifestyle changes, statins for cholesterol management, antiplatelet agents (aspirin), beta-blockers, and in severe cases, procedures such as angioplasty with stenting or coronary artery bypass grafting.
risk_factors: High cholesterol; high blood pressure; smoking; diabetes; obesity; family history of heart disease; physical inactivity; age
prevention: Heart-healthy diet, regular exercise, smoking cessation, cholesterol and blood pressure management, and routine cardiovascular screening.

title: What are the common symptoms of Asthma?
content_text: Q: What are the common symptoms of Asthma? A: Common symptoms include wheezing; shortness of breath; chest tightness; coughing, especially at night or early morning; difficulty sleeping due to breathing problems.
symptoms: Wheezing; shortness of breath; chest tightness; coughing, especially at night or early morning; difficulty sleeping due to breathing problems

title: What is Epilepsy?
content_text: Q: What is Epilepsy? A: Epilepsy is a chronic neurological disorder characterized by recurrent, unprovoked seizures caused by abnormal electrical activity in the brain, varying widely in type and severity among individuals.
symptoms: Recurrent seizures (varying from brief staring spells to convulsions); temporary confusion; uncontrollable jerking movements; loss of consciousness; psychic symptoms such as fear or anxiety before a seizure
treatments: Antiepileptic drugs (e.g., levetiracetam, valproate, lamotrigine) are the mainstay of treatment, with surgery, dietary therapy (ketogenic diet), or neurostimulation devices considered for drug-resistant cases.
risk_factors: Family history of epilepsy; brain injury or trauma; stroke; brain infections; developmental disorders; prenatal injury
prevention: Preventing head injuries, managing cardiovascular risk factors to reduce stroke risk, and prompt treatment of infections that can affect the brain.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood or after age 60

title: Chronic Kidney Disease - Overview
content_text: A progressive condition involving gradual loss of kidney function over time, often resulting from diabetes and hypertension, ultimately leading to kidney failure if not managed appropriately.
symptoms: Fatigue; swelling in legs, ankles, or feet; persistent itching; nausea; loss of appetite; muscle cramps; changes in urination frequency
treatments: Management focuses on slowing progression through blood pressure control (ACE inhibitors/ARBs), blood sugar management, dietary modifications (sodium and protein restriction), and treating underlying causes. Advanced stages may require dialysis or transplantation.
risk_factors: Diabetes; high blood pressure; family history of kidney disease; age over 60; obesity; smoking; cardiovascular disease
prevention: Controlling blood pressure and blood sugar, avoiding NSAIDs when possible, maintaining healthy weight, and regular kidney function monitoring for at-risk individuals.

title: What is Coronary Artery Disease?
content_text: Q: What is Coronary Artery Disease? A: Coronary Artery Disease is a condition in which the coronary arteries become narrowed or blocked due to buildup of cholesterol-containing plaques (atherosclerosis), reducing blood flow to the heart muscle and increasing the risk of heart attack.
symptoms: Chest pain or discomfort (angina); shortness of breath; fatigue; pain in arms or shoulders; cold sweats; nausea
treatments: Lifestyle changes, statins for cholesterol management, antiplatelet agents (aspirin), beta-blockers, and in severe cases, procedures such as angioplasty with stenting or coronary artery bypass grafting.
risk_factors: High cholesterol; high blood pressure; smoking; diabetes; obesity; family history of heart disease; physical inactivity; age
prevention: Heart-healthy diet, regular exercise, smoking cessation, cholesterol and blood pressure management, and routine cardiovascular screening.

title: Pneumonia - Clinical Decision Support Summary
content_text: Clinical decision support summary for Pneumonia (ICD-10: J18). Category: Respiratory. Typical severity: Moderate to Severe. Chronicity: typically an acute (short-term) condition. Transmission risk: considered contagious and can spread between people. Primary risk factors: Age under 2 or over 65; chronic diseases (asthma, COPD, heart disease); weakened immune system; smoking; recent respiratory infection; hospitalization. Recommended initial management: Bacterial pneumonia is treated with antibiotics; viral pneumonia is managed with supportive care and antivirals when appropriate. Hospitalization may be required for severe cases.
symptoms: Cough with phlegm; fever; chills; shortness of breath; chest pain when breathing or coughing; fatigue; confusion (especially in older adults)
treatments: Bacterial pneumonia is treated with antibiotics; viral pneumonia is managed with supportive care and antivirals when appropriate. Hospitalization may be required for severe cases.
risk_factors: Age under 2 or over 65; chronic diseases (asthma, COPD, heart disease); weakened immune system; smoking; recent respiratory infection; hospitalization
prevention: Vaccination (pneumococcal, influenza), good hygiene, avoiding smoking, and managing underlying chronic conditions.

title: What is Major Depressive Disorder?
content_text: Q: What is Major Depressive Disorder? A: Major Depressive Disorder is a common but serious mood disorder characterized by persistent feelings of sadness, hopelessness, and loss of interest or pleasure in activities, significantly affecting daily functioning for at least two weeks.
symptoms: Persistent sad or empty mood; loss of interest in hobbies; fatigue or low energy; difficulty concentrating; changes in appetite or weight; sleep disturbances; thoughts of death or suicide
treatments: Psychotherapy (cognitive behavioral therapy, interpersonal therapy) and/or antidepressant medications (SSRIs, SNRIs). Severe cases may require combination therapy or, rarely, electroconvulsive therapy.
risk_factors: Family history of depression; history of trauma or abuse; chronic illness; substance use; major life changes or stress; certain medications
prevention: Stress management, strong social support networks, regular physical activity, adequate sleep, and early intervention for at-risk individuals.

title: Sleep Apnea - Symptom Guide
content_text: Patients with Sleep Apnea commonly present with the following symptoms: Loud snoring; episodes of stopped breathing observed by others; gasping for air during sleep; excessive daytime sleepiness; morning headaches; difficulty concentrating; irritability. Symptom severity and combination vary by individual and disease stage.
symptoms: Loud snoring; episodes of stopped breathing observed by others; gasping for air during sleep; excessive daytime sleepiness; morning headaches; difficulty concentrating; irritability
treatments: Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) therapy is the standard treatment. Weight loss, positional therapy, oral appliances, and in select cases, surgery, may also be used.
risk_factors: Obesity; older age; male sex; large neck circumference; family history; smoking; nasal congestion; use of alcohol or sedatives
prevention: Maintaining healthy weight, avoiding alcohol and sedatives before sleep, sleeping on one's side, and treating nasal congestion.

title: Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis) - Prevention Guide
content_text: Prevention strategies for Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis) focus on modifiable risk factors. Regular moisturizing, avoiding harsh soaps and known irritants, using lukewarm water for bathing, and wearing soft, breathable fabrics. Individuals with additional risk factors (Family history of eczema, asthma, or hay fever; dry skin; environmental irritants; stress; certain climates) should consult a healthcare provider for personalized guidance.
symptoms: Dry, scaly skin; intense itching; red to brownish-gray patches; small raised bumps that may leak fluid when scratched; thickened, cracked skin
treatments: Regular moisturizing, topical corticosteroids for flare-ups, antihistamines for itching, and identification/avoidance of triggers. Severe cases may require systemic immunomodulators.
risk_factors: Family history of eczema, asthma, or hay fever; dry skin; environmental irritants; stress; certain climates
prevention: Regular moisturizing, avoiding harsh soaps and known irritants, using lukewarm water for bathing, and wearing soft, breathable fabrics.
common_age_group: Children, with many cases persisting into adulthood

title: What is Generalized Anxiety Disorder?
content_text: Q: What is Generalized Anxiety Disorder? A: Generalized Anxiety Disorder is a mental health condition characterized by persistent and excessive worry about various aspects of life that is difficult to control and interferes with daily functioning, lasting at least six months.
symptoms: Excessive worry; restlessness; fatigue; difficulty concentrating; irritability; muscle tension; sleep disturbances
treatments: Cognitive behavioral therapy is highly effective; medications such as SSRIs, SNRIs, or buspirone may be used. Relaxation techniques and lifestyle changes support management.
risk_factors: Family history of anxiety disorders; history of trauma; chronic health conditions; personality traits; substance use
prevention: Stress management techniques, regular physical activity, limiting caffeine and alcohol, and adequate sleep hygiene.

title: Rheumatoid Arthritis - Treatment Guideline (Archived Copy)
content_text: Clinical management of Rheumatoid Arthritis typically follows a stepwise approach. Disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs) such as methotrexate, biologic agents, NSAIDs for symptom relief, and physical therapy to maintain joint function. Treatment plans should be individualized based on patient comorbidities, preferences, and response to therapy.
symptoms: Joint pain and swelling, often symmetrical; morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes; fatigue; low-grade fever; joint deformity in advanced stages
treatments: Disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs) such as methotrexate, biologic agents, NSAIDs for symptom relief, and physical therapy to maintain joint function.
risk_factors: Female sex; family history of autoimmune disease; smoking; obesity; age between 30 and 60
prevention: No definitive prevention exists, but smoking cessation and maintaining healthy weight may reduce risk; early diagnosis and treatment slow disease progression.

title: What causes or increases the risk of Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye)?
content_text: Q: What causes or increases the risk of Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye)? A: Key risk factors include close contact with infected individuals; allergies; contact lens use; exposure to irritants; recent upper respiratory infection.
symptoms: Redness in the white of the eye; increased tear production; itchy or burning eyes; discharge (watery, mucous, or pus-like); blurred vision; crusting of eyelids
treatments: Viral conjunctivitis is managed with supportive care (cool compresses, artificial tears). Bacterial conjunctivitis may require antibiotic eye drops. Allergic conjunctivitis is treated with antihistamine eye drops.
risk_factors: Close contact with infected individuals; allergies; contact lens use; exposure to irritants; recent upper respiratory infection
prevention: Frequent handwashing, avoiding touching or rubbing the eyes, not sharing personal items such as towels, and proper contact lens hygiene.

title: What causes or increases the risk of Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease?
content_text: Q: What causes or increases the risk of Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease? A: Key risk factors include obesity; hiatal hernia; pregnancy; smoking; certain medications; large meals or eating late at night.
symptoms: Heartburn; regurgitation of food or sour liquid; chest pain; difficulty swallowing; chronic cough; sensation of a lump in the throat
treatments: Lifestyle modifications (weight loss, smaller meals, avoiding trigger foods) combined with proton pump inhibitors or H2 receptor blockers. Severe or refractory cases may require surgical intervention.
risk_factors: Obesity; hiatal hernia; pregnancy; smoking; certain medications; large meals or eating late at night
prevention: Avoiding large or fatty meals, not lying down immediately after eating, weight management, and limiting alcohol and caffeine intake.

title: Obesity - Clinical Decision Support Summary
content_text: Clinical decision support summary for Obesity (ICD-10: E66). Category: Metabolic. Typical severity: Mild to Severe. Chronicity: a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management. Transmission risk: not considered contagious and does not spread between people. Primary risk factors: Poor diet; physical inactivity; genetics; certain medications; insufficient sleep; high stress levels; socioeconomic factors. Recommended initial management: Comprehensive lifestyle intervention including dietary changes, increased physical activity, and behavioral therapy. Pharmacotherapy (e.g., GLP-1 agonists) or bariatric surgery may be considered for severe cases.
symptoms: Excess body fat; breathlessness on exertion; increased sweating; difficulty with physical activity; joint and back pain; low self-esteem
treatments: Comprehensive lifestyle intervention including dietary changes, increased physical activity, and behavioral therapy. Pharmacotherapy (e.g., GLP-1 agonists) or bariatric surgery may be considered for severe cases.
risk_factors: Poor diet; physical inactivity; genetics; certain medications; insufficient sleep; high stress levels; socioeconomic factors
prevention: Balanced nutrition, regular physical activity, adequate sleep, and community/environmental support for healthy choices.

title: How is Asthma treated?
content_text: Q: How is Asthma treated? A: Controller medications (inhaled corticosteroids, long-acting beta-agonists) for long-term control, plus short-acting bronchodilators (e.g., albuterol) for acute symptom relief. Avoidance of known triggers is essential.
symptoms: Wheezing; shortness of breath; chest tightness; coughing, especially at night or early morning; difficulty sleeping due to breathing problems
treatments: Controller medications (inhaled corticosteroids, long-acting beta-agonists) for long-term control, plus short-acting bronchodilators (e.g., albuterol) for acute symptom relief. Avoidance of known triggers is essential.
risk_factors: Family history of asthma or allergies; exposure to tobacco smoke; air pollution; occupational exposure to irritants; respiratory infections in childhood
prevention: Avoiding known allergens and irritants, controlling indoor air quality, getting recommended vaccinations, and adherence to prescribed controller therapy.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood

title: What is Tuberculosis?
content_text: Q: What is Tuberculosis? A: Tuberculosis is a potentially serious infectious disease caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis that primarily affects the lungs, spread through airborne droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes.
symptoms: Persistent cough lasting three weeks or longer, sometimes with blood; chest pain; unintentional weight loss; fatigue; fever; night sweats; loss of appetite
treatments: Standard treatment involves a 6-9 month course of multiple antibiotics (isoniazid, rifampin, ethambutol, pyrazinamide), with directly observed therapy recommended to ensure adherence and prevent drug resistance.
risk_factors: Close contact with an infected person; weakened immune system (e.g., HIV); living in or traveling to areas with high TB rates; healthcare work; substance use
prevention: BCG vaccination in countries with high TB prevalence, prompt identification and treatment of active cases, and infection control measures in healthcare settings.
common_age_group: All ages, higher burden in low-resource settings

title: Is Migraine a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Migraine a chronic condition? A: Migraine is generally classified as a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management.
symptoms: Throbbing or pulsing headache, often one-sided; nausea and vomiting; sensitivity to light and sound; visual disturbances (aura); dizziness
treatments: Acute treatment includes NSAIDs or triptans for moderate to severe attacks. Preventive therapy (beta-blockers, anticonvulsants, CGRP inhibitors) is considered for frequent migraines.
risk_factors: Family history of migraine; hormonal changes (especially in women); stress; certain foods or food additives; changes in sleep patterns; sensory stimuli
prevention: Identifying and avoiding personal triggers, maintaining regular sleep and meal schedules, stress reduction techniques, and preventive medication when prescribed.

title: Can Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease be prevented?
content_text: Q: Can Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease be prevented? A: Avoiding smoking and secondhand smoke, reducing occupational and environmental exposure to lung irritants, and vaccination against respiratory infections.
symptoms: Chronic cough with mucus production; shortness of breath, especially during physical activity; wheezing; chest tightness; frequent respiratory infections
treatments: Bronchodilators (short and long-acting), inhaled corticosteroids for frequent exacerbations, pulmonary rehabilitation, oxygen therapy in advanced stages, and smoking cessation as the most critical intervention.
risk_factors: Smoking (primary cause); long-term exposure to air pollutants or occupational dust/chemicals; genetic factors (alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency); history of childhood respiratory infections
prevention: Avoiding smoking and secondhand smoke, reducing occupational and environmental exposure to lung irritants, and vaccination against respiratory infections.

title: Can Tuberculosis be prevented?

symptoms: Persistent cough lasting three weeks or longer, sometimes with blood; chest pain; unintentional weight loss; fatigue; fever; night sweats; loss of appetite

title: Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident) - Prevention Guide
content_text: Prevention strategies for Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident) focus on modifiable risk factors. Controlling blood pressure, cholesterol, and blood sugar, smoking cessation, regular exercise, and managing atrial fibrillation when present. Individuals with additional risk factors (High blood pressure; smoking; diabetes; high cholesterol; atrial fibrillation; obesity; family history of stroke; age over 55) should consult a healthcare provider for personalized guidance.
symptoms: Sudden numbness or weakness of face, arm, or leg, especially on one side; sudden confusion or trouble speaking; sudden vision problems; sudden trouble walking or loss of balance; sudden severe headache
treatments: Acute ischemic stroke may be treated with thrombolytic therapy (tPA) within a narrow time window or mechanical thrombectomy. Long-term management includes antiplatelet/anticoagulant therapy, blood pressure control, and rehabilitation.
risk_factors: High blood pressure; smoking; diabetes; high cholesterol; atrial fibrillation; obesity; family history of stroke; age over 55
prevention: Controlling blood pressure, cholesterol, and blood sugar, smoking cessation, regular exercise, and managing atrial fibrillation when present.

title: What is Essential Hypertension?
content_text: Q: What is Essential Hypertension? A: Essential Hypertension is a common chronic condition in which blood pressure in the arteries is persistently elevated without an identifiable secondary cause, increasing the risk of heart disease, stroke, and kidney damage over time.
symptoms: Often asymptomatic; occasional headaches; shortness of breath; nosebleeds; dizziness in severe cases
treatments: Lifestyle changes (DASH diet, sodium reduction, exercise, weight management) combined with antihypertensive medications such as ACE inhibitors, ARBs, calcium channel blockers, or thiazide diuretics depending on patient profile.
risk_factors: High sodium intake; obesity; sedentary lifestyle; excessive alcohol consumption; chronic stress; family history; aging
prevention: Reducing dietary sodium, maintaining healthy weight, limiting alcohol, regular aerobic exercise, and managing stress.
source_name: American Heart Association - High Blood Pressure

title: Coronary Artery Disease - Treatment Guideline
content_text: Clinical management of Coronary Artery Disease typically follows a stepwise approach. Lifestyle changes, statins for cholesterol management, antiplatelet agents (aspirin), beta-blockers, and in severe cases, procedures such as angioplasty with stenting or coronary artery bypass grafting. Treatment plans should be individualized based on patient comorbidities, preferences, and response to therapy.
symptoms: Chest pain or discomfort (angina); shortness of breath; fatigue; pain in arms or shoulders; cold sweats; nausea
treatments: Lifestyle changes, statins for cholesterol management, antiplatelet agents (aspirin), beta-blockers, and in severe cases, procedures such as angioplasty with stenting or coronary artery bypass grafting.
risk_factors: High cholesterol; high blood pressure; smoking; diabetes; obesity; family history of heart disease; physical inactivity; age
prevention: Heart-healthy diet, regular exercise, smoking cessation, cholesterol and blood pressure management, and routine cardiovascular screening.

title: Can Pneumonia be prevented?
content_text: Q: Can Pneumonia be prevented? A: Vaccination (pneumococcal, influenza), good hygiene, avoiding smoking, and managing underlying chronic conditions.
symptoms: Cough with phlegm; fever; chills; shortness of breath; chest pain when breathing or coughing; fatigue; confusion (especially in older adults)
treatments: Bacterial pneumonia is treated with antibiotics; viral pneumonia is managed with supportive care and antivirals when appropriate. Hospitalization may be required for severe cases.
risk_factors: Age under 2 or over 65; chronic diseases (asthma, COPD, heart disease); weakened immune system; smoking; recent respiratory infection; hospitalization
prevention: Vaccination (pneumococcal, influenza), good hygiene, avoiding smoking, and managing underlying chronic conditions.

risk_factors: Family history of asthma or allergies; exposure to tobacco smoke; air pollution; occupational exposure to irritants; respiratory infections in childhood

title: Is Generalized Anxiety Disorder contagious?
content_text: Q: Is Generalized Anxiety Disorder contagious? A: Based on current medical guidance, Generalized Anxiety Disorder is not contagious and does not spread between people.
symptoms: Excessive worry; restlessness; fatigue; difficulty concentrating; irritability; muscle tension; sleep disturbances

risk_factors: Family history of anxiety disorders; history of trauma; chronic health conditions; personality traits; substance use

title: What causes or increases the risk of Osteoarthritis?
content_text: Q: What causes or increases the risk of Osteoarthritis? A: Key risk factors include older age; obesity; joint injury; repetitive stress on joints; genetics; female sex.
symptoms: Joint pain that worsens with activity; stiffness, especially after rest; loss of flexibility; grating sensation; bone spurs; swelling around the joint
treatments: Weight management, physical therapy, pain relievers (acetaminophen, NSAIDs), and in advanced cases, joint replacement surgery.
risk_factors: Older age; obesity; joint injury; repetitive stress on joints; genetics; female sex
prevention: Maintaining healthy weight, regular low-impact exercise, avoiding repetitive joint stress, and proper injury management.

title: Is Asthma contagious?

treatments: Controller medications (inhaled corticosteroids, long-acting beta-agonists) for long-term control, plus short-acting bronchodilators (e.g., albuterol) for acute symptom relief. Avoidance of known triggers is essential.
risk_factors: Family history of asthma or allergies; exposure to tobacco smoke; air pollution; occupational exposure to irritants; respiratory infections in childhood
prevention: Avoiding known allergens and irritants, controlling indoor air quality, getting recommended vaccinations, and adherence to prescribed controller therapy.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood

title: Hypothyroidism - Treatment Guideline
content_text: Clinical management of Hypothyroidism typically follows a stepwise approach. Lifelong thyroid hormone replacement therapy (levothyroxine), with regular monitoring of thyroid hormone levels to adjust dosage. Treatment plans should be individualized based on patient comorbidities, preferences, and response to therapy.
symptoms: Fatigue; weight gain; cold intolerance; dry skin; constipation; hair thinning; depression; muscle weakness; slowed heart rate
treatments: Lifelong thyroid hormone replacement therapy (levothyroxine), with regular monitoring of thyroid hormone levels to adjust dosage.
risk_factors: Female sex; age over 60; family history of thyroid disease; autoimmune conditions; previous thyroid surgery or radiation treatment
prevention: No specific prevention exists for autoimmune causes; adequate dietary iodine intake helps prevent iodine-deficiency-related hypothyroidism.

title: Is Allergic Rhinitis a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Allergic Rhinitis a chronic condition? A: Allergic Rhinitis is generally classified as a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management.
symptoms: Sneezing; runny or stuffy nose; itchy or watery eyes; itchy throat; postnasal drip; fatigue
treatments: Antihistamines, intranasal corticosteroids, decongestants for short-term relief, and allergen immunotherapy for long-term management of severe cases.
risk_factors: Family history of allergies; other allergic conditions (asthma, eczema); exposure to environmental allergens; early childhood exposure to tobacco smoke
prevention: Avoiding known allergens, using air purifiers, keeping windows closed during high pollen seasons, and regular cleaning to reduce dust mites.
common_age_group: All ages, often begins in childhood or adolescence

title: Is Obesity contagious?

symptoms: Excess body fat; breathlessness on exertion; increased sweating; difficulty with physical activity; joint and back pain; low self-esteem

risk_factors: Poor diet; physical inactivity; genetics; certain medications; insufficient sleep; high stress levels; socioeconomic factors
prevention: Balanced nutrition, regular physical activity, adequate sleep, and community/environmental support for healthy choices.

title: Can Chronic Kidney Disease be prevented?
content_text: Q: Can Chronic Kidney Disease be prevented? A: Controlling blood pressure and blood sugar, avoiding NSAIDs when possible, maintaining healthy weight, and regular kidney function monitoring for at-risk individuals.
symptoms: Fatigue; swelling in legs, ankles, or feet; persistent itching; nausea; loss of appetite; muscle cramps; changes in urination frequency
treatments: Management focuses on slowing progression through blood pressure control (ACE inhibitors/ARBs), blood sugar management, dietary modifications (sodium and protein restriction), and treating underlying causes. Advanced stages may require dialysis or transplantation.
risk_factors: Diabetes; high blood pressure; family history of kidney disease; age over 60; obesity; smoking; cardiovascular disease
prevention: Controlling blood pressure and blood sugar, avoiding NSAIDs when possible, maintaining healthy weight, and regular kidney function monitoring for at-risk individuals.

title: Is Influenza a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Influenza a chronic condition? A: Influenza is generally classified as typically an acute (short-term) condition.
symptoms: Fever or feeling feverish; chills; cough; sore throat; runny or stuffy nose; muscle or body aches; headaches; fatigue
treatments: Supportive care (rest, fluids, antipyretics) for most cases. Antiviral medications such as oseltamivir may be prescribed within 48 hours of symptom onset for high-risk patients.
risk_factors: Age under 5 or over 65; pregnancy; chronic medical conditions (asthma, diabetes, heart disease); weakened immune system; close contact with infected individuals
prevention: Annual influenza vaccination, frequent handwashing, avoiding close contact with sick individuals, and covering coughs and sneezes.

title: Psoriasis - Prevention Guide
content_text: Prevention strategies for Psoriasis focus on modifiable risk factors. No definitive prevention exists; managing triggers such as stress, smoking, and certain medications may reduce flare frequency. Individuals with additional risk factors (Family history of psoriasis; smoking; obesity; stress; certain infections; certain medications (e.g., lithium, beta-blockers)) should consult a healthcare provider for personalized guidance.
symptoms: Red patches of skin covered with thick, silvery scales; dry, cracked skin that may bleed; itching, burning, or soreness; thickened or ridged nails; stiff and swollen joints (in psoriatic arthritis)
treatments: Topical treatments (corticosteroids, vitamin D analogs) for mild cases, phototherapy for moderate cases, and systemic agents or biologics for moderate-to-severe disease.
risk_factors: Family history of psoriasis; smoking; obesity; stress; certain infections; certain medications (e.g., lithium, beta-blockers)
prevention: No definitive prevention exists; managing triggers such as stress, smoking, and certain medications may reduce flare frequency.

title: Hyperlipidemia - Overview
content_text: A condition characterized by abnormally high levels of lipids (cholesterol and/or triglycerides) in the blood, a major risk factor for atherosclerosis, heart disease, and stroke.
symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; in severe cases, fatty deposits around the eyes or joints (xanthomas); occasionally associated with pancreatitis symptoms if triglycerides are very high
treatments: Lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, weight loss) as first-line therapy, with statins as the primary pharmacologic treatment; additional agents (ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors) for high-risk patients.
risk_factors: Diet high in saturated fats; obesity; physical inactivity; smoking; diabetes; family history of high cholesterol; age
prevention: Heart-healthy diet low in saturated and trans fats, regular physical activity, maintaining healthy weight, and routine lipid screening.

title: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus - Overview
content_text: A chronic metabolic disorder characterized by insulin resistance and relative insulin deficiency, leading to persistently elevated blood glucose levels. It develops gradually, often over years, and is strongly linked to obesity, sedentary lifestyle, and genetic predisposition.
symptoms: Increased thirst; frequent urination; fatigue; blurred vision; slow-healing sores; unexplained weight loss; numbness or tingling in hands or feet
treatments: First-line management includes lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, weight loss) and metformin. Additional agents such as SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 receptor agonists, sulfonylureas, or insulin are added based on glycemic targets and comorbidities.
risk_factors: Obesity; family history of diabetes; age over 45; physical inactivity; history of gestational diabetes; high blood pressure
prevention: Maintaining healthy body weight, regular physical activity (150 minutes/week), balanced diet low in refined sugars, and routine screening for at-risk individuals.

title: Who is most commonly affected by Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident)?
content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident)? A: Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident) most commonly affects Adults 55+.
symptoms: Sudden numbness or weakness of face, arm, or leg, especially on one side; sudden confusion or trouble speaking; sudden vision problems; sudden trouble walking or loss of balance; sudden severe headache
treatments: Acute ischemic stroke may be treated with thrombolytic therapy (tPA) within a narrow time window or mechanical thrombectomy. Long-term management includes antiplatelet/anticoagulant therapy, blood pressure control, and rehabilitation.
risk_factors: High blood pressure; smoking; diabetes; high cholesterol; atrial fibrillation; obesity; family history of stroke; age over 55
prevention: Controlling blood pressure, cholesterol, and blood sugar, smoking cessation, regular exercise, and managing atrial fibrillation when present.

title: Iron Deficiency Anemia - Clinical Decision Support Summary
content_text: Clinical decision support summary for Iron Deficiency Anemia (ICD-10: D50). Category: Hematologic. Typical severity: Mild to Moderate. Chronicity: typically an acute (short-term) condition. Transmission risk: not considered contagious and does not spread between people. Primary risk factors: Poor dietary iron intake; heavy menstrual periods; pregnancy; gastrointestinal bleeding; chronic conditions affecting absorption (e.g., celiac disease). Recommended initial management: Oral or intravenous iron supplementation, dietary modification to increase iron-rich foods, and treatment of underlying causes such as bleeding sources.
symptoms: Fatigue; pale skin; shortness of breath; dizziness; brittle nails; headaches; cold hands and feet; unusual cravings for non-nutritive substances (pica)
treatments: Oral or intravenous iron supplementation, dietary modification to increase iron-rich foods, and treatment of underlying causes such as bleeding sources.
risk_factors: Poor dietary iron intake; heavy menstrual periods; pregnancy; gastrointestinal bleeding; chronic conditions affecting absorption (e.g., celiac disease)
prevention: Eating iron-rich foods (lean meat, beans, fortified cereals), pairing with vitamin C for absorption, and screening in high-risk groups such as pregnant women.
common_age_group: Women of reproductive age; young children

title: What is Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus?
content_text: Q: What is Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus? A: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus is a chronic metabolic disorder characterized by insulin resistance and relative insulin deficiency, leading to persistently elevated blood glucose levels. It develops gradually, often over years, and is strongly linked to obesity, sedentary lifestyle, and genetic predisposition.
symptoms: Increased thirst; frequent urination; fatigue; blurred vision; slow-healing sores; unexplained weight loss; numbness or tingling in hands or feet
treatments: First-line management includes lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, weight loss) and metformin. Additional agents such as SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 receptor agonists, sulfonylureas, or insulin are added based on glycemic targets and comorbidities.
risk_factors: Obesity; family history of diabetes; age over 45; physical inactivity; history of gestational diabetes; high blood pressure
prevention: Maintaining healthy body weight, regular physical activity (150 minutes/week), balanced diet low in refined sugars, and routine screening for at-risk individuals.

title: What is Pneumonia?
content_text: Q: What is Pneumonia? A: Pneumonia is an infection that inflames the air sacs in one or both lungs, which may fill with fluid or pus, causing cough, fever, chills, and difficulty breathing; can be caused by bacteria, viruses, or fungi.
symptoms: Cough with phlegm; fever; chills; shortness of breath; chest pain when breathing or coughing; fatigue; confusion (especially in older adults)
treatments: Bacterial pneumonia is treated with antibiotics; viral pneumonia is managed with supportive care and antivirals when appropriate. Hospitalization may be required for severe cases.
risk_factors: Age under 2 or over 65; chronic diseases (asthma, COPD, heart disease); weakened immune system; smoking; recent respiratory infection; hospitalization
prevention: Vaccination (pneumococcal, influenza), good hygiene, avoiding smoking, and managing underlying chronic conditions.

title: Is Osteoarthritis contagious?
content_text: Q: Is Osteoarthritis contagious? A: Based on current medical guidance, Osteoarthritis is not contagious and does not spread between people.
symptoms: Joint pain that worsens with activity; stiffness, especially after rest; loss of flexibility; grating sensation; bone spurs; swelling around the joint
treatments: Weight management, physical therapy, pain relievers (acetaminophen, NSAIDs), and in advanced cases, joint replacement surgery.
risk_factors: Older age; obesity; joint injury; repetitive stress on joints; genetics; female sex
prevention: Maintaining healthy weight, regular low-impact exercise, avoiding repetitive joint stress, and proper injury management.

title: What is Obesity?
content_text: Q: What is Obesity? A: Obesity is a complex condition involving an excessive amount of body fat, increasing the risk of numerous health problems including diabetes, heart disease, and certain cancers, generally defined by a BMI of 30 or higher.
symptoms: Excess body fat; breathlessness on exertion; increased sweating; difficulty with physical activity; joint and back pain; low self-esteem
treatments: Comprehensive lifestyle intervention including dietary changes, increased physical activity, and behavioral therapy. Pharmacotherapy (e.g., GLP-1 agonists) or bariatric surgery may be considered for severe cases.
risk_factors: Poor diet; physical inactivity; genetics; certain medications; insufficient sleep; high stress levels; socioeconomic factors
prevention: Balanced nutrition, regular physical activity, adequate sleep, and community/environmental support for healthy choices.

title: What causes or increases the risk of Pneumonia?
content_text: Q: What causes or increases the risk of Pneumonia? A: Key risk factors include age under 2 or over 65; chronic diseases (asthma, COPD, heart disease); weakened immune system; smoking; recent respiratory infection; hospitalization.
symptoms: Cough with phlegm; fever; chills; shortness of breath; chest pain when breathing or coughing; fatigue; confusion (especially in older adults)
