prevention: Vaccination (pneumococcal, influenza), good hygiene, avoiding smoking, and managing underlying chronic conditions.

title: Who is most commonly affected by Essential Hypertension?
content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Essential Hypertension? A: Essential Hypertension most commonly affects Adults 30+.
symptoms: Often asymptomatic; occasional headaches; shortness of breath; nosebleeds; dizziness in severe cases
treatments: Lifestyle changes (DASH diet, sodium reduction, exercise, weight management) combined with antihypertensive medications such as ACE inhibitors, ARBs, calcium channel blockers, or thiazide diuretics depending on patient profile.
risk_factors: High sodium intake; obesity; sedentary lifestyle; excessive alcohol consumption; chronic stress; family history; aging
prevention: Reducing dietary sodium, maintaining healthy weight, limiting alcohol, regular aerobic exercise, and managing stress.
source_name: American Heart Association - High Blood Pressure

title: Who is most commonly affected by Peptic Ulcer Disease?
content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Peptic Ulcer Disease? A: Peptic Ulcer Disease most commonly affects Adults 30-60.
symptoms: Burning stomach pain; bloating; heartburn; nausea; intolerance to fatty foods; in severe cases, dark stools or vomiting blood
treatments: Antibiotic regimens to eradicate H. pylori when present, proton pump inhibitors to reduce stomach acid, and discontinuation of NSAIDs when possible.
risk_factors: H. pylori infection; regular use of NSAIDs; smoking; excessive alcohol consumption; stress (as a contributing factor)
prevention: Limiting NSAID use, avoiding excessive alcohol, not smoking, and prompt treatment of H. pylori infection.

title: Who is most commonly affected by Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis)?
content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis)? A: Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis) most commonly affects Children, with many cases persisting into adulthood.
symptoms: Dry, scaly skin; intense itching; red to brownish-gray patches; small raised bumps that may leak fluid when scratched; thickened, cracked skin
treatments: Regular moisturizing, topical corticosteroids for flare-ups, antihistamines for itching, and identification/avoidance of triggers. Severe cases may require systemic immunomodulators.
risk_factors: Family history of eczema, asthma, or hay fever; dry skin; environmental irritants; stress; certain climates
prevention: Regular moisturizing, avoiding harsh soaps and known irritants, using lukewarm water for bathing, and wearing soft, breathable fabrics.
common_age_group: Children, with many cases persisting into adulthood

title: What causes or increases the risk of Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident)?
content_text: Q: What causes or increases the risk of Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident)? A: Key risk factors include high blood pressure; smoking; diabetes; high cholesterol; atrial fibrillation; obesity; family history of stroke; age over 55.
symptoms: Sudden numbness or weakness of face, arm, or leg, especially on one side; sudden confusion or trouble speaking; sudden vision problems; sudden trouble walking or loss of balance; sudden severe headache
treatments: Acute ischemic stroke may be treated with thrombolytic therapy (tPA) within a narrow time window or mechanical thrombectomy. Long-term management includes antiplatelet/anticoagulant therapy, blood pressure control, and rehabilitation.
risk_factors: High blood pressure; smoking; diabetes; high cholesterol; atrial fibrillation; obesity; family history of stroke; age over 55
prevention: Controlling blood pressure, cholesterol, and blood sugar, smoking cessation, regular exercise, and managing atrial fibrillation when present.

title: Hyperlipidemia - Treatment Guideline
content_text: Clinical management of Hyperlipidemia typically follows a stepwise approach. Lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, weight loss) as first-line therapy, with statins as the primary pharmacologic treatment; additional agents (ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors) for high-risk patients. Treatment plans should be individualized based on patient comorbidities, preferences, and response to therapy.
symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; in severe cases, fatty deposits around the eyes or joints (xanthomas); occasionally associated with pancreatitis symptoms if triglycerides are very high
treatments: Lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, weight loss) as first-line therapy, with statins as the primary pharmacologic treatment; additional agents (ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors) for high-risk patients.
risk_factors: Diet high in saturated fats; obesity; physical inactivity; smoking; diabetes; family history of high cholesterol; age
prevention: Heart-healthy diet low in saturated and trans fats, regular physical activity, maintaining healthy weight, and routine lipid screening.

title: How is Iron Deficiency Anemia treated?
content_text: Q: How is Iron Deficiency Anemia treated? A: Oral or intravenous iron supplementation, dietary modification to increase iron-rich foods, and treatment of underlying causes such as bleeding sources.
symptoms: Fatigue; pale skin; shortness of breath; dizziness; brittle nails; headaches; cold hands and feet; unusual cravings for non-nutritive substances (pica)
treatments: Oral or intravenous iron supplementation, dietary modification to increase iron-rich foods, and treatment of underlying causes such as bleeding sources.
risk_factors: Poor dietary iron intake; heavy menstrual periods; pregnancy; gastrointestinal bleeding; chronic conditions affecting absorption (e.g., celiac disease)
prevention: Eating iron-rich foods (lean meat, beans, fortified cereals), pairing with vitamin C for absorption, and screening in high-risk groups such as pregnant women.
common_age_group: Women of reproductive age; young children

title: Can Rheumatoid Arthritis be prevented?
content_text: Q: Can Rheumatoid Arthritis be prevented? A: No definitive prevention exists, but smoking cessation and maintaining healthy weight may reduce risk; early diagnosis and treatment slow disease progression.
symptoms: Joint pain and swelling, often symmetrical; morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes; fatigue; low-grade fever; joint deformity in advanced stages
treatments: Disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs) such as methotrexate, biologic agents, NSAIDs for symptom relief, and physical therapy to maintain joint function.
risk_factors: Female sex; family history of autoimmune disease; smoking; obesity; age between 30 and 60
prevention: No definitive prevention exists, but smoking cessation and maintaining healthy weight may reduce risk; early diagnosis and treatment slow disease progression.

title: Chickenpox (Varicella) - Treatment Guideline
content_text: Clinical management of Chickenpox (Varicella) typically follows a stepwise approach. Supportive care including antihistamines for itching, calamine lotion, and antipyretics. Antiviral medication (acyclovir) may be used for high-risk individuals or severe cases. Treatment plans should be individualized based on patient comorbidities, preferences, and response to therapy.
symptoms: Itchy rash with fluid-filled blisters; fever; tiredness; loss of appetite; headache; the rash typically appears in waves over several days
treatments: Supportive care including antihistamines for itching, calamine lotion, and antipyretics. Antiviral medication (acyclovir) may be used for high-risk individuals or severe cases.
risk_factors: Lack of vaccination; close contact with an infected person; weakened immune system; age under 12 (unvaccinated)
prevention: Varicella vaccination, typically given in two doses during childhood, is highly effective at preventing infection or reducing severity.

title: Is Generalized Anxiety Disorder a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Generalized Anxiety Disorder a chronic condition? A: Generalized Anxiety Disorder is generally classified as a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management.
symptoms: Excessive worry; restlessness; fatigue; difficulty concentrating; irritability; muscle tension; sleep disturbances
treatments: Cognitive behavioral therapy is highly effective; medications such as SSRIs, SNRIs, or buspirone may be used. Relaxation techniques and lifestyle changes support management.
risk_factors: Family history of anxiety disorders; history of trauma; chronic health conditions; personality traits; substance use
prevention: Stress management techniques, regular physical activity, limiting caffeine and alcohol, and adequate sleep hygiene.

title: Who is most commonly affected by Asthma?
content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Asthma? A: Asthma most commonly affects All ages, often diagnosed in childhood.
symptoms: Wheezing; shortness of breath; chest tightness; coughing, especially at night or early morning; difficulty sleeping due to breathing problems
treatments: Controller medications (inhaled corticosteroids, long-acting beta-agonists) for long-term control, plus short-acting bronchodilators (e.g., albuterol) for acute symptom relief. Avoidance of known triggers is essential.
risk_factors: Family history of asthma or allergies; exposure to tobacco smoke; air pollution; occupational exposure to irritants; respiratory infections in childhood
prevention: Avoiding known allergens and irritants, controlling indoor air quality, getting recommended vaccinations, and adherence to prescribed controller therapy.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood

title: Who is most commonly affected by Urinary Tract Infection?
content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Urinary Tract Infection? A: Urinary Tract Infection most commonly affects Women of all ages, older adults.
symptoms: Strong, persistent urge to urinate; burning sensation during urination; cloudy or strong-smelling urine; pelvic pain in women; frequent, small amounts of urine
treatments: Short course of antibiotics (e.g., nitrofurantoin, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) tailored to local resistance patterns. Increased fluid intake supports recovery.
risk_factors: Female anatomy; sexual activity; certain types of birth control; menopause; urinary tract abnormalities; catheter use; weakened immune system
prevention: Adequate hydration, urinating after intercourse, proper hygiene practices, and avoiding irritating feminine products.
common_age_group: Women of all ages, older adults

title: Is Urinary Tract Infection contagious?
content_text: Q: Is Urinary Tract Infection contagious? A: Based on current medical guidance, Urinary Tract Infection is not contagious and does not spread between people.

title: Is Psoriasis contagious?
content_text: Q: Is Psoriasis contagious? A: Based on current medical guidance, Psoriasis is not contagious and does not spread between people.
symptoms: Red patches of skin covered with thick, silvery scales; dry, cracked skin that may bleed; itching, burning, or soreness; thickened or ridged nails; stiff and swollen joints (in psoriatic arthritis)
treatments: Topical treatments (corticosteroids, vitamin D analogs) for mild cases, phototherapy for moderate cases, and systemic agents or biologics for moderate-to-severe disease.
risk_factors: Family history of psoriasis; smoking; obesity; stress; certain infections; certain medications (e.g., lithium, beta-blockers)
prevention: No definitive prevention exists; managing triggers such as stress, smoking, and certain medications may reduce flare frequency.

title: What is Malaria?
content_text: Q: What is Malaria? A: Malaria is a life-threatening disease caused by Plasmodium parasites transmitted to humans through the bites of infected female Anopheles mosquitoes, most prevalent in tropical and subtropical regions.
symptoms: Fever and chills; headache; nausea and vomiting; muscle pain and fatigue; sweating; in severe cases, confusion, seizures, or difficulty breathing
treatments: Artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs) are the standard treatment for uncomplicated malaria. Severe malaria requires hospitalization and intravenous antimalarial drugs.
risk_factors: Living in or traveling to malaria-endemic regions; lack of access to preventive measures (bed nets, prophylaxis); pregnancy; young age
prevention: Use of insecticide-treated bed nets, indoor residual spraying, antimalarial prophylaxis for travelers, and prompt diagnosis and treatment of cases.
common_age_group: Children under 5 in endemic regions; travelers

title: What causes or increases the risk of Allergic Rhinitis?
content_text: Q: What causes or increases the risk of Allergic Rhinitis? A: Key risk factors include family history of allergies; other allergic conditions (asthma, eczema); exposure to environmental allergens; early childhood exposure to tobacco smoke.
symptoms: Sneezing; runny or stuffy nose; itchy or watery eyes; itchy throat; postnasal drip; fatigue
treatments: Antihistamines, intranasal corticosteroids, decongestants for short-term relief, and allergen immunotherapy for long-term management of severe cases.
risk_factors: Family history of allergies; other allergic conditions (asthma, eczema); exposure to environmental allergens; early childhood exposure to tobacco smoke
prevention: Avoiding known allergens, using air purifiers, keeping windows closed during high pollen seasons, and regular cleaning to reduce dust mites.
common_age_group: All ages, often begins in childhood or adolescence

title: How is Major Depressive Disorder treated?
content_text: Q: How is Major Depressive Disorder treated? A: Psychotherapy (cognitive behavioral therapy, interpersonal therapy) and/or antidepressant medications (SSRIs, SNRIs). Severe cases may require combination therapy or, rarely, electroconvulsive therapy.
symptoms: Persistent sad or empty mood; loss of interest in hobbies; fatigue or low energy; difficulty concentrating; changes in appetite or weight; sleep disturbances; thoughts of death or suicide
treatments: Psychotherapy (cognitive behavioral therapy, interpersonal therapy) and/or antidepressant medications (SSRIs, SNRIs). Severe cases may require combination therapy or, rarely, electroconvulsive therapy.
risk_factors: Family history of depression; history of trauma or abuse; chronic illness; substance use; major life changes or stress; certain medications
prevention: Stress management, strong social support networks, regular physical activity, adequate sleep, and early intervention for at-risk individuals.

title: What are the common symptoms of Influenza?
content_text: Q: What are the common symptoms of Influenza? A: Common symptoms include fever or feeling feverish; chills; cough; sore throat; runny or stuffy nose; muscle or body aches; headaches; fatigue.
symptoms: Fever or feeling feverish; chills; cough; sore throat; runny or stuffy nose; muscle or body aches; headaches; fatigue
treatments: Supportive care (rest, fluids, antipyretics) for most cases. Antiviral medications such as oseltamivir may be prescribed within 48 hours of symptom onset for high-risk patients.
risk_factors: Age under 5 or over 65; pregnancy; chronic medical conditions (asthma, diabetes, heart disease); weakened immune system; close contact with infected individuals
prevention: Annual influenza vaccination, frequent handwashing, avoiding close contact with sick individuals, and covering coughs and sneezes.

title: Is Chronic Kidney Disease a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Chronic Kidney Disease a chronic condition? A: Chronic Kidney Disease is generally classified as a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management.
symptoms: Fatigue; swelling in legs, ankles, or feet; persistent itching; nausea; loss of appetite; muscle cramps; changes in urination frequency
treatments: Management focuses on slowing progression through blood pressure control (ACE inhibitors/ARBs), blood sugar management, dietary modifications (sodium and protein restriction), and treating underlying causes. Advanced stages may require dialysis or transplantation.
risk_factors: Diabetes; high blood pressure; family history of kidney disease; age over 60; obesity; smoking; cardiovascular disease
prevention: Controlling blood pressure and blood sugar, avoiding NSAIDs when possible, maintaining healthy weight, and regular kidney function monitoring for at-risk individuals.

title: Is Celiac Disease a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Celiac Disease a chronic condition? A: Celiac Disease is generally classified as a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management.
symptoms: Diarrhea or constipation; bloating and gas; abdominal pain; fatigue; weight loss; anemia; skin rash (dermatitis herpetiformis); in children, failure to thrive
treatments: The only effective treatment is a strict, lifelong gluten-free diet. Nutritional supplementation may be needed to correct deficiencies (iron, vitamin D, B12, folate).
risk_factors: Family history of celiac disease or autoimmune disorders; type 1 diabetes; Down syndrome; autoimmune thyroid disease
prevention: No known prevention; early diagnosis and dietary management prevent complications such as malnutrition and osteoporosis.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood or 20s-30s

title: Can Hypothyroidism be prevented?
content_text: Q: Can Hypothyroidism be prevented? A: No specific prevention exists for autoimmune causes; adequate dietary iodine intake helps prevent iodine-deficiency-related hypothyroidism.
symptoms: Fatigue; weight gain; cold intolerance; dry skin; constipation; hair thinning; depression; muscle weakness; slowed heart rate
treatments: Lifelong thyroid hormone replacement therapy (levothyroxine), with regular monitoring of thyroid hormone levels to adjust dosage.
risk_factors: Female sex; age over 60; family history of thyroid disease; autoimmune conditions; previous thyroid surgery or radiation treatment
prevention: No specific prevention exists for autoimmune causes; adequate dietary iodine intake helps prevent iodine-deficiency-related hypothyroidism.

title: What are the common symptoms of Iron Deficiency Anemia?
content_text: Q: What are the common symptoms of Iron Deficiency Anemia? A: Common symptoms include fatigue; pale skin; shortness of breath; dizziness; brittle nails; headaches; cold hands and feet; unusual cravings for non-nutritive substances (pica).
symptoms: Fatigue; pale skin; shortness of breath; dizziness; brittle nails; headaches; cold hands and feet; unusual cravings for non-nutritive substances (pica)
treatments: Oral or intravenous iron supplementation, dietary modification to increase iron-rich foods, and treatment of underlying causes such as bleeding sources.
risk_factors: Poor dietary iron intake; heavy menstrual periods; pregnancy; gastrointestinal bleeding; chronic conditions affecting absorption (e.g., celiac disease)
prevention: Eating iron-rich foods (lean meat, beans, fortified cereals), pairing with vitamin C for absorption, and screening in high-risk groups such as pregnant women.
common_age_group: Women of reproductive age; young children

title: Celiac Disease - Treatment Guideline
content_text: Clinical management of Celiac Disease typically follows a stepwise approach. The only effective treatment is a strict, lifelong gluten-free diet. Nutritional supplementation may be needed to correct deficiencies (iron, vitamin D, B12, folate). Treatment plans should be individualized based on patient comorbidities, preferences, and response to therapy.
symptoms: Diarrhea or constipation; bloating and gas; abdominal pain; fatigue; weight loss; anemia; skin rash (dermatitis herpetiformis); in children, failure to thrive
treatments: The only effective treatment is a strict, lifelong gluten-free diet. Nutritional supplementation may be needed to correct deficiencies (iron, vitamin D, B12, folate).
risk_factors: Family history of celiac disease or autoimmune disorders; type 1 diabetes; Down syndrome; autoimmune thyroid disease
prevention: No known prevention; early diagnosis and dietary management prevent complications such as malnutrition and osteoporosis.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood or 20s-30s

title: Psoriasis - Overview
content_text: A chronic autoimmune condition that causes rapid buildup of skin cells, resulting in scaling, inflammation, and thick, silvery, scaly patches, often on the elbows, knees, and scalp.
symptoms: Red patches of skin covered with thick, silvery scales; dry, cracked skin that may bleed; itching, burning, or soreness; thickened or ridged nails; stiff and swollen joints (in psoriatic arthritis)
treatments: Topical treatments (corticosteroids, vitamin D analogs) for mild cases, phototherapy for moderate cases, and systemic agents or biologics for moderate-to-severe disease.
risk_factors: Family history of psoriasis; smoking; obesity; stress; certain infections; certain medications (e.g., lithium, beta-blockers)
prevention: No definitive prevention exists; managing triggers such as stress, smoking, and certain medications may reduce flare frequency.

title: Is Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease a chronic condition? A: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease is generally classified as a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management.
symptoms: Chronic cough with mucus production; shortness of breath, especially during physical activity; wheezing; chest tightness; frequent respiratory infections
treatments: Bronchodilators (short and long-acting), inhaled corticosteroids for frequent exacerbations, pulmonary rehabilitation, oxygen therapy in advanced stages, and smoking cessation as the most critical intervention.
risk_factors: Smoking (primary cause); long-term exposure to air pollutants or occupational dust/chemicals; genetic factors (alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency); history of childhood respiratory infections
prevention: Avoiding smoking and secondhand smoke, reducing occupational and environmental exposure to lung irritants, and vaccination against respiratory infections.

title: Is Major Depressive Disorder contagious?
content_text: Q: Is Major Depressive Disorder contagious? A: Based on current medical guidance, Major Depressive Disorder is not contagious and does not spread between people.
symptoms: Persistent sad or empty mood; loss of interest in hobbies; fatigue or low energy; difficulty concentrating; changes in appetite or weight; sleep disturbances; thoughts of death or suicide
treatments: Psychotherapy (cognitive behavioral therapy, interpersonal therapy) and/or antidepressant medications (SSRIs, SNRIs). Severe cases may require combination therapy or, rarely, electroconvulsive therapy.
risk_factors: Family history of depression; history of trauma or abuse; chronic illness; substance use; major life changes or stress; certain medications
prevention: Stress management, strong social support networks, regular physical activity, adequate sleep, and early intervention for at-risk individuals.

title: Tuberculosis - Symptom Guide
content_text: Patients with Tuberculosis commonly present with the following symptoms: Persistent cough lasting three weeks or longer, sometimes with blood; chest pain; unintentional weight loss; fatigue; fever; night sweats; loss of appetite. Symptom severity and combination vary by individual and disease stage.
symptoms: Persistent cough lasting three weeks or longer, sometimes with blood; chest pain; unintentional weight loss; fatigue; fever; night sweats; loss of appetite
treatments: Standard treatment involves a 6-9 month course of multiple antibiotics (isoniazid, rifampin, ethambutol, pyrazinamide), with directly observed therapy recommended to ensure adherence and prevent drug resistance.
risk_factors: Close contact with an infected person; weakened immune system (e.g., HIV); living in or traveling to areas with high TB rates; healthcare work; substance use
prevention: BCG vaccination in countries with high TB prevalence, prompt identification and treatment of active cases, and infection control measures in healthcare settings.
common_age_group: All ages, higher burden in low-resource settings

title: Influenza - Symptom Guide
content_text: Patients with Influenza commonly present with the following symptoms: Fever or feeling feverish; chills; cough; sore throat; runny or stuffy nose; muscle or body aches; headaches; fatigue. Symptom severity and combination vary by individual and disease stage.
symptoms: Fever or feeling feverish; chills; cough; sore throat; runny or stuffy nose; muscle or body aches; headaches; fatigue
treatments: Supportive care (rest, fluids, antipyretics) for most cases. Antiviral medications such as oseltamivir may be prescribed within 48 hours of symptom onset for high-risk patients.
risk_factors: Age under 5 or over 65; pregnancy; chronic medical conditions (asthma, diabetes, heart disease); weakened immune system; close contact with infected individuals
prevention: Annual influenza vaccination, frequent handwashing, avoiding close contact with sick individuals, and covering coughs and sneezes.

title: What causes or increases the risk of Major Depressive Disorder?
content_text: Q: What causes or increases the risk of Major Depressive Disorder? A: Key risk factors include family history of depression; history of trauma or abuse; chronic illness; substance use; major life changes or stress; certain medications.

title: Sleep Apnea - Clinical Decision Support Summary
content_text: Clinical decision support summary for Sleep Apnea (ICD-10: G47.33). Category: Respiratory. Typical severity: Mild to Severe. Chronicity: a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management. Transmission risk: not considered contagious and does not spread between people. Primary risk factors: Obesity; older age; male sex; large neck circumference; family history; smoking; nasal congestion; use of alcohol or sedatives. Recommended initial management: Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) therapy is the standard treatment. Weight loss, positional therapy, oral appliances, and in select cases, surgery, may also be used.
symptoms: Loud snoring; episodes of stopped breathing observed by others; gasping for air during sleep; excessive daytime sleepiness; morning headaches; difficulty concentrating; irritability
treatments: Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) therapy is the standard treatment. Weight loss, positional therapy, oral appliances, and in select cases, surgery, may also be used.
risk_factors: Obesity; older age; male sex; large neck circumference; family history; smoking; nasal congestion; use of alcohol or sedatives
prevention: Maintaining healthy weight, avoiding alcohol and sedatives before sleep, sleeping on one's side, and treating nasal congestion.

title: Epilepsy - Overview
content_text: A chronic neurological disorder characterized by recurrent, unprovoked seizures caused by abnormal electrical activity in the brain, varying widely in type and severity among individuals.
symptoms: Recurrent seizures (varying from brief staring spells to convulsions); temporary confusion; uncontrollable jerking movements; loss of consciousness; psychic symptoms such as fear or anxiety before a seizure
treatments: Antiepileptic drugs (e.g., levetiracetam, valproate, lamotrigine) are the mainstay of treatment, with surgery, dietary therapy (ketogenic diet), or neurostimulation devices considered for drug-resistant cases.
risk_factors: Family history of epilepsy; brain injury or trauma; stroke; brain infections; developmental disorders; prenatal injury
prevention: Preventing head injuries, managing cardiovascular risk factors to reduce stroke risk, and prompt treatment of infections that can affect the brain.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood or after age 60

title: Coronary Artery Disease - Overview
content_text: A condition in which the coronary arteries become narrowed or blocked due to buildup of cholesterol-containing plaques (atherosclerosis), reducing blood flow to the heart muscle and increasing the risk of heart attack.
symptoms: Chest pain or discomfort (angina); shortness of breath; fatigue; pain in arms or shoulders; cold sweats; nausea
treatments: Lifestyle changes, statins for cholesterol management, antiplatelet agents (aspirin), beta-blockers, and in severe cases, procedures such as angioplasty with stenting or coronary artery bypass grafting.
risk_factors: High cholesterol; high blood pressure; smoking; diabetes; obesity; family history of heart disease; physical inactivity; age
prevention: Heart-healthy diet, regular exercise, smoking cessation, cholesterol and blood pressure management, and routine cardiovascular screening.

title: Rheumatoid Arthritis - Prevention Guide
content_text: Prevention strategies for Rheumatoid Arthritis focus on modifiable risk factors. No definitive prevention exists, but smoking cessation and maintaining healthy weight may reduce risk; early diagnosis and treatment slow disease progression. Individuals with additional risk factors (Female sex; family history of autoimmune disease; smoking; obesity; age between 30 and 60) should consult a healthcare provider for personalized guidance.
symptoms: Joint pain and swelling, often symmetrical; morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes; fatigue; low-grade fever; joint deformity in advanced stages
treatments: Disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs) such as methotrexate, biologic agents, NSAIDs for symptom relief, and physical therapy to maintain joint function.
risk_factors: Female sex; family history of autoimmune disease; smoking; obesity; age between 30 and 60
prevention: No definitive prevention exists, but smoking cessation and maintaining healthy weight may reduce risk; early diagnosis and treatment slow disease progression.

title: Essential Hypertension - Symptom Guide
content_text: Patients with Essential Hypertension commonly present with the following symptoms: Often asymptomatic; occasional headaches; shortness of breath; nosebleeds; dizziness in severe cases. Symptom severity and combination vary by individual and disease stage.
symptoms: Often asymptomatic; occasional headaches; shortness of breath; nosebleeds; dizziness in severe cases
treatments: Lifestyle changes (DASH diet, sodium reduction, exercise, weight management) combined with antihypertensive medications such as ACE inhibitors, ARBs, calcium channel blockers, or thiazide diuretics depending on patient profile.
risk_factors: High sodium intake; obesity; sedentary lifestyle; excessive alcohol consumption; chronic stress; family history; aging
prevention: Reducing dietary sodium, maintaining healthy weight, limiting alcohol, regular aerobic exercise, and managing stress.
source_name: American Heart Association - High Blood Pressure

title: Is Malaria a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Malaria a chronic condition? A: Malaria is generally classified as typically an acute (short-term) condition.
symptoms: Fever and chills; headache; nausea and vomiting; muscle pain and fatigue; sweating; in severe cases, confusion, seizures, or difficulty breathing
treatments: Artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs) are the standard treatment for uncomplicated malaria. Severe malaria requires hospitalization and intravenous antimalarial drugs.
risk_factors: Living in or traveling to malaria-endemic regions; lack of access to preventive measures (bed nets, prophylaxis); pregnancy; young age
prevention: Use of insecticide-treated bed nets, indoor residual spraying, antimalarial prophylaxis for travelers, and prompt diagnosis and treatment of cases.
common_age_group: Children under 5 in endemic regions; travelers

title: Hypothyroidism - Clinical Decision Support Summary
content_text: Clinical decision support summary for Hypothyroidism (ICD-10: E03). Category: Endocrine. Typical severity: Mild to Moderate. Chronicity: a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management. Transmission risk: not considered contagious and does not spread between people. Primary risk factors: Female sex; age over 60; family history of thyroid disease; autoimmune conditions; previous thyroid surgery or radiation treatment. Recommended initial management: Lifelong thyroid hormone replacement therapy (levothyroxine), with regular monitoring of thyroid hormone levels to adjust dosage.
symptoms: Fatigue; weight gain; cold intolerance; dry skin; constipation; hair thinning; depression; muscle weakness; slowed heart rate
treatments: Lifelong thyroid hormone replacement therapy (levothyroxine), with regular monitoring of thyroid hormone levels to adjust dosage.
risk_factors: Female sex; age over 60; family history of thyroid disease; autoimmune conditions; previous thyroid surgery or radiation treatment
prevention: No specific prevention exists for autoimmune causes; adequate dietary iodine intake helps prevent iodine-deficiency-related hypothyroidism.

title: What causes or increases the risk of Chickenpox (Varicella)?
content_text: Q: What causes or increases the risk of Chickenpox (Varicella)? A: Key risk factors include lack of vaccination; close contact with an infected person; weakened immune system; age under 12 (unvaccinated).
symptoms: Itchy rash with fluid-filled blisters; fever; tiredness; loss of appetite; headache; the rash typically appears in waves over several days
treatments: Supportive care including antihistamines for itching, calamine lotion, and antipyretics. Antiviral medication (acyclovir) may be used for high-risk individuals or severe cases.
risk_factors: Lack of vaccination; close contact with an infected person; weakened immune system; age under 12 (unvaccinated)
prevention: Varicella vaccination, typically given in two doses during childhood, is highly effective at preventing infection or reducing severity.

title: How is Obesity treated?
content_text: Q: How is Obesity treated? A: Comprehensive lifestyle intervention including dietary changes, increased physical activity, and behavioral therapy. Pharmacotherapy (e.g., GLP-1 agonists) or bariatric surgery may be considered for severe cases.
symptoms: Excess body fat; breathlessness on exertion; increased sweating; difficulty with physical activity; joint and back pain; low self-esteem
treatments: Comprehensive lifestyle intervention including dietary changes, increased physical activity, and behavioral therapy. Pharmacotherapy (e.g., GLP-1 agonists) or bariatric surgery may be considered for severe cases.
risk_factors: Poor diet; physical inactivity; genetics; certain medications; insufficient sleep; high stress levels; socioeconomic factors
prevention: Balanced nutrition, regular physical activity, adequate sleep, and community/environmental support for healthy choices.

title: What causes or increases the risk of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus?
content_text: Q: What causes or increases the risk of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus? A: Key risk factors include obesity; family history of diabetes; age over 45; physical inactivity; history of gestational diabetes; high blood pressure.
symptoms: Increased thirst; frequent urination; fatigue; blurred vision; slow-healing sores; unexplained weight loss; numbness or tingling in hands or feet
treatments: First-line management includes lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, weight loss) and metformin. Additional agents such as SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 receptor agonists, sulfonylureas, or insulin are added based on glycemic targets and comorbidities.
risk_factors: Obesity; family history of diabetes; age over 45; physical inactivity; history of gestational diabetes; high blood pressure
prevention: Maintaining healthy body weight, regular physical activity (150 minutes/week), balanced diet low in refined sugars, and routine screening for at-risk individuals.

title: Can Osteoarthritis be prevented?
content_text: Q: Can Osteoarthritis be prevented? A: Maintaining healthy weight, regular low-impact exercise, avoiding repetitive joint stress, and proper injury management.
symptoms: Joint pain that worsens with activity; stiffness, especially after rest; loss of flexibility; grating sensation; bone spurs; swelling around the joint
treatments: Weight management, physical therapy, pain relievers (acetaminophen, NSAIDs), and in advanced cases, joint replacement surgery.
risk_factors: Older age; obesity; joint injury; repetitive stress on joints; genetics; female sex
prevention: Maintaining healthy weight, regular low-impact exercise, avoiding repetitive joint stress, and proper injury management.

title: Chronic Kidney Disease - Prevention Guide
content_text: Prevention strategies for Chronic Kidney Disease focus on modifiable risk factors. Controlling blood pressure and blood sugar, avoiding NSAIDs when possible, maintaining healthy weight, and regular kidney function monitoring for at-risk individuals. Individuals with additional risk factors (Diabetes; high blood pressure; family history of kidney disease; age over 60; obesity; smoking; cardiovascular disease) should consult a healthcare provider for personalized guidance.
symptoms: Fatigue; swelling in legs, ankles, or feet; persistent itching; nausea; loss of appetite; muscle cramps; changes in urination frequency
treatments: Management focuses on slowing progression through blood pressure control (ACE inhibitors/ARBs), blood sugar management, dietary modifications (sodium and protein restriction), and treating underlying causes. Advanced stages may require dialysis or transplantation.
risk_factors: Diabetes; high blood pressure; family history of kidney disease; age over 60; obesity; smoking; cardiovascular disease
prevention: Controlling blood pressure and blood sugar, avoiding NSAIDs when possible, maintaining healthy weight, and regular kidney function monitoring for at-risk individuals.

title: What is Hyperlipidemia?
content_text: Q: What is Hyperlipidemia? A: Hyperlipidemia is a condition characterized by abnormally high levels of lipids (cholesterol and/or triglycerides) in the blood, a major risk factor for atherosclerosis, heart disease, and stroke.
symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; in severe cases, fatty deposits around the eyes or joints (xanthomas); occasionally associated with pancreatitis symptoms if triglycerides are very high
treatments: Lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, weight loss) as first-line therapy, with statins as the primary pharmacologic treatment; additional agents (ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors) for high-risk patients.
risk_factors: Diet high in saturated fats; obesity; physical inactivity; smoking; diabetes; family history of high cholesterol; age
prevention: Heart-healthy diet low in saturated and trans fats, regular physical activity, maintaining healthy weight, and routine lipid screening.

title: What are the common symptoms of Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident)?
content_text: Q: What are the common symptoms of Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident)? A: Common symptoms include sudden numbness or weakness of face, arm, or leg, especially on one side; sudden confusion or trouble speaking; sudden vision problems; sudden trouble walking or loss of balance; sudden severe headache.
symptoms: Sudden numbness or weakness of face, arm, or leg, especially on one side; sudden confusion or trouble speaking; sudden vision problems; sudden trouble walking or loss of balance; sudden severe headache
treatments: Acute ischemic stroke may be treated with thrombolytic therapy (tPA) within a narrow time window or mechanical thrombectomy. Long-term management includes antiplatelet/anticoagulant therapy, blood pressure control, and rehabilitation.
risk_factors: High blood pressure; smoking; diabetes; high cholesterol; atrial fibrillation; obesity; family history of stroke; age over 55
prevention: Controlling blood pressure, cholesterol, and blood sugar, smoking cessation, regular exercise, and managing atrial fibrillation when present.

content_text: Q: What are the common symptoms of Psoriasis? A: Common symptoms include red patches of skin covered with thick, silvery scales; dry, cracked skin that may bleed; itching, burning, or soreness; thickened or ridged nails; stiff and swollen joints (in psoriatic arthritis).
symptoms: Red patches of skin covered with thick, silvery scales; dry, cracked skin that may bleed; itching, burning, or soreness; thickened or ridged nails; stiff and swollen joints (in psoriatic arthritis)
treatments: Topical treatments (corticosteroids, vitamin D analogs) for mild cases, phototherapy for moderate cases, and systemic agents or biologics for moderate-to-severe disease.
risk_factors: Family history of psoriasis; smoking; obesity; stress; certain infections; certain medications (e.g., lithium, beta-blockers)
prevention: No definitive prevention exists; managing triggers such as stress, smoking, and certain medications may reduce flare frequency.

title: Chickenpox (Varicella) - Clinical Decision Support Summary
content_text: Clinical decision support summary for Chickenpox (Varicella) (ICD-10: B01). Category: Infectious. Typical severity: Mild to Moderate. Chronicity: typically an acute (short-term) condition. Transmission risk: considered contagious and can spread between people. Primary risk factors: Lack of vaccination; close contact with an infected person; weakened immune system; age under 12 (unvaccinated). Recommended initial management: Supportive care including antihistamines for itching, calamine lotion, and antipyretics. Antiviral medication (acyclovir) may be used for high-risk individuals or severe cases.
symptoms: Itchy rash with fluid-filled blisters; fever; tiredness; loss of appetite; headache; the rash typically appears in waves over several days
treatments: Supportive care including antihistamines for itching, calamine lotion, and antipyretics. Antiviral medication (acyclovir) may be used for high-risk individuals or severe cases.
risk_factors: Lack of vaccination; close contact with an infected person; weakened immune system; age under 12 (unvaccinated)
prevention: Varicella vaccination, typically given in two doses during childhood, is highly effective at preventing infection or reducing severity.

title: Can Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus be prevented?
content_text: Q: Can Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus be prevented? A: Maintaining healthy body weight, regular physical activity (150 minutes/week), balanced diet low in refined sugars, and routine screening for at-risk individuals.
symptoms: Increased thirst; frequent urination; fatigue; blurred vision; slow-healing sores; unexplained weight loss; numbness or tingling in hands or feet
treatments: First-line management includes lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, weight loss) and metformin. Additional agents such as SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 receptor agonists, sulfonylureas, or insulin are added based on glycemic targets and comorbidities.
risk_factors: Obesity; family history of diabetes; age over 45; physical inactivity; history of gestational diabetes; high blood pressure
prevention: Maintaining healthy body weight, regular physical activity (150 minutes/week), balanced diet low in refined sugars, and routine screening for at-risk individuals.

title: Is Coronary Artery Disease contagious?
content_text: Q: Is Coronary Artery Disease contagious? A: Based on current medical guidance, Coronary Artery Disease is not contagious and does not spread between people.
symptoms: Chest pain or discomfort (angina); shortness of breath; fatigue; pain in arms or shoulders; cold sweats; nausea
treatments: Lifestyle changes, statins for cholesterol management, antiplatelet agents (aspirin), beta-blockers, and in severe cases, procedures such as angioplasty with stenting or coronary artery bypass grafting.
risk_factors: High cholesterol; high blood pressure; smoking; diabetes; obesity; family history of heart disease; physical inactivity; age
prevention: Heart-healthy diet, regular exercise, smoking cessation, cholesterol and blood pressure management, and routine cardiovascular screening.

title: How is Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye) treated?
content_text: Q: How is Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye) treated? A: Viral conjunctivitis is managed with supportive care (cool compresses, artificial tears). Bacterial conjunctivitis may require antibiotic eye drops. Allergic conjunctivitis is treated with antihistamine eye drops.
symptoms: Redness in the white of the eye; increased tear production; itchy or burning eyes; discharge (watery, mucous, or pus-like); blurred vision; crusting of eyelids
treatments: Viral conjunctivitis is managed with supportive care (cool compresses, artificial tears). Bacterial conjunctivitis may require antibiotic eye drops. Allergic conjunctivitis is treated with antihistamine eye drops.
risk_factors: Close contact with infected individuals; allergies; contact lens use; exposure to irritants; recent upper respiratory infection
prevention: Frequent handwashing, avoiding touching or rubbing the eyes, not sharing personal items such as towels, and proper contact lens hygiene.

title: What is Migraine?
content_text: Q: What is Migraine? A: Migraine is a neurological condition characterized by recurrent moderate to severe headaches, often accompanied by nausea, vomiting, and sensitivity to light and sound, lasting from a few hours to several days.
symptoms: Throbbing or pulsing headache, often one-sided; nausea and vomiting; sensitivity to light and sound; visual disturbances (aura); dizziness
treatments: Acute treatment includes NSAIDs or triptans for moderate to severe attacks. Preventive therapy (beta-blockers, anticonvulsants, CGRP inhibitors) is considered for frequent migraines.
risk_factors: Family history of migraine; hormonal changes (especially in women); stress; certain foods or food additives; changes in sleep patterns; sensory stimuli
prevention: Identifying and avoiding personal triggers, maintaining regular sleep and meal schedules, stress reduction techniques, and preventive medication when prescribed.

title: Hypothyroidism - Symptom Guide
content_text: Patients with Hypothyroidism commonly present with the following symptoms: Fatigue; weight gain; cold intolerance; dry skin; constipation; hair thinning; depression; muscle weakness; slowed heart rate. Symptom severity and combination vary by individual and disease stage.
symptoms: Fatigue; weight gain; cold intolerance; dry skin; constipation; hair thinning; depression; muscle weakness; slowed heart rate
treatments: Lifelong thyroid hormone replacement therapy (levothyroxine), with regular monitoring of thyroid hormone levels to adjust dosage.
risk_factors: Female sex; age over 60; family history of thyroid disease; autoimmune conditions; previous thyroid surgery or radiation treatment
prevention: No specific prevention exists for autoimmune causes; adequate dietary iodine intake helps prevent iodine-deficiency-related hypothyroidism.

title: What is Influenza?
content_text: Q: What is Influenza? A: Influenza is a contagious respiratory illness caused by influenza viruses that infect the nose, throat, and lungs, ranging from mild to severe illness, and occasionally leading to death, particularly in high-risk groups.
symptoms: Fever or feeling feverish; chills; cough; sore throat; runny or stuffy nose; muscle or body aches; headaches; fatigue
treatments: Supportive care (rest, fluids, antipyretics) for most cases. Antiviral medications such as oseltamivir may be prescribed within 48 hours of symptom onset for high-risk patients.
risk_factors: Age under 5 or over 65; pregnancy; chronic medical conditions (asthma, diabetes, heart disease); weakened immune system; close contact with infected individuals
prevention: Annual influenza vaccination, frequent handwashing, avoiding close contact with sick individuals, and covering coughs and sneezes.

title: Can Epilepsy be prevented?
content_text: Q: Can Epilepsy be prevented? A: Preventing head injuries, managing cardiovascular risk factors to reduce stroke risk, and prompt treatment of infections that can affect the brain.
symptoms: Recurrent seizures (varying from brief staring spells to convulsions); temporary confusion; uncontrollable jerking movements; loss of consciousness; psychic symptoms such as fear or anxiety before a seizure
treatments: Antiepileptic drugs (e.g., levetiracetam, valproate, lamotrigine) are the mainstay of treatment, with surgery, dietary therapy (ketogenic diet), or neurostimulation devices considered for drug-resistant cases.
risk_factors: Family history of epilepsy; brain injury or trauma; stroke; brain infections; developmental disorders; prenatal injury
prevention: Preventing head injuries, managing cardiovascular risk factors to reduce stroke risk, and prompt treatment of infections that can affect the brain.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood or after age 60

title: Is Allergic Rhinitis contagious?
content_text: Q: Is Allergic Rhinitis contagious? A: Based on current medical guidance, Allergic Rhinitis is not contagious and does not spread between people.
symptoms: Sneezing; runny or stuffy nose; itchy or watery eyes; itchy throat; postnasal drip; fatigue
treatments: Antihistamines, intranasal corticosteroids, decongestants for short-term relief, and allergen immunotherapy for long-term management of severe cases.
risk_factors: Family history of allergies; other allergic conditions (asthma, eczema); exposure to environmental allergens; early childhood exposure to tobacco smoke
prevention: Avoiding known allergens, using air purifiers, keeping windows closed during high pollen seasons, and regular cleaning to reduce dust mites.
common_age_group: All ages, often begins in childhood or adolescence

title: Pneumonia - Treatment Guideline
content_text: Clinical management of Pneumonia typically follows a stepwise approach. Bacterial pneumonia is treated with antibiotics; viral pneumonia is managed with supportive care and antivirals when appropriate. Hospitalization may be required for severe cases. Treatment plans should be individualized based on patient comorbidities, preferences, and response to therapy.
symptoms: Cough with phlegm; fever; chills; shortness of breath; chest pain when breathing or coughing; fatigue; confusion (especially in older adults)
treatments: Bacterial pneumonia is treated with antibiotics; viral pneumonia is managed with supportive care and antivirals when appropriate. Hospitalization may be required for severe cases.
risk_factors: Age under 2 or over 65; chronic diseases (asthma, COPD, heart disease); weakened immune system; smoking; recent respiratory infection; hospitalization
prevention: Vaccination (pneumococcal, influenza), good hygiene, avoiding smoking, and managing underlying chronic conditions.

title: How is Peptic Ulcer Disease treated?
content_text: Q: How is Peptic Ulcer Disease treated? A: Antibiotic regimens to eradicate H. pylori when present, proton pump inhibitors to reduce stomach acid, and discontinuation of NSAIDs when possible.
symptoms: Burning stomach pain; bloating; heartburn; nausea; intolerance to fatty foods; in severe cases, dark stools or vomiting blood
treatments: Antibiotic regimens to eradicate H. pylori when present, proton pump inhibitors to reduce stomach acid, and discontinuation of NSAIDs when possible.
risk_factors: H. pylori infection; regular use of NSAIDs; smoking; excessive alcohol consumption; stress (as a contributing factor)
prevention: Limiting NSAID use, avoiding excessive alcohol, not smoking, and prompt treatment of H. pylori infection.

title: How is Osteoarthritis treated?
content_text: Q: How is Osteoarthritis treated? A: Weight management, physical therapy, pain relievers (acetaminophen, NSAIDs), and in advanced cases, joint replacement surgery.
symptoms: Joint pain that worsens with activity; stiffness, especially after rest; loss of flexibility; grating sensation; bone spurs; swelling around the joint
treatments: Weight management, physical therapy, pain relievers (acetaminophen, NSAIDs), and in advanced cases, joint replacement surgery.
risk_factors: Older age; obesity; joint injury; repetitive stress on joints; genetics; female sex
prevention: Maintaining healthy weight, regular low-impact exercise, avoiding repetitive joint stress, and proper injury management.

title: How is Rheumatoid Arthritis treated?
content_text: Q: How is Rheumatoid Arthritis treated? A: Disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs) such as methotrexate, biologic agents, NSAIDs for symptom relief, and physical therapy to maintain joint function.
symptoms: Joint pain and swelling, often symmetrical; morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes; fatigue; low-grade fever; joint deformity in advanced stages
treatments: Disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs) such as methotrexate, biologic agents, NSAIDs for symptom relief, and physical therapy to maintain joint function.
risk_factors: Female sex; family history of autoimmune disease; smoking; obesity; age between 30 and 60
prevention: No definitive prevention exists, but smoking cessation and maintaining healthy weight may reduce risk; early diagnosis and treatment slow disease progression.

title: Essential Hypertension - Clinical Decision Support Summary
content_text: Clinical decision support summary for Essential Hypertension (ICD-10: I10). Category: Cardiovascular. Typical severity: Moderate. Chronicity: a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management. Transmission risk: not considered contagious and does not spread between people. Primary risk factors: High sodium intake; obesity; sedentary lifestyle; excessive alcohol consumption; chronic stress; family history; aging. Recommended initial management: Lifestyle changes (DASH diet, sodium reduction, exercise, weight management) combined with antihypertensive medications such as ACE inhibitors, ARBs, calcium channel blockers, or thiazide diuretics depending on patient profile.
symptoms: Often asymptomatic; occasional headaches; shortness of breath; nosebleeds; dizziness in severe cases
treatments: Lifestyle changes (DASH diet, sodium reduction, exercise, weight management) combined with antihypertensive medications such as ACE inhibitors, ARBs, calcium channel blockers, or thiazide diuretics depending on patient profile.
risk_factors: High sodium intake; obesity; sedentary lifestyle; excessive alcohol consumption; chronic stress; family history; aging
prevention: Reducing dietary sodium, maintaining healthy weight, limiting alcohol, regular aerobic exercise, and managing stress.
source_name: American Heart Association - High Blood Pressure

title: What causes or increases the risk of Obesity?
content_text: Q: What causes or increases the risk of Obesity? A: Key risk factors include poor diet; physical inactivity; genetics; certain medications; insufficient sleep; high stress levels; socioeconomic factors.
symptoms: Excess body fat; breathlessness on exertion; increased sweating; difficulty with physical activity; joint and back pain; low self-esteem
treatments: Comprehensive lifestyle intervention including dietary changes, increased physical activity, and behavioral therapy. Pharmacotherapy (e.g., GLP-1 agonists) or bariatric surgery may be considered for severe cases.
risk_factors: Poor diet; physical inactivity; genetics; certain medications; insufficient sleep; high stress levels; socioeconomic factors
prevention: Balanced nutrition, regular physical activity, adequate sleep, and community/environmental support for healthy choices.

title: Who is most commonly affected by Iron Deficiency Anemia?
content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Iron Deficiency Anemia? A: Iron Deficiency Anemia most commonly affects Women of reproductive age; young children.
symptoms: Fatigue; pale skin; shortness of breath; dizziness; brittle nails; headaches; cold hands and feet; unusual cravings for non-nutritive substances (pica)
treatments: Oral or intravenous iron supplementation, dietary modification to increase iron-rich foods, and treatment of underlying causes such as bleeding sources.
risk_factors: Poor dietary iron intake; heavy menstrual periods; pregnancy; gastrointestinal bleeding; chronic conditions affecting absorption (e.g., celiac disease)
prevention: Eating iron-rich foods (lean meat, beans, fortified cereals), pairing with vitamin C for absorption, and screening in high-risk groups such as pregnant women.
common_age_group: Women of reproductive age; young children

title: What is Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease?
content_text: Q: What is Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease? A: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease is a digestive disorder in which stomach acid frequently flows back into the esophagus, irritating the lining and causing heartburn and other symptoms, potentially leading to complications if untreated.
symptoms: Heartburn; regurgitation of food or sour liquid; chest pain; difficulty swallowing; chronic cough; sensation of a lump in the throat
treatments: Lifestyle modifications (weight loss, smaller meals, avoiding trigger foods) combined with proton pump inhibitors or H2 receptor blockers. Severe or refractory cases may require surgical intervention.
risk_factors: Obesity; hiatal hernia; pregnancy; smoking; certain medications; large meals or eating late at night
prevention: Avoiding large or fatty meals, not lying down immediately after eating, weight management, and limiting alcohol and caffeine intake.

title: Is Urinary Tract Infection a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Urinary Tract Infection a chronic condition? A: Urinary Tract Infection is generally classified as typically an acute (short-term) condition.
symptoms: Strong, persistent urge to urinate; burning sensation during urination; cloudy or strong-smelling urine; pelvic pain in women; frequent, small amounts of urine
treatments: Short course of antibiotics (e.g., nitrofurantoin, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) tailored to local resistance patterns. Increased fluid intake supports recovery.
risk_factors: Female anatomy; sexual activity; certain types of birth control; menopause; urinary tract abnormalities; catheter use; weakened immune system
prevention: Adequate hydration, urinating after intercourse, proper hygiene practices, and avoiding irritating feminine products.
common_age_group: Women of all ages, older adults

title: Who is most commonly affected by Chronic Kidney Disease?
content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Chronic Kidney Disease? A: Chronic Kidney Disease most commonly affects Adults 60+.
symptoms: Fatigue; swelling in legs, ankles, or feet; persistent itching; nausea; loss of appetite; muscle cramps; changes in urination frequency
treatments: Management focuses on slowing progression through blood pressure control (ACE inhibitors/ARBs), blood sugar management, dietary modifications (sodium and protein restriction), and treating underlying causes. Advanced stages may require dialysis or transplantation.
risk_factors: Diabetes; high blood pressure; family history of kidney disease; age over 60; obesity; smoking; cardiovascular disease
prevention: Controlling blood pressure and blood sugar, avoiding NSAIDs when possible, maintaining healthy weight, and regular kidney function monitoring for at-risk individuals.

title: Celiac Disease - Symptom Guide
content_text: Patients with Celiac Disease commonly present with the following symptoms: Diarrhea or constipation; bloating and gas; abdominal pain; fatigue; weight loss; anemia; skin rash (dermatitis herpetiformis); in children, failure to thrive. Symptom severity and combination vary by individual and disease stage.
symptoms: Diarrhea or constipation; bloating and gas; abdominal pain; fatigue; weight loss; anemia; skin rash (dermatitis herpetiformis); in children, failure to thrive
treatments: The only effective treatment is a strict, lifelong gluten-free diet. Nutritional supplementation may be needed to correct deficiencies (iron, vitamin D, B12, folate).
risk_factors: Family history of celiac disease or autoimmune disorders; type 1 diabetes; Down syndrome; autoimmune thyroid disease
prevention: No known prevention; early diagnosis and dietary management prevent complications such as malnutrition and osteoporosis.

title: Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye) - Treatment Guideline
content_text: Clinical management of Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye) typically follows a stepwise approach. Viral conjunctivitis is managed with supportive care (cool compresses, artificial tears). Bacterial conjunctivitis may require antibiotic eye drops. Allergic conjunctivitis is treated with antihistamine eye drops. Treatment plans should be individualized based on patient comorbidities, preferences, and response to therapy.
symptoms: Redness in the white of the eye; increased tear production; itchy or burning eyes; discharge (watery, mucous, or pus-like); blurred vision; crusting of eyelids
treatments: Viral conjunctivitis is managed with supportive care (cool compresses, artificial tears). Bacterial conjunctivitis may require antibiotic eye drops. Allergic conjunctivitis is treated with antihistamine eye drops.
risk_factors: Close contact with infected individuals; allergies; contact lens use; exposure to irritants; recent upper respiratory infection
prevention: Frequent handwashing, avoiding touching or rubbing the eyes, not sharing personal items such as towels, and proper contact lens hygiene.

title: Migraine - Overview
content_text: A neurological condition characterized by recurrent moderate to severe headaches, often accompanied by nausea, vomiting, and sensitivity to light and sound, lasting from a few hours to several days.
symptoms: Throbbing or pulsing headache, often one-sided; nausea and vomiting; sensitivity to light and sound; visual disturbances (aura); dizziness
treatments: Acute treatment includes NSAIDs or triptans for moderate to severe attacks. Preventive therapy (beta-blockers, anticonvulsants, CGRP inhibitors) is considered for frequent migraines.
risk_factors: Family history of migraine; hormonal changes (especially in women); stress; certain foods or food additives; changes in sleep patterns; sensory stimuli
prevention: Identifying and avoiding personal triggers, maintaining regular sleep and meal schedules, stress reduction techniques, and preventive medication when prescribed.

title: What are the common symptoms of Obesity?
content_text: Q: What are the common symptoms of Obesity? A: Common symptoms include excess body fat; breathlessness on exertion; increased sweating; difficulty with physical activity; joint and back pain; low self-esteem.
symptoms: Excess body fat; breathlessness on exertion; increased sweating; difficulty with physical activity; joint and back pain; low self-esteem
treatments: Comprehensive lifestyle intervention including dietary changes, increased physical activity, and behavioral therapy. Pharmacotherapy (e.g., GLP-1 agonists) or bariatric surgery may be considered for severe cases.
risk_factors: Poor diet; physical inactivity; genetics; certain medications; insufficient sleep; high stress levels; socioeconomic factors
prevention: Balanced nutrition, regular physical activity, adequate sleep, and community/environmental support for healthy choices.

title: What are the common symptoms of Malaria?
content_text: Q: What are the common symptoms of Malaria? A: Common symptoms include fever and chills; headache; nausea and vomiting; muscle pain and fatigue; sweating; in severe cases, confusion, seizures, or difficulty breathing.
symptoms: Fever and chills; headache; nausea and vomiting; muscle pain and fatigue; sweating; in severe cases, confusion, seizures, or difficulty breathing
treatments: Artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs) are the standard treatment for uncomplicated malaria. Severe malaria requires hospitalization and intravenous antimalarial drugs.
risk_factors: Living in or traveling to malaria-endemic regions; lack of access to preventive measures (bed nets, prophylaxis); pregnancy; young age
prevention: Use of insecticide-treated bed nets, indoor residual spraying, antimalarial prophylaxis for travelers, and prompt diagnosis and treatment of cases.
common_age_group: Children under 5 in endemic regions; travelers

title: What causes or increases the risk of Asthma?
content_text: Q: What causes or increases the risk of Asthma? A: Key risk factors include family history of asthma or allergies; exposure to tobacco smoke; air pollution; occupational exposure to irritants; respiratory infections in childhood.
symptoms: Wheezing; shortness of breath; chest tightness; coughing, especially at night or early morning; difficulty sleeping due to breathing problems
treatments: Controller medications (inhaled corticosteroids, long-acting beta-agonists) for long-term control, plus short-acting bronchodilators (e.g., albuterol) for acute symptom relief. Avoidance of known triggers is essential.
risk_factors: Family history of asthma or allergies; exposure to tobacco smoke; air pollution; occupational exposure to irritants; respiratory infections in childhood
prevention: Avoiding known allergens and irritants, controlling indoor air quality, getting recommended vaccinations, and adherence to prescribed controller therapy.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood

title: Is Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus contagious?
content_text: Q: Is Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus contagious? A: Based on current medical guidance, Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus is not contagious and does not spread between people.
symptoms: Increased thirst; frequent urination; fatigue; blurred vision; slow-healing sores; unexplained weight loss; numbness or tingling in hands or feet
treatments: First-line management includes lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, weight loss) and metformin. Additional agents such as SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 receptor agonists, sulfonylureas, or insulin are added based on glycemic targets and comorbidities.
risk_factors: Obesity; family history of diabetes; age over 45; physical inactivity; history of gestational diabetes; high blood pressure
prevention: Maintaining healthy body weight, regular physical activity (150 minutes/week), balanced diet low in refined sugars, and routine screening for at-risk individuals.

title: Iron Deficiency Anemia - Treatment Guideline
content_text: Clinical management of Iron Deficiency Anemia typically follows a stepwise approach. Oral or intravenous iron supplementation, dietary modification to increase iron-rich foods, and treatment of underlying causes such as bleeding sources. Treatment plans should be individualized based on patient comorbidities, preferences, and response to therapy.
symptoms: Fatigue; pale skin; shortness of breath; dizziness; brittle nails; headaches; cold hands and feet; unusual cravings for non-nutritive substances (pica)
treatments: Oral or intravenous iron supplementation, dietary modification to increase iron-rich foods, and treatment of underlying causes such as bleeding sources.
risk_factors: Poor dietary iron intake; heavy menstrual periods; pregnancy; gastrointestinal bleeding; chronic conditions affecting absorption (e.g., celiac disease)
prevention: Eating iron-rich foods (lean meat, beans, fortified cereals), pairing with vitamin C for absorption, and screening in high-risk groups such as pregnant women.
common_age_group: Women of reproductive age; young children

title: Malaria - Symptom Guide
content_text: Patients with Malaria commonly present with the following symptoms: Fever and chills; headache; nausea and vomiting; muscle pain and fatigue; sweating; in severe cases, confusion, seizures, or difficulty breathing. Symptom severity and combination vary by individual and disease stage.
symptoms: Fever and chills; headache; nausea and vomiting; muscle pain and fatigue; sweating; in severe cases, confusion, seizures, or difficulty breathing
treatments: Artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs) are the standard treatment for uncomplicated malaria. Severe malaria requires hospitalization and intravenous antimalarial drugs.
risk_factors: Living in or traveling to malaria-endemic regions; lack of access to preventive measures (bed nets, prophylaxis); pregnancy; young age
prevention: Use of insecticide-treated bed nets, indoor residual spraying, antimalarial prophylaxis for travelers, and prompt diagnosis and treatment of cases.

title: Is Tuberculosis a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Tuberculosis a chronic condition? A: Tuberculosis is generally classified as typically an acute (short-term) condition.
symptoms: Persistent cough lasting three weeks or longer, sometimes with blood; chest pain; unintentional weight loss; fatigue; fever; night sweats; loss of appetite
treatments: Standard treatment involves a 6-9 month course of multiple antibiotics (isoniazid, rifampin, ethambutol, pyrazinamide), with directly observed therapy recommended to ensure adherence and prevent drug resistance.
risk_factors: Close contact with an infected person; weakened immune system (e.g., HIV); living in or traveling to areas with high TB rates; healthcare work; substance use
prevention: BCG vaccination in countries with high TB prevalence, prompt identification and treatment of active cases, and infection control measures in healthcare settings.
common_age_group: All ages, higher burden in low-resource settings

title: What are the common symptoms of Celiac Disease?
content_text: Q: What are the common symptoms of Celiac Disease? A: Common symptoms include diarrhea or constipation; bloating and gas; abdominal pain; fatigue; weight loss; anemia; skin rash (dermatitis herpetiformis); in children, failure to thrive.
symptoms: Diarrhea or constipation; bloating and gas; abdominal pain; fatigue; weight loss; anemia; skin rash (dermatitis herpetiformis); in children, failure to thrive
treatments: The only effective treatment is a strict, lifelong gluten-free diet. Nutritional supplementation may be needed to correct deficiencies (iron, vitamin D, B12, folate).
risk_factors: Family history of celiac disease or autoimmune disorders; type 1 diabetes; Down syndrome; autoimmune thyroid disease
prevention: No known prevention; early diagnosis and dietary management prevent complications such as malnutrition and osteoporosis.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood or 20s-30s

title: Can Celiac Disease be prevented?
content_text: Q: Can Celiac Disease be prevented? A: No known prevention; early diagnosis and dietary management prevent complications such as malnutrition and osteoporosis.
symptoms: Diarrhea or constipation; bloating and gas; abdominal pain; fatigue; weight loss; anemia; skin rash (dermatitis herpetiformis); in children, failure to thrive

title: Who is most commonly affected by Hypothyroidism?
content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Hypothyroidism? A: Hypothyroidism most commonly affects Women 60+.
symptoms: Fatigue; weight gain; cold intolerance; dry skin; constipation; hair thinning; depression; muscle weakness; slowed heart rate
treatments: Lifelong thyroid hormone replacement therapy (levothyroxine), with regular monitoring of thyroid hormone levels to adjust dosage.
risk_factors: Female sex; age over 60; family history of thyroid disease; autoimmune conditions; previous thyroid surgery or radiation treatment
prevention: No specific prevention exists for autoimmune causes; adequate dietary iodine intake helps prevent iodine-deficiency-related hypothyroidism.

title: Who is most commonly affected by Coronary Artery Disease?
content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Coronary Artery Disease? A: Coronary Artery Disease most commonly affects Adults 45+.
symptoms: Chest pain or discomfort (angina); shortness of breath; fatigue; pain in arms or shoulders; cold sweats; nausea
treatments: Lifestyle changes, statins for cholesterol management, antiplatelet agents (aspirin), beta-blockers, and in severe cases, procedures such as angioplasty with stenting or coronary artery bypass grafting.
risk_factors: High cholesterol; high blood pressure; smoking; diabetes; obesity; family history of heart disease; physical inactivity; age
prevention: Heart-healthy diet, regular exercise, smoking cessation, cholesterol and blood pressure management, and routine cardiovascular screening.

title: What are the common symptoms of Sleep Apnea?
content_text: Q: What are the common symptoms of Sleep Apnea? A: Common symptoms include loud snoring; episodes of stopped breathing observed by others; gasping for air during sleep; excessive daytime sleepiness; morning headaches; difficulty concentrating; irritability.
symptoms: Loud snoring; episodes of stopped breathing observed by others; gasping for air during sleep; excessive daytime sleepiness; morning headaches; difficulty concentrating; irritability
treatments: Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) therapy is the standard treatment. Weight loss, positional therapy, oral appliances, and in select cases, surgery, may also be used.
risk_factors: Obesity; older age; male sex; large neck circumference; family history; smoking; nasal congestion; use of alcohol or sedatives
prevention: Maintaining healthy weight, avoiding alcohol and sedatives before sleep, sleeping on one's side, and treating nasal congestion.

title: Can Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease be prevented?
content_text: Q: Can Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease be prevented? A: Avoiding large or fatty meals, not lying down immediately after eating, weight management, and limiting alcohol and caffeine intake.
symptoms: Heartburn; regurgitation of food or sour liquid; chest pain; difficulty swallowing; chronic cough; sensation of a lump in the throat
treatments: Lifestyle modifications (weight loss, smaller meals, avoiding trigger foods) combined with proton pump inhibitors or H2 receptor blockers. Severe or refractory cases may require surgical intervention.
risk_factors: Obesity; hiatal hernia; pregnancy; smoking; certain medications; large meals or eating late at night
prevention: Avoiding large or fatty meals, not lying down immediately after eating, weight management, and limiting alcohol and caffeine intake.

title: Can Sleep Apnea be prevented?
content_text: Q: Can Sleep Apnea be prevented? A: Maintaining healthy weight, avoiding alcohol and sedatives before sleep, sleeping on one's side, and treating nasal congestion.

title: Who is most commonly affected by Major Depressive Disorder?
content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Major Depressive Disorder? A: Major Depressive Disorder most commonly affects Adults 18-44.
symptoms: Persistent sad or empty mood; loss of interest in hobbies; fatigue or low energy; difficulty concentrating; changes in appetite or weight; sleep disturbances; thoughts of death or suicide
treatments: Psychotherapy (cognitive behavioral therapy, interpersonal therapy) and/or antidepressant medications (SSRIs, SNRIs). Severe cases may require combination therapy or, rarely, electroconvulsive therapy.
risk_factors: Family history of depression; history of trauma or abuse; chronic illness; substance use; major life changes or stress; certain medications
prevention: Stress management, strong social support networks, regular physical activity, adequate sleep, and early intervention for at-risk individuals.
