title: Major Depressive Disorder - Overview
content_text: A common but serious mood disorder characterized by persistent feelings of sadness, hopelessness, and loss of interest or pleasure in activities, significantly affecting daily functioning for at least two weeks.
symptoms: Persistent sad or empty mood; loss of interest in hobbies; fatigue or low energy; difficulty concentrating; changes in appetite or weight; sleep disturbances; thoughts of death or suicide
treatments: Psychotherapy (cognitive behavioral therapy, interpersonal therapy) and/or antidepressant medications (SSRIs, SNRIs). Severe cases may require combination therapy or, rarely, electroconvulsive therapy.
risk_factors: Family history of depression; history of trauma or abuse; chronic illness; substance use; major life changes or stress; certain medications
prevention: Stress management, strong social support networks, regular physical activity, adequate sleep, and early intervention for at-risk individuals.

title: Allergic Rhinitis - Treatment Guideline
content_text: Clinical management of Allergic Rhinitis typically follows a stepwise approach. Antihistamines, intranasal corticosteroids, decongestants for short-term relief, and allergen immunotherapy for long-term management of severe cases. Treatment plans should be individualized based on patient comorbidities, preferences, and response to therapy.
symptoms: Sneezing; runny or stuffy nose; itchy or watery eyes; itchy throat; postnasal drip; fatigue
treatments: Antihistamines, intranasal corticosteroids, decongestants for short-term relief, and allergen immunotherapy for long-term management of severe cases.
risk_factors: Family history of allergies; other allergic conditions (asthma, eczema); exposure to environmental allergens; early childhood exposure to tobacco smoke
prevention: Avoiding known allergens, using air purifiers, keeping windows closed during high pollen seasons, and regular cleaning to reduce dust mites.
common_age_group: All ages, often begins in childhood or adolescence

title: Celiac Disease - Clinical Decision Support Summary
content_text: Clinical decision support summary for Celiac Disease (ICD-10: K90.0). Category: Gastrointestinal. Typical severity: Mild to Moderate. Chronicity: a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management. Transmission risk: not considered contagious and does not spread between people. Primary risk factors: Family history of celiac disease or autoimmune disorders; type 1 diabetes; Down syndrome; autoimmune thyroid disease. Recommended initial management: The only effective treatment is a strict, lifelong gluten-free diet. Nutritional supplementation may be needed to correct deficiencies (iron, vitamin D, B12, folate).
symptoms: Diarrhea or constipation; bloating and gas; abdominal pain; fatigue; weight loss; anemia; skin rash (dermatitis herpetiformis); in children, failure to thrive
treatments: The only effective treatment is a strict, lifelong gluten-free diet. Nutritional supplementation may be needed to correct deficiencies (iron, vitamin D, B12, folate).
risk_factors: Family history of celiac disease or autoimmune disorders; type 1 diabetes; Down syndrome; autoimmune thyroid disease
prevention: No known prevention; early diagnosis and dietary management prevent complications such as malnutrition and osteoporosis.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood or 20s-30s

title: Epilepsy - Prevention Guide
content_text: Prevention strategies for Epilepsy focus on modifiable risk factors. Preventing head injuries, managing cardiovascular risk factors to reduce stroke risk, and prompt treatment of infections that can affect the brain. Individuals with additional risk factors (Family history of epilepsy; brain injury or trauma; stroke; brain infections; developmental disorders; prenatal injury) should consult a healthcare provider for personalized guidance.
symptoms: Recurrent seizures (varying from brief staring spells to convulsions); temporary confusion; uncontrollable jerking movements; loss of consciousness; psychic symptoms such as fear or anxiety before a seizure
treatments: Antiepileptic drugs (e.g., levetiracetam, valproate, lamotrigine) are the mainstay of treatment, with surgery, dietary therapy (ketogenic diet), or neurostimulation devices considered for drug-resistant cases.
risk_factors: Family history of epilepsy; brain injury or trauma; stroke; brain infections; developmental disorders; prenatal injury
prevention: Preventing head injuries, managing cardiovascular risk factors to reduce stroke risk, and prompt treatment of infections that can affect the brain.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood or after age 60

title: How is Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident) treated?
content_text: Q: How is Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident) treated? A: Acute ischemic stroke may be treated with thrombolytic therapy (tPA) within a narrow time window or mechanical thrombectomy. Long-term management includes antiplatelet/anticoagulant therapy, blood pressure control, and rehabilitation.
symptoms: Sudden numbness or weakness of face, arm, or leg, especially on one side; sudden confusion or trouble speaking; sudden vision problems; sudden trouble walking or loss of balance; sudden severe headache
treatments: Acute ischemic stroke may be treated with thrombolytic therapy (tPA) within a narrow time window or mechanical thrombectomy. Long-term management includes antiplatelet/anticoagulant therapy, blood pressure control, and rehabilitation.
risk_factors: High blood pressure; smoking; diabetes; high cholesterol; atrial fibrillation; obesity; family history of stroke; age over 55
prevention: Controlling blood pressure, cholesterol, and blood sugar, smoking cessation, regular exercise, and managing atrial fibrillation when present.

title: Is Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident) contagious?
content_text: Q: Is Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident) contagious? A: Based on current medical guidance, Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident) is not contagious and does not spread between people.
symptoms: Sudden numbness or weakness of face, arm, or leg, especially on one side; sudden confusion or trouble speaking; sudden vision problems; sudden trouble walking or loss of balance; sudden severe headache
treatments: Acute ischemic stroke may be treated with thrombolytic therapy (tPA) within a narrow time window or mechanical thrombectomy. Long-term management includes antiplatelet/anticoagulant therapy, blood pressure control, and rehabilitation.
risk_factors: High blood pressure; smoking; diabetes; high cholesterol; atrial fibrillation; obesity; family history of stroke; age over 55
prevention: Controlling blood pressure, cholesterol, and blood sugar, smoking cessation, regular exercise, and managing atrial fibrillation when present.

title: Psoriasis - Clinical Decision Support Summary
content_text: Clinical decision support summary for Psoriasis (ICD-10: L40). Category: Dermatologic. Typical severity: Mild to Severe. Chronicity: a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management. Transmission risk: not considered contagious and does not spread between people. Primary risk factors: Family history of psoriasis; smoking; obesity; stress; certain infections; certain medications (e.g., lithium, beta-blockers). Recommended initial management: Topical treatments (corticosteroids, vitamin D analogs) for mild cases, phototherapy for moderate cases, and systemic agents or biologics for moderate-to-severe disease.
symptoms: Red patches of skin covered with thick, silvery scales; dry, cracked skin that may bleed; itching, burning, or soreness; thickened or ridged nails; stiff and swollen joints (in psoriatic arthritis)
treatments: Topical treatments (corticosteroids, vitamin D analogs) for mild cases, phototherapy for moderate cases, and systemic agents or biologics for moderate-to-severe disease.
risk_factors: Family history of psoriasis; smoking; obesity; stress; certain infections; certain medications (e.g., lithium, beta-blockers)
prevention: No definitive prevention exists; managing triggers such as stress, smoking, and certain medications may reduce flare frequency.

title: Is Hypothyroidism a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Hypothyroidism a chronic condition? A: Hypothyroidism is generally classified as a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management.
symptoms: Fatigue; weight gain; cold intolerance; dry skin; constipation; hair thinning; depression; muscle weakness; slowed heart rate
treatments: Lifelong thyroid hormone replacement therapy (levothyroxine), with regular monitoring of thyroid hormone levels to adjust dosage.
risk_factors: Female sex; age over 60; family history of thyroid disease; autoimmune conditions; previous thyroid surgery or radiation treatment
prevention: No specific prevention exists for autoimmune causes; adequate dietary iodine intake helps prevent iodine-deficiency-related hypothyroidism.

title: Essential Hypertension - Treatment Guideline
content_text: Clinical management of Essential Hypertension typically follows a stepwise approach. Lifestyle changes (DASH diet, sodium reduction, exercise, weight management) combined with antihypertensive medications such as ACE inhibitors, ARBs, calcium channel blockers, or thiazide diuretics depending on patient profile. Treatment plans should be individualized based on patient comorbidities, preferences, and response to therapy.
symptoms: Often asymptomatic; occasional headaches; shortness of breath; nosebleeds; dizziness in severe cases
treatments: Lifestyle changes (DASH diet, sodium reduction, exercise, weight management) combined with antihypertensive medications such as ACE inhibitors, ARBs, calcium channel blockers, or thiazide diuretics depending on patient profile.
risk_factors: High sodium intake; obesity; sedentary lifestyle; excessive alcohol consumption; chronic stress; family history; aging
prevention: Reducing dietary sodium, maintaining healthy weight, limiting alcohol, regular aerobic exercise, and managing stress.
source_name: American Heart Association - High Blood Pressure

title: Is Chronic Kidney Disease contagious?
content_text: Q: Is Chronic Kidney Disease contagious? A: Based on current medical guidance, Chronic Kidney Disease is not contagious and does not spread between people.
symptoms: Fatigue; swelling in legs, ankles, or feet; persistent itching; nausea; loss of appetite; muscle cramps; changes in urination frequency
treatments: Management focuses on slowing progression through blood pressure control (ACE inhibitors/ARBs), blood sugar management, dietary modifications (sodium and protein restriction), and treating underlying causes. Advanced stages may require dialysis or transplantation.
risk_factors: Diabetes; high blood pressure; family history of kidney disease; age over 60; obesity; smoking; cardiovascular disease
prevention: Controlling blood pressure and blood sugar, avoiding NSAIDs when possible, maintaining healthy weight, and regular kidney function monitoring for at-risk individuals.

title: Sleep Apnea - Prevention Guide
content_text: Prevention strategies for Sleep Apnea focus on modifiable risk factors. Maintaining healthy weight, avoiding alcohol and sedatives before sleep, sleeping on one's side, and treating nasal congestion. Individuals with additional risk factors (Obesity; older age; male sex; large neck circumference; family history; smoking; nasal congestion; use of alcohol or sedatives) should consult a healthcare provider for personalized guidance.
symptoms: Loud snoring; episodes of stopped breathing observed by others; gasping for air during sleep; excessive daytime sleepiness; morning headaches; difficulty concentrating; irritability
treatments: Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) therapy is the standard treatment. Weight loss, positional therapy, oral appliances, and in select cases, surgery, may also be used.
risk_factors: Obesity; older age; male sex; large neck circumference; family history; smoking; nasal congestion; use of alcohol or sedatives
prevention: Maintaining healthy weight, avoiding alcohol and sedatives before sleep, sleeping on one's side, and treating nasal congestion.

title: Who is most commonly affected by Osteoarthritis?
content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Osteoarthritis? A: Osteoarthritis most commonly affects Adults 50+.
symptoms: Joint pain that worsens with activity; stiffness, especially after rest; loss of flexibility; grating sensation; bone spurs; swelling around the joint
treatments: Weight management, physical therapy, pain relievers (acetaminophen, NSAIDs), and in advanced cases, joint replacement surgery.
risk_factors: Older age; obesity; joint injury; repetitive stress on joints; genetics; female sex
prevention: Maintaining healthy weight, regular low-impact exercise, avoiding repetitive joint stress, and proper injury management.

title: Is Essential Hypertension a chronic condition?

treatments: Lifestyle changes (DASH diet, sodium reduction, exercise, weight management) combined with antihypertensive medications such as ACE inhibitors, ARBs, calcium channel blockers, or thiazide diuretics depending on patient profile.

prevention: Reducing dietary sodium, maintaining healthy weight, limiting alcohol, regular aerobic exercise, and managing stress.

title: Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis) - Treatment Guideline
content_text: Clinical management of Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis) typically follows a stepwise approach. Regular moisturizing, topical corticosteroids for flare-ups, antihistamines for itching, and identification/avoidance of triggers. Severe cases may require systemic immunomodulators. Treatment plans should be individualized based on patient comorbidities, preferences, and response to therapy.
symptoms: Dry, scaly skin; intense itching; red to brownish-gray patches; small raised bumps that may leak fluid when scratched; thickened, cracked skin
treatments: Regular moisturizing, topical corticosteroids for flare-ups, antihistamines for itching, and identification/avoidance of triggers. Severe cases may require systemic immunomodulators.
risk_factors: Family history of eczema, asthma, or hay fever; dry skin; environmental irritants; stress; certain climates
prevention: Regular moisturizing, avoiding harsh soaps and known irritants, using lukewarm water for bathing, and wearing soft, breathable fabrics.
common_age_group: Children, with many cases persisting into adulthood

title: Generalized Anxiety Disorder - Overview
content_text: A mental health condition characterized by persistent and excessive worry about various aspects of life that is difficult to control and interferes with daily functioning, lasting at least six months.
symptoms: Excessive worry; restlessness; fatigue; difficulty concentrating; irritability; muscle tension; sleep disturbances
treatments: Cognitive behavioral therapy is highly effective; medications such as SSRIs, SNRIs, or buspirone may be used. Relaxation techniques and lifestyle changes support management.
risk_factors: Family history of anxiety disorders; history of trauma; chronic health conditions; personality traits; substance use
prevention: Stress management techniques, regular physical activity, limiting caffeine and alcohol, and adequate sleep hygiene.

title: Who is most commonly affected by Rheumatoid Arthritis?
content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Rheumatoid Arthritis? A: Rheumatoid Arthritis most commonly affects Adults 30-60.
symptoms: Joint pain and swelling, often symmetrical; morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes; fatigue; low-grade fever; joint deformity in advanced stages
treatments: Disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs) such as methotrexate, biologic agents, NSAIDs for symptom relief, and physical therapy to maintain joint function.
risk_factors: Female sex; family history of autoimmune disease; smoking; obesity; age between 30 and 60
prevention: No definitive prevention exists, but smoking cessation and maintaining healthy weight may reduce risk; early diagnosis and treatment slow disease progression.

title: Is Epilepsy a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Epilepsy a chronic condition? A: Epilepsy is generally classified as a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management. Q: Is Epilepsy a chronic condition? A: Epilepsy is generally classified as a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management.
symptoms: Recurrent seizures (varying from brief staring spells to convulsions); temporary confusion; uncontrollable jerking movements; loss of consciousness; psychic symptoms such as fear or anxiety before a seizure
treatments: Antiepileptic drugs (e.g., levetiracetam, valproate, lamotrigine) are the mainstay of treatment, with surgery, dietary therapy (ketogenic diet), or neurostimulation devices considered for drug-resistant cases.
risk_factors: Family history of epilepsy; brain injury or trauma; stroke; brain infections; developmental disorders; prenatal injury
prevention: Preventing head injuries, managing cardiovascular risk factors to reduce stroke risk, and prompt treatment of infections that can affect the brain.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood or after age 60

title: Who is most commonly affected by Migraine?
content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Migraine? A: Migraine most commonly affects Adults 18-55, more common in women.
symptoms: Throbbing or pulsing headache, often one-sided; nausea and vomiting; sensitivity to light and sound; visual disturbances (aura); dizziness
treatments: Acute treatment includes NSAIDs or triptans for moderate to severe attacks. Preventive therapy (beta-blockers, anticonvulsants, CGRP inhibitors) is considered for frequent migraines.
risk_factors: Family history of migraine; hormonal changes (especially in women); stress; certain foods or food additives; changes in sleep patterns; sensory stimuli
prevention: Identifying and avoiding personal triggers, maintaining regular sleep and meal schedules, stress reduction techniques, and preventive medication when prescribed.

title: Is Hyperlipidemia a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Hyperlipidemia a chronic condition? A: Hyperlipidemia is generally classified as a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management.
symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; in severe cases, fatty deposits around the eyes or joints (xanthomas); occasionally associated with pancreatitis symptoms if triglycerides are very high
treatments: Lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, weight loss) as first-line therapy, with statins as the primary pharmacologic treatment; additional agents (ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors) for high-risk patients.
risk_factors: Diet high in saturated fats; obesity; physical inactivity; smoking; diabetes; family history of high cholesterol; age
prevention: Heart-healthy diet low in saturated and trans fats, regular physical activity, maintaining healthy weight, and routine lipid screening.

title: What are the common symptoms of Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease?
content_text: Q: What are the common symptoms of Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease? A: Common symptoms include heartburn; regurgitation of food or sour liquid; chest pain; difficulty swallowing; chronic cough; sensation of a lump in the throat.
symptoms: Heartburn; regurgitation of food or sour liquid; chest pain; difficulty swallowing; chronic cough; sensation of a lump in the throat
treatments: Lifestyle modifications (weight loss, smaller meals, avoiding trigger foods) combined with proton pump inhibitors or H2 receptor blockers. Severe or refractory cases may require surgical intervention.
risk_factors: Obesity; hiatal hernia; pregnancy; smoking; certain medications; large meals or eating late at night
prevention: Avoiding large or fatty meals, not lying down immediately after eating, weight management, and limiting alcohol and caffeine intake.

title: What causes or increases the risk of Essential Hypertension?
content_text: Q: What causes or increases the risk of Essential Hypertension? A: Key risk factors include high sodium intake; obesity; sedentary lifestyle; excessive alcohol consumption; chronic stress; family history; aging.
symptoms: Often asymptomatic; occasional headaches; shortness of breath; nosebleeds; dizziness in severe cases
treatments: Lifestyle changes (DASH diet, sodium reduction, exercise, weight management) combined with antihypertensive medications such as ACE inhibitors, ARBs, calcium channel blockers, or thiazide diuretics depending on patient profile.
risk_factors: High sodium intake; obesity; sedentary lifestyle; excessive alcohol consumption; chronic stress; family history; aging
prevention: Reducing dietary sodium, maintaining healthy weight, limiting alcohol, regular aerobic exercise, and managing stress.
source_name: American Heart Association - High Blood Pressure

title: Can Malaria be prevented?
content_text: Q: Can Malaria be prevented? A: Use of insecticide-treated bed nets, indoor residual spraying, antimalarial prophylaxis for travelers, and prompt diagnosis and treatment of cases.
symptoms: Fever and chills; headache; nausea and vomiting; muscle pain and fatigue; sweating; in severe cases, confusion, seizures, or difficulty breathing
treatments: Artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs) are the standard treatment for uncomplicated malaria. Severe malaria requires hospitalization and intravenous antimalarial drugs.
risk_factors: Living in or traveling to malaria-endemic regions; lack of access to preventive measures (bed nets, prophylaxis); pregnancy; young age
prevention: Use of insecticide-treated bed nets, indoor residual spraying, antimalarial prophylaxis for travelers, and prompt diagnosis and treatment of cases.
common_age_group: Children under 5 in endemic regions; travelers

title: Is Osteoarthritis a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Osteoarthritis a chronic condition? A: Osteoarthritis is generally classified as a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management.
symptoms: Joint pain that worsens with activity; stiffness, especially after rest; loss of flexibility; grating sensation; bone spurs; swelling around the joint
treatments: Weight management, physical therapy, pain relievers (acetaminophen, NSAIDs), and in advanced cases, joint replacement surgery.
risk_factors: Older age; obesity; joint injury; repetitive stress on joints; genetics; female sex
prevention: Maintaining healthy weight, regular low-impact exercise, avoiding repetitive joint stress, and proper injury management.

title: Chronic Kidney Disease - Treatment Guideline
content_text: Clinical management of Chronic Kidney Disease typically follows a stepwise approach. Management focuses on slowing progression through blood pressure control (ACE inhibitors/ARBs), blood sugar management, dietary modifications (sodium and protein restriction), and treating underlying causes. Advanced stages may require dialysis or transplantation. Treatment plans should be individualized based on patient comorbidities, preferences, and response to therapy.
symptoms: Fatigue; swelling in legs, ankles, or feet; persistent itching; nausea; loss of appetite; muscle cramps; changes in urination frequency
treatments: Management focuses on slowing progression through blood pressure control (ACE inhibitors/ARBs), blood sugar management, dietary modifications (sodium and protein restriction), and treating underlying causes. Advanced stages may require dialysis or transplantation.
risk_factors: Diabetes; high blood pressure; family history of kidney disease; age over 60; obesity; smoking; cardiovascular disease
prevention: Controlling blood pressure and blood sugar, avoiding NSAIDs when possible, maintaining healthy weight, and regular kidney function monitoring for at-risk individuals.

title: What causes or increases the risk of Influenza?
content_text: Q: What causes or increases the risk of Influenza? A: Key risk factors include age under 5 or over 65; pregnancy; chronic medical conditions (asthma, diabetes, heart disease); weakened immune system; close contact with infected individuals.
symptoms: Fever or feeling feverish; chills; cough; sore throat; runny or stuffy nose; muscle or body aches; headaches; fatigue
treatments: Supportive care (rest, fluids, antipyretics) for most cases. Antiviral medications such as oseltamivir may be prescribed within 48 hours of symptom onset for high-risk patients.
risk_factors: Age under 5 or over 65; pregnancy; chronic medical conditions (asthma, diabetes, heart disease); weakened immune system; close contact with infected individuals
prevention: Annual influenza vaccination, frequent handwashing, avoiding close contact with sick individuals, and covering coughs and sneezes.

title: How is Psoriasis treated?
content_text: Q: How is Psoriasis treated? A: Topical treatments (corticosteroids, vitamin D analogs) for mild cases, phototherapy for moderate cases, and systemic agents or biologics for moderate-to-severe disease.
symptoms: Red patches of skin covered with thick, silvery scales; dry, cracked skin that may bleed; itching, burning, or soreness; thickened or ridged nails; stiff and swollen joints (in psoriatic arthritis)
treatments: Topical treatments (corticosteroids, vitamin D analogs) for mild cases, phototherapy for moderate cases, and systemic agents or biologics for moderate-to-severe disease.
risk_factors: Family history of psoriasis; smoking; obesity; stress; certain infections; certain medications (e.g., lithium, beta-blockers)
prevention: No definitive prevention exists; managing triggers such as stress, smoking, and certain medications may reduce flare frequency.

title: What is Iron Deficiency Anemia?
content_text: Q: What is Iron Deficiency Anemia? A: Iron Deficiency Anemia is a condition resulting from insufficient iron to produce adequate hemoglobin, leading to reduced oxygen-carrying capacity of the blood and a range of systemic symptoms.
symptoms: Fatigue; pale skin; shortness of breath; dizziness; brittle nails; headaches; cold hands and feet; unusual cravings for non-nutritive substances (pica)
treatments: Oral or intravenous iron supplementation, dietary modification to increase iron-rich foods, and treatment of underlying causes such as bleeding sources.
risk_factors: Poor dietary iron intake; heavy menstrual periods; pregnancy; gastrointestinal bleeding; chronic conditions affecting absorption (e.g., celiac disease)
prevention: Eating iron-rich foods (lean meat, beans, fortified cereals), pairing with vitamin C for absorption, and screening in high-risk groups such as pregnant women.
common_age_group: Women of reproductive age; young children

title: Allergic Rhinitis - Prevention Guide
content_text: Prevention strategies for Allergic Rhinitis focus on modifiable risk factors. Avoiding known allergens, using air purifiers, keeping windows closed during high pollen seasons, and regular cleaning to reduce dust mites. Individuals with additional risk factors (Family history of allergies; other allergic conditions (asthma, eczema); exposure to environmental allergens; early childhood exposure to tobacco smoke) should consult a healthcare provider for personalized guidance.
symptoms: Sneezing; runny or stuffy nose; itchy or watery eyes; itchy throat; postnasal drip; fatigue
treatments: Antihistamines, intranasal corticosteroids, decongestants for short-term relief, and allergen immunotherapy for long-term management of severe cases.
risk_factors: Family history of allergies; other allergic conditions (asthma, eczema); exposure to environmental allergens; early childhood exposure to tobacco smoke
prevention: Avoiding known allergens, using air purifiers, keeping windows closed during high pollen seasons, and regular cleaning to reduce dust mites.
common_age_group: All ages, often begins in childhood or adolescence

title: How is Rheumatoid Arthritis treated? (Archived Copy)
content_text: Q: How is Rheumatoid Arthritis treated? A: Disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs) such as methotrexate, biologic agents, NSAIDs for symptom relief, and physical therapy to maintain joint function.
symptoms: Joint pain and swelling, often symmetrical; morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes; fatigue; low-grade fever; joint deformity in advanced stages
treatments: Disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs) such as methotrexate, biologic agents, NSAIDs for symptom relief, and physical therapy to maintain joint function.
risk_factors: Female sex; family history of autoimmune disease; smoking; obesity; age between 30 and 60
prevention: No definitive prevention exists, but smoking cessation and maintaining healthy weight may reduce risk; early diagnosis and treatment slow disease progression.

content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Hyperlipidemia? A: Hyperlipidemia most commonly affects Adults 40+.
symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; in severe cases, fatty deposits around the eyes or joints (xanthomas); occasionally associated with pancreatitis symptoms if triglycerides are very high
treatments: Lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, weight loss) as first-line therapy, with statins as the primary pharmacologic treatment; additional agents (ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors) for high-risk patients.

title: Generalized Anxiety Disorder - Symptom Guide
content_text: Patients with Generalized Anxiety Disorder commonly present with the following symptoms: Excessive worry; restlessness; fatigue; difficulty concentrating; irritability; muscle tension; sleep disturbances. Symptom severity and combination vary by individual and disease stage.
symptoms: Excessive worry; restlessness; fatigue; difficulty concentrating; irritability; muscle tension; sleep disturbances
treatments: Cognitive behavioral therapy is highly effective; medications such as SSRIs, SNRIs, or buspirone may be used. Relaxation techniques and lifestyle changes support management.
risk_factors: Family history of anxiety disorders; history of trauma; chronic health conditions; personality traits; substance use
prevention: Stress management techniques, regular physical activity, limiting caffeine and alcohol, and adequate sleep hygiene.

title: Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident) - Symptom Guide
content_text: Patients with Stroke (Cerebrovascular Accident) commonly present with the following symptoms: Sudden numbness or weakness of face, arm, or leg, especially on one side; sudden confusion or trouble speaking; sudden vision problems; sudden trouble walking or loss of balance; sudden severe headache. Symptom severity and combination vary by individual and disease stage.
symptoms: Sudden numbness or weakness of face, arm, or leg, especially on one side; sudden confusion or trouble speaking; sudden vision problems; sudden trouble walking or loss of balance; sudden severe headache
treatments: Acute ischemic stroke may be treated with thrombolytic therapy (tPA) within a narrow time window or mechanical thrombectomy. Long-term management includes antiplatelet/anticoagulant therapy, blood pressure control, and rehabilitation.
risk_factors: High blood pressure; smoking; diabetes; high cholesterol; atrial fibrillation; obesity; family history of stroke; age over 55
prevention: Controlling blood pressure, cholesterol, and blood sugar, smoking cessation, regular exercise, and managing atrial fibrillation when present.

title: Can Major Depressive Disorder be prevented?
content_text: Q: Can Major Depressive Disorder be prevented? A: Stress management, strong social support networks, regular physical activity, adequate sleep, and early intervention for at-risk individuals.
symptoms: Persistent sad or empty mood; loss of interest in hobbies; fatigue or low energy; difficulty concentrating; changes in appetite or weight; sleep disturbances; thoughts of death or suicide
treatments: Psychotherapy (cognitive behavioral therapy, interpersonal therapy) and/or antidepressant medications (SSRIs, SNRIs). Severe cases may require combination therapy or, rarely, electroconvulsive therapy.
risk_factors: Family history of depression; history of trauma or abuse; chronic illness; substance use; major life changes or stress; certain medications
prevention: Stress management, strong social support networks, regular physical activity, adequate sleep, and early intervention for at-risk individuals.

title: Pneumonia - Overview
content_text: An infection that inflames the air sacs in one or both lungs, which may fill with fluid or pus, causing cough, fever, chills, and difficulty breathing; can be caused by bacteria, viruses, or fungi.
symptoms: Cough with phlegm; fever; chills; shortness of breath; chest pain when breathing or coughing; fatigue; confusion (especially in older adults)
treatments: Bacterial pneumonia is treated with antibiotics; viral pneumonia is managed with supportive care and antivirals when appropriate. Hospitalization may be required for severe cases.
risk_factors: Age under 2 or over 65; chronic diseases (asthma, COPD, heart disease); weakened immune system; smoking; recent respiratory infection; hospitalization
prevention: Vaccination (pneumococcal, influenza), good hygiene, avoiding smoking, and managing underlying chronic conditions.

title: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease - Clinical Decision Support Summary
content_text: Clinical decision support summary for Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (ICD-10: J44). Category: Respiratory. Typical severity: Moderate to Severe. Chronicity: a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management. Transmission risk: not considered contagious and does not spread between people. Primary risk factors: Smoking (primary cause); long-term exposure to air pollutants or occupational dust/chemicals; genetic factors (alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency); history of childhood respiratory infections. Recommended initial management: Bronchodilators (short and long-acting), inhaled corticosteroids for frequent exacerbations, pulmonary rehabilitation, oxygen therapy in advanced stages, and smoking cessation as the most critical intervention.
symptoms: Chronic cough with mucus production; shortness of breath, especially during physical activity; wheezing; chest tightness; frequent respiratory infections
treatments: Bronchodilators (short and long-acting), inhaled corticosteroids for frequent exacerbations, pulmonary rehabilitation, oxygen therapy in advanced stages, and smoking cessation as the most critical intervention.
risk_factors: Smoking (primary cause); long-term exposure to air pollutants or occupational dust/chemicals; genetic factors (alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency); history of childhood respiratory infections
prevention: Avoiding smoking and secondhand smoke, reducing occupational and environmental exposure to lung irritants, and vaccination against respiratory infections.

title: Can Obesity be prevented?
content_text: Q: Can Obesity be prevented? A: Balanced nutrition, regular physical activity, adequate sleep, and community/environmental support for healthy choices.
symptoms: Excess body fat; breathlessness on exertion; increased sweating; difficulty with physical activity; joint and back pain; low self-esteem
treatments: Comprehensive lifestyle intervention including dietary changes, increased physical activity, and behavioral therapy. Pharmacotherapy (e.g., GLP-1 agonists) or bariatric surgery may be considered for severe cases.
risk_factors: Poor diet; physical inactivity; genetics; certain medications; insufficient sleep; high stress levels; socioeconomic factors
prevention: Balanced nutrition, regular physical activity, adequate sleep, and community/environmental support for healthy choices.

title: Is Coronary Artery Disease a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Coronary Artery Disease a chronic condition? A: Coronary Artery Disease is generally classified as a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management.
symptoms: Chest pain or discomfort (angina); shortness of breath; fatigue; pain in arms or shoulders; cold sweats; nausea
treatments: Lifestyle changes, statins for cholesterol management, antiplatelet agents (aspirin), beta-blockers, and in severe cases, procedures such as angioplasty with stenting or coronary artery bypass grafting.
risk_factors: High cholesterol; high blood pressure; smoking; diabetes; obesity; family history of heart disease; physical inactivity; age
prevention: Heart-healthy diet, regular exercise, smoking cessation, cholesterol and blood pressure management, and routine cardiovascular screening.

title: Is Sleep Apnea a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Sleep Apnea a chronic condition? A: Sleep Apnea is generally classified as a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management.
symptoms: Loud snoring; episodes of stopped breathing observed by others; gasping for air during sleep; excessive daytime sleepiness; morning headaches; difficulty concentrating; irritability
treatments: Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) therapy is the standard treatment. Weight loss, positional therapy, oral appliances, and in select cases, surgery, may also be used.
risk_factors: Obesity; older age; male sex; large neck circumference; family history; smoking; nasal congestion; use of alcohol or sedatives
prevention: Maintaining healthy weight, avoiding alcohol and sedatives before sleep, sleeping on one's side, and treating nasal congestion.

title: Who is most commonly affected by Celiac Disease?
content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Celiac Disease? A: Celiac Disease most commonly affects All ages, often diagnosed in childhood or 20s-30s.
symptoms: Diarrhea or constipation; bloating and gas; abdominal pain; fatigue; weight loss; anemia; skin rash (dermatitis herpetiformis); in children, failure to thrive
treatments: The only effective treatment is a strict, lifelong gluten-free diet. Nutritional supplementation may be needed to correct deficiencies (iron, vitamin D, B12, folate).
risk_factors: Family history of celiac disease or autoimmune disorders; type 1 diabetes; Down syndrome; autoimmune thyroid disease
prevention: No known prevention; early diagnosis and dietary management prevent complications such as malnutrition and osteoporosis.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood or 20s-30s

title: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus - Prevention Guide (Archived Copy)
content_text: Prevention strategies for Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus focus on modifiable risk factors. Maintaining healthy body weight, regular physical activity (150 minutes/week), balanced diet low in refined sugars, and routine screening for at-risk individuals. Individuals with additional risk factors (Obesity; family history of diabetes; age over 45; physical inactivity; history of gestational diabetes; high blood pressure) should consult a healthcare provider for personalized guidance.
symptoms: Increased thirst; frequent urination; fatigue; blurred vision; slow-healing sores; unexplained weight loss; numbness or tingling in hands or feet
treatments: First-line management includes lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, weight loss) and metformin. Additional agents such as SGLT2 inhibitors, GLP-1 receptor agonists, sulfonylureas, or insulin are added based on glycemic targets and comorbidities.
risk_factors: Obesity; family history of diabetes; age over 45; physical inactivity; history of gestational diabetes; high blood pressure
prevention: Maintaining healthy body weight, regular physical activity (150 minutes/week), balanced diet low in refined sugars, and routine screening for at-risk individuals.

title: What is Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease?
content_text: Q: What is Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease? A: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease is a progressive lung disease characterized by chronic airflow limitation, primarily caused by long-term exposure to irritants such as cigarette smoke, leading to chronic bronchitis and/or emphysema.
symptoms: Chronic cough with mucus production; shortness of breath, especially during physical activity; wheezing; chest tightness; frequent respiratory infections
treatments: Bronchodilators (short and long-acting), inhaled corticosteroids for frequent exacerbations, pulmonary rehabilitation, oxygen therapy in advanced stages, and smoking cessation as the most critical intervention.
risk_factors: Smoking (primary cause); long-term exposure to air pollutants or occupational dust/chemicals; genetic factors (alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency); history of childhood respiratory infections
prevention: Avoiding smoking and secondhand smoke, reducing occupational and environmental exposure to lung irritants, and vaccination against respiratory infections.

title: Iron Deficiency Anemia - Prevention Guide
content_text: Prevention strategies for Iron Deficiency Anemia focus on modifiable risk factors. Eating iron-rich foods (lean meat, beans, fortified cereals), pairing with vitamin C for absorption, and screening in high-risk groups such as pregnant women. Individuals with additional risk factors (Poor dietary iron intake; heavy menstrual periods; pregnancy; gastrointestinal bleeding; chronic conditions affecting absorption (e.g., celiac disease)) should consult a healthcare provider for personalized guidance.
symptoms: Fatigue; pale skin; shortness of breath; dizziness; brittle nails; headaches; cold hands and feet; unusual cravings for non-nutritive substances (pica)
treatments: Oral or intravenous iron supplementation, dietary modification to increase iron-rich foods, and treatment of underlying causes such as bleeding sources.
risk_factors: Poor dietary iron intake; heavy menstrual periods; pregnancy; gastrointestinal bleeding; chronic conditions affecting absorption (e.g., celiac disease)
prevention: Eating iron-rich foods (lean meat, beans, fortified cereals), pairing with vitamin C for absorption, and screening in high-risk groups such as pregnant women.
common_age_group: Women of reproductive age; young children

title: What are the common symptoms of Osteoarthritis?
content_text: Q: What are the common symptoms of Osteoarthritis? A: Common symptoms include joint pain that worsens with activity; stiffness, especially after rest; loss of flexibility; grating sensation; bone spurs; swelling around the joint.
symptoms: Joint pain that worsens with activity; stiffness, especially after rest; loss of flexibility; grating sensation; bone spurs; swelling around the joint
treatments: Weight management, physical therapy, pain relievers (acetaminophen, NSAIDs), and in advanced cases, joint replacement surgery.
risk_factors: Older age; obesity; joint injury; repetitive stress on joints; genetics; female sex
prevention: Maintaining healthy weight, regular low-impact exercise, avoiding repetitive joint stress, and proper injury management.

title: Can Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye) be prevented?
content_text: Q: Can Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye) be prevented? A: Frequent handwashing, avoiding touching or rubbing the eyes, not sharing personal items such as towels, and proper contact lens hygiene.
symptoms: Redness in the white of the eye; increased tear production; itchy or burning eyes; discharge (watery, mucous, or pus-like); blurred vision; crusting of eyelids
treatments: Viral conjunctivitis is managed with supportive care (cool compresses, artificial tears). Bacterial conjunctivitis may require antibiotic eye drops. Allergic conjunctivitis is treated with antihistamine eye drops.
risk_factors: Close contact with infected individuals; allergies; contact lens use; exposure to irritants; recent upper respiratory infection
prevention: Frequent handwashing, avoiding touching or rubbing the eyes, not sharing personal items such as towels, and proper contact lens hygiene.

title: Migraine - Symptom Guide
content_text: Patients with Migraine commonly present with the following symptoms: Throbbing or pulsing headache, often one-sided; nausea and vomiting; sensitivity to light and sound; visual disturbances (aura); dizziness. Symptom severity and combination vary by individual and disease stage.
symptoms: Throbbing or pulsing headache, often one-sided; nausea and vomiting; sensitivity to light and sound; visual disturbances (aura); dizziness
treatments: Acute treatment includes NSAIDs or triptans for moderate to severe attacks. Preventive therapy (beta-blockers, anticonvulsants, CGRP inhibitors) is considered for frequent migraines.
risk_factors: Family history of migraine; hormonal changes (especially in women); stress; certain foods or food additives; changes in sleep patterns; sensory stimuli
prevention: Identifying and avoiding personal triggers, maintaining regular sleep and meal schedules, stress reduction techniques, and preventive medication when prescribed.

title: Psoriasis - Symptom Guide
content_text: Patients with Psoriasis commonly present with the following symptoms: Red patches of skin covered with thick, silvery scales; dry, cracked skin that may bleed; itching, burning, or soreness; thickened or ridged nails; stiff and swollen joints (in psoriatic arthritis). Symptom severity and combination vary by individual and disease stage.
symptoms: Red patches of skin covered with thick, silvery scales; dry, cracked skin that may bleed; itching, burning, or soreness; thickened or ridged nails; stiff and swollen joints (in psoriatic arthritis)
treatments: Topical treatments (corticosteroids, vitamin D analogs) for mild cases, phototherapy for moderate cases, and systemic agents or biologics for moderate-to-severe disease.
risk_factors: Family history of psoriasis; smoking; obesity; stress; certain infections; certain medications (e.g., lithium, beta-blockers)
prevention: No definitive prevention exists; managing triggers such as stress, smoking, and certain medications may reduce flare frequency.

title: Obesity - Symptom Guide
content_text: Patients with Obesity commonly present with the following symptoms: Excess body fat; breathlessness on exertion; increased sweating; difficulty with physical activity; joint and back pain; low self-esteem. Symptom severity and combination vary by individual and disease stage.
symptoms: Excess body fat; breathlessness on exertion; increased sweating; difficulty with physical activity; joint and back pain; low self-esteem
treatments: Comprehensive lifestyle intervention including dietary changes, increased physical activity, and behavioral therapy. Pharmacotherapy (e.g., GLP-1 agonists) or bariatric surgery may be considered for severe cases.
risk_factors: Poor diet; physical inactivity; genetics; certain medications; insufficient sleep; high stress levels; socioeconomic factors
prevention: Balanced nutrition, regular physical activity, adequate sleep, and community/environmental support for healthy choices.

title: What is Asthma?
content_text: Q: What is Asthma? A: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory disease of the airways causing recurring episodes of wheezing, breathlessness, chest tightness, and coughing due to airway hyperresponsiveness and reversible airflow obstruction.
symptoms: Wheezing; shortness of breath; chest tightness; coughing, especially at night or early morning; difficulty sleeping due to breathing problems
treatments: Controller medications (inhaled corticosteroids, long-acting beta-agonists) for long-term control, plus short-acting bronchodilators (e.g., albuterol) for acute symptom relief. Avoidance of known triggers is essential.
risk_factors: Family history of asthma or allergies; exposure to tobacco smoke; air pollution; occupational exposure to irritants; respiratory infections in childhood
prevention: Avoiding known allergens and irritants, controlling indoor air quality, getting recommended vaccinations, and adherence to prescribed controller therapy.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood

title: Tuberculosis - Clinical Decision Support Summary
content_text: Clinical decision support summary for Tuberculosis (ICD-10: A15). Category: Infectious. Typical severity: Severe. Chronicity: typically an acute (short-term) condition. Transmission risk: considered contagious and can spread between people. Primary risk factors: Close contact with an infected person; weakened immune system (e.g., HIV); living in or traveling to areas with high TB rates; healthcare work; substance use. Recommended initial management: Standard treatment involves a 6-9 month course of multiple antibiotics (isoniazid, rifampin, ethambutol, pyrazinamide), with directly observed therapy recommended to ensure adherence and prevent drug resistance.
symptoms: Persistent cough lasting three weeks or longer, sometimes with blood; chest pain; unintentional weight loss; fatigue; fever; night sweats; loss of appetite
treatments: Standard treatment involves a 6-9 month course of multiple antibiotics (isoniazid, rifampin, ethambutol, pyrazinamide), with directly observed therapy recommended to ensure adherence and prevent drug resistance.
risk_factors: Close contact with an infected person; weakened immune system (e.g., HIV); living in or traveling to areas with high TB rates; healthcare work; substance use
prevention: BCG vaccination in countries with high TB prevalence, prompt identification and treatment of active cases, and infection control measures in healthcare settings.
common_age_group: All ages, higher burden in low-resource settings

title: Malaria - Clinical Decision Support Summary
content_text: Clinical decision support summary for Malaria (ICD-10: B54). Category: Infectious. Typical severity: Severe. Chronicity: typically an acute (short-term) condition. Transmission risk: not considered contagious and does not spread between people. Primary risk factors: Living in or traveling to malaria-endemic regions; lack of access to preventive measures (bed nets, prophylaxis); pregnancy; young age. Recommended initial management: Artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs) are the standard treatment for uncomplicated malaria. Severe malaria requires hospitalization and intravenous antimalarial drugs.
symptoms: Fever and chills; headache; nausea and vomiting; muscle pain and fatigue; sweating; in severe cases, confusion, seizures, or difficulty breathing
treatments: Artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs) are the standard treatment for uncomplicated malaria. Severe malaria requires hospitalization and intravenous antimalarial drugs.
risk_factors: Living in or traveling to malaria-endemic regions; lack of access to preventive measures (bed nets, prophylaxis); pregnancy; young age
prevention: Use of insecticide-treated bed nets, indoor residual spraying, antimalarial prophylaxis for travelers, and prompt diagnosis and treatment of cases.
common_age_group: Children under 5 in endemic regions; travelers

title: Is Asthma a chronic condition?
content_text: Q: Is Asthma a chronic condition? A: Asthma is generally classified as a chronic (long-term) condition requiring ongoing management.
symptoms: Wheezing; shortness of breath; chest tightness; coughing, especially at night or early morning; difficulty sleeping due to breathing problems
treatments: Controller medications (inhaled corticosteroids, long-acting beta-agonists) for long-term control, plus short-acting bronchodilators (e.g., albuterol) for acute symptom relief. Avoidance of known triggers is essential.
risk_factors: Family history of asthma or allergies; exposure to tobacco smoke; air pollution; occupational exposure to irritants; respiratory infections in childhood
prevention: Avoiding known allergens and irritants, controlling indoor air quality, getting recommended vaccinations, and adherence to prescribed controller therapy.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood

title: What are the common symptoms of Hypothyroidism?
content_text: Q: What are the common symptoms of Hypothyroidism? A: Common symptoms include fatigue; weight gain; cold intolerance; dry skin; constipation; hair thinning; depression; muscle weakness; slowed heart rate.
symptoms: Fatigue; weight gain; cold intolerance; dry skin; constipation; hair thinning; depression; muscle weakness; slowed heart rate
treatments: Lifelong thyroid hormone replacement therapy (levothyroxine), with regular monitoring of thyroid hormone levels to adjust dosage.
risk_factors: Female sex; age over 60; family history of thyroid disease; autoimmune conditions; previous thyroid surgery or radiation treatment
prevention: No specific prevention exists for autoimmune causes; adequate dietary iodine intake helps prevent iodine-deficiency-related hypothyroidism.

title: Epilepsy - Symptom Guide
content_text: Patients with Epilepsy commonly present with the following symptoms: Recurrent seizures (varying from brief staring spells to convulsions); temporary confusion; uncontrollable jerking movements; loss of consciousness; psychic symptoms such as fear or anxiety before a seizure. Symptom severity and combination vary by individual and disease stage.
symptoms: Recurrent seizures (varying from brief staring spells to convulsions); temporary confusion; uncontrollable jerking movements; loss of consciousness; psychic symptoms such as fear or anxiety before a seizure
treatments: Antiepileptic drugs (e.g., levetiracetam, valproate, lamotrigine) are the mainstay of treatment, with surgery, dietary therapy (ketogenic diet), or neurostimulation devices considered for drug-resistant cases.
risk_factors: Family history of epilepsy; brain injury or trauma; stroke; brain infections; developmental disorders; prenatal injury
prevention: Preventing head injuries, managing cardiovascular risk factors to reduce stroke risk, and prompt treatment of infections that can affect the brain.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood or after age 60

title: Migraine - Prevention Guide
content_text: Prevention strategies for Migraine focus on modifiable risk factors. Identifying and avoiding personal triggers, maintaining regular sleep and meal schedules, stress reduction techniques, and preventive medication when prescribed. Individuals with additional risk factors (Family history of migraine; hormonal changes (especially in women); stress; certain foods or food additives; changes in sleep patterns; sensory stimuli) should consult a healthcare provider for personalized guidance.

treatments: Acute treatment includes NSAIDs or triptans for moderate to severe attacks. Preventive therapy (beta-blockers, anticonvulsants, CGRP inhibitors) is considered for frequent migraines.
risk_factors: Family history of migraine; hormonal changes (especially in women); stress; certain foods or food additives; changes in sleep patterns; sensory stimuli
prevention: Identifying and avoiding personal triggers, maintaining regular sleep and meal schedules, stress reduction techniques, and preventive medication when prescribed.

title: Is Peptic Ulcer Disease contagious?
content_text: Q: Is Peptic Ulcer Disease contagious? A: Based on current medical guidance, Peptic Ulcer Disease is not contagious and does not spread between people.
symptoms: Burning stomach pain; bloating; heartburn; nausea; intolerance to fatty foods; in severe cases, dark stools or vomiting blood
treatments: Antibiotic regimens to eradicate H. pylori when present, proton pump inhibitors to reduce stomach acid, and discontinuation of NSAIDs when possible.
risk_factors: H. pylori infection; regular use of NSAIDs; smoking; excessive alcohol consumption; stress (as a contributing factor)
prevention: Limiting NSAID use, avoiding excessive alcohol, not smoking, and prompt treatment of H. pylori infection.

title: Can Influenza be prevented?
content_text: Q: Can Influenza be prevented? A: Annual influenza vaccination, frequent handwashing, avoiding close contact with sick individuals, and covering coughs and sneezes.
symptoms: Fever or feeling feverish; chills; cough; sore throat; runny or stuffy nose; muscle or body aches; headaches; fatigue
treatments: Supportive care (rest, fluids, antipyretics) for most cases. Antiviral medications such as oseltamivir may be prescribed within 48 hours of symptom onset for high-risk patients.
risk_factors: Age under 5 or over 65; pregnancy; chronic medical conditions (asthma, diabetes, heart disease); weakened immune system; close contact with infected individuals
prevention: Annual influenza vaccination, frequent handwashing, avoiding close contact with sick individuals, and covering coughs and sneezes.

title: Is Tuberculosis contagious?
content_text: Q: Is Tuberculosis contagious? A: Based on current medical guidance, Tuberculosis is contagious and can spread between people.
symptoms: Persistent cough lasting three weeks or longer, sometimes with blood; chest pain; unintentional weight loss; fatigue; fever; night sweats; loss of appetite
treatments: Standard treatment involves a 6-9 month course of multiple antibiotics (isoniazid, rifampin, ethambutol, pyrazinamide), with directly observed therapy recommended to ensure adherence and prevent drug resistance.
risk_factors: Close contact with an infected person; weakened immune system (e.g., HIV); living in or traveling to areas with high TB rates; healthcare work; substance use

title: Influenza - Clinical Decision Support Summary
content_text: Clinical decision support summary for Influenza (ICD-10: J11). Category: Infectious. Typical severity: Mild to Severe. Chronicity: typically an acute (short-term) condition. Transmission risk: considered contagious and can spread between people. Primary risk factors: Age under 5 or over 65; pregnancy; chronic medical conditions (asthma, diabetes, heart disease); weakened immune system; close contact with infected individuals. Recommended initial management: Supportive care (rest, fluids, antipyretics) for most cases. Antiviral medications such as oseltamivir may be prescribed within 48 hours of symptom onset for high-risk patients.
symptoms: Fever or feeling feverish; chills; cough; sore throat; runny or stuffy nose; muscle or body aches; headaches; fatigue
treatments: Supportive care (rest, fluids, antipyretics) for most cases. Antiviral medications such as oseltamivir may be prescribed within 48 hours of symptom onset for high-risk patients.
risk_factors: Age under 5 or over 65; pregnancy; chronic medical conditions (asthma, diabetes, heart disease); weakened immune system; close contact with infected individuals
prevention: Annual influenza vaccination, frequent handwashing, avoiding close contact with sick individuals, and covering coughs and sneezes.

title: What is Celiac Disease?
content_text: Q: What is Celiac Disease? A: Celiac Disease is an autoimmune disorder in which ingestion of gluten triggers an immune response that damages the small intestine lining, impairing nutrient absorption.
symptoms: Diarrhea or constipation; bloating and gas; abdominal pain; fatigue; weight loss; anemia; skin rash (dermatitis herpetiformis); in children, failure to thrive
treatments: The only effective treatment is a strict, lifelong gluten-free diet. Nutritional supplementation may be needed to correct deficiencies (iron, vitamin D, B12, folate).
risk_factors: Family history of celiac disease or autoimmune disorders; type 1 diabetes; Down syndrome; autoimmune thyroid disease
prevention: No known prevention; early diagnosis and dietary management prevent complications such as malnutrition and osteoporosis.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood or 20s-30s

title: Allergic Rhinitis - Overview
content_text: An allergic response causing inflammation of the nasal passages, triggered by airborne allergens such as pollen, dust mites, or pet dander, resulting in cold-like symptoms.
symptoms: Sneezing; runny or stuffy nose; itchy or watery eyes; itchy throat; postnasal drip; fatigue
treatments: Antihistamines, intranasal corticosteroids, decongestants for short-term relief, and allergen immunotherapy for long-term management of severe cases.
risk_factors: Family history of allergies; other allergic conditions (asthma, eczema); exposure to environmental allergens; early childhood exposure to tobacco smoke
prevention: Avoiding known allergens, using air purifiers, keeping windows closed during high pollen seasons, and regular cleaning to reduce dust mites.
common_age_group: All ages, often begins in childhood or adolescence

title: How is Generalized Anxiety Disorder treated?
content_text: Q: How is Generalized Anxiety Disorder treated? A: Cognitive behavioral therapy is highly effective; medications such as SSRIs, SNRIs, or buspirone may be used. Relaxation techniques and lifestyle changes support management.
symptoms: Excessive worry; restlessness; fatigue; difficulty concentrating; irritability; muscle tension; sleep disturbances
treatments: Cognitive behavioral therapy is highly effective; medications such as SSRIs, SNRIs, or buspirone may be used. Relaxation techniques and lifestyle changes support management.
risk_factors: Family history of anxiety disorders; history of trauma; chronic health conditions; personality traits; substance use
prevention: Stress management techniques, regular physical activity, limiting caffeine and alcohol, and adequate sleep hygiene.

title: What causes or increases the risk of Hyperlipidemia?
content_text: Q: What causes or increases the risk of Hyperlipidemia? A: Key risk factors include diet high in saturated fats; obesity; physical inactivity; smoking; diabetes; family history of high cholesterol; age.
symptoms: Usually asymptomatic; in severe cases, fatty deposits around the eyes or joints (xanthomas); occasionally associated with pancreatitis symptoms if triglycerides are very high
treatments: Lifestyle modification (diet, exercise, weight loss) as first-line therapy, with statins as the primary pharmacologic treatment; additional agents (ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors) for high-risk patients.
risk_factors: Diet high in saturated fats; obesity; physical inactivity; smoking; diabetes; family history of high cholesterol; age
prevention: Heart-healthy diet low in saturated and trans fats, regular physical activity, maintaining healthy weight, and routine lipid screening.

title: Pneumonia - Prevention Guide
content_text: Prevention strategies for Pneumonia focus on modifiable risk factors. Vaccination (pneumococcal, influenza), good hygiene, avoiding smoking, and managing underlying chronic conditions. Individuals with additional risk factors (Age under 2 or over 65; chronic diseases (asthma, COPD, heart disease); weakened immune system; smoking; recent respiratory infection; hospitalization) should consult a healthcare provider for personalized guidance.
symptoms: Cough with phlegm; fever; chills; shortness of breath; chest pain when breathing or coughing; fatigue; confusion (especially in older adults)
treatments: Bacterial pneumonia is treated with antibiotics; viral pneumonia is managed with supportive care and antivirals when appropriate. Hospitalization may be required for severe cases.
risk_factors: Age under 2 or over 65; chronic diseases (asthma, COPD, heart disease); weakened immune system; smoking; recent respiratory infection; hospitalization
prevention: Vaccination (pneumococcal, influenza), good hygiene, avoiding smoking, and managing underlying chronic conditions.

title: What are the common symptoms of Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis)?
content_text: Q: What are the common symptoms of Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis)? A: Common symptoms include dry, scaly skin; intense itching; red to brownish-gray patches; small raised bumps that may leak fluid when scratched; thickened, cracked skin.
symptoms: Dry, scaly skin; intense itching; red to brownish-gray patches; small raised bumps that may leak fluid when scratched; thickened, cracked skin
treatments: Regular moisturizing, topical corticosteroids for flare-ups, antihistamines for itching, and identification/avoidance of triggers. Severe cases may require systemic immunomodulators.
risk_factors: Family history of eczema, asthma, or hay fever; dry skin; environmental irritants; stress; certain climates
prevention: Regular moisturizing, avoiding harsh soaps and known irritants, using lukewarm water for bathing, and wearing soft, breathable fabrics.
common_age_group: Children, with many cases persisting into adulthood

title: Rheumatoid Arthritis - Symptom Guide
content_text: Patients with Rheumatoid Arthritis commonly present with the following symptoms: Joint pain and swelling, often symmetrical; morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes; fatigue; low-grade fever; joint deformity in advanced stages. Symptom severity and combination vary by individual and disease stage.
symptoms: Joint pain and swelling, often symmetrical; morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes; fatigue; low-grade fever; joint deformity in advanced stages
treatments: Disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs) such as methotrexate, biologic agents, NSAIDs for symptom relief, and physical therapy to maintain joint function.
risk_factors: Female sex; family history of autoimmune disease; smoking; obesity; age between 30 and 60
prevention: No definitive prevention exists, but smoking cessation and maintaining healthy weight may reduce risk; early diagnosis and treatment slow disease progression.

title: Can Essential Hypertension be prevented?
content_text: Q: Can Essential Hypertension be prevented? A: Reducing dietary sodium, maintaining healthy weight, limiting alcohol, regular aerobic exercise, and managing stress.
symptoms: Often asymptomatic; occasional headaches; shortness of breath; nosebleeds; dizziness in severe cases
treatments: Lifestyle changes (DASH diet, sodium reduction, exercise, weight management) combined with antihypertensive medications such as ACE inhibitors, ARBs, calcium channel blockers, or thiazide diuretics depending on patient profile.
risk_factors: High sodium intake; obesity; sedentary lifestyle; excessive alcohol consumption; chronic stress; family history; aging
prevention: Reducing dietary sodium, maintaining healthy weight, limiting alcohol, regular aerobic exercise, and managing stress.
source_name: American Heart Association - High Blood Pressure

title: Osteoarthritis - Overview
content_text: The most common form of arthritis, caused by the gradual breakdown of cartilage cushioning the ends of bones, resulting in pain, stiffness, and reduced joint mobility, most often affecting hands, knees, hips, and spine.
symptoms: Joint pain that worsens with activity; stiffness, especially after rest; loss of flexibility; grating sensation; bone spurs; swelling around the joint
treatments: Weight management, physical therapy, pain relievers (acetaminophen, NSAIDs), and in advanced cases, joint replacement surgery.
risk_factors: Older age; obesity; joint injury; repetitive stress on joints; genetics; female sex
prevention: Maintaining healthy weight, regular low-impact exercise, avoiding repetitive joint stress, and proper injury management.

title: Sleep Apnea - Overview
content_text: A sleep disorder characterized by repeated interruptions in breathing during sleep, most commonly due to relaxation of throat muscles causing airway obstruction (obstructive sleep apnea).
symptoms: Loud snoring; episodes of stopped breathing observed by others; gasping for air during sleep; excessive daytime sleepiness; morning headaches; difficulty concentrating; irritability
treatments: Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) therapy is the standard treatment. Weight loss, positional therapy, oral appliances, and in select cases, surgery, may also be used.
risk_factors: Obesity; older age; male sex; large neck circumference; family history; smoking; nasal congestion; use of alcohol or sedatives
prevention: Maintaining healthy weight, avoiding alcohol and sedatives before sleep, sleeping on one's side, and treating nasal congestion.

content_text: Q: Is Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis) contagious? A: Based on current medical guidance, Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis) is not contagious and does not spread between people.
symptoms: Dry, scaly skin; intense itching; red to brownish-gray patches; small raised bumps that may leak fluid when scratched; thickened, cracked skin
treatments: Regular moisturizing, topical corticosteroids for flare-ups, antihistamines for itching, and identification/avoidance of triggers. Severe cases may require systemic immunomodulators.
risk_factors: Family history of eczema, asthma, or hay fever; dry skin; environmental irritants; stress; certain climates
prevention: Regular moisturizing, avoiding harsh soaps and known irritants, using lukewarm water for bathing, and wearing soft, breathable fabrics.
common_age_group: Children, with many cases persisting into adulthood

title: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease - Symptom Guide
content_text: Patients with Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease commonly present with the following symptoms: Heartburn; regurgitation of food or sour liquid; chest pain; difficulty swallowing; chronic cough; sensation of a lump in the throat. Symptom severity and combination vary by individual and disease stage.
symptoms: Heartburn; regurgitation of food or sour liquid; chest pain; difficulty swallowing; chronic cough; sensation of a lump in the throat
treatments: Lifestyle modifications (weight loss, smaller meals, avoiding trigger foods) combined with proton pump inhibitors or H2 receptor blockers. Severe or refractory cases may require surgical intervention.
risk_factors: Obesity; hiatal hernia; pregnancy; smoking; certain medications; large meals or eating late at night
prevention: Avoiding large or fatty meals, not lying down immediately after eating, weight management, and limiting alcohol and caffeine intake.

title: Major Depressive Disorder - Symptom Guide
content_text: Patients with Major Depressive Disorder commonly present with the following symptoms: Persistent sad or empty mood; loss of interest in hobbies; fatigue or low energy; difficulty concentrating; changes in appetite or weight; sleep disturbances; thoughts of death or suicide. Symptom severity and combination vary by individual and disease stage.
symptoms: Persistent sad or empty mood; loss of interest in hobbies; fatigue or low energy; difficulty concentrating; changes in appetite or weight; sleep disturbances; thoughts of death or suicide
treatments: Psychotherapy (cognitive behavioral therapy, interpersonal therapy) and/or antidepressant medications (SSRIs, SNRIs). Severe cases may require combination therapy or, rarely, electroconvulsive therapy.
risk_factors: Family history of depression; history of trauma or abuse; chronic illness; substance use; major life changes or stress; certain medications
prevention: Stress management, strong social support networks, regular physical activity, adequate sleep, and early intervention for at-risk individuals.

title: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease - Symptom Guide
content_text: Patients with Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease commonly present with the following symptoms: Chronic cough with mucus production; shortness of breath, especially during physical activity; wheezing; chest tightness; frequent respiratory infections. Symptom severity and combination vary by individual and disease stage.
symptoms: Chronic cough with mucus production; shortness of breath, especially during physical activity; wheezing; chest tightness; frequent respiratory infections
treatments: Bronchodilators (short and long-acting), inhaled corticosteroids for frequent exacerbations, pulmonary rehabilitation, oxygen therapy in advanced stages, and smoking cessation as the most critical intervention.
risk_factors: Smoking (primary cause); long-term exposure to air pollutants or occupational dust/chemicals; genetic factors (alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency); history of childhood respiratory infections
prevention: Avoiding smoking and secondhand smoke, reducing occupational and environmental exposure to lung irritants, and vaccination against respiratory infections.

title: How is Sleep Apnea treated?
content_text: Q: How is Sleep Apnea treated? A: Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) therapy is the standard treatment. Weight loss, positional therapy, oral appliances, and in select cases, surgery, may also be used.
symptoms: Loud snoring; episodes of stopped breathing observed by others; gasping for air during sleep; excessive daytime sleepiness; morning headaches; difficulty concentrating; irritability
treatments: Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) therapy is the standard treatment. Weight loss, positional therapy, oral appliances, and in select cases, surgery, may also be used.
risk_factors: Obesity; older age; male sex; large neck circumference; family history; smoking; nasal congestion; use of alcohol or sedatives
prevention: Maintaining healthy weight, avoiding alcohol and sedatives before sleep, sleeping on one's side, and treating nasal congestion.

title: Celiac Disease - Overview
content_text: An autoimmune disorder in which ingestion of gluten triggers an immune response that damages the small intestine lining, impairing nutrient absorption.
symptoms: Diarrhea or constipation; bloating and gas; abdominal pain; fatigue; weight loss; anemia; skin rash (dermatitis herpetiformis); in children, failure to thrive
treatments: The only effective treatment is a strict, lifelong gluten-free diet. Nutritional supplementation may be needed to correct deficiencies (iron, vitamin D, B12, folate).
risk_factors: Family history of celiac disease or autoimmune disorders; type 1 diabetes; Down syndrome; autoimmune thyroid disease
prevention: No known prevention; early diagnosis and dietary management prevent complications such as malnutrition and osteoporosis.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood or 20s-30s

title: Who is most commonly affected by Generalized Anxiety Disorder?
content_text: Q: Who is most commonly affected by Generalized Anxiety Disorder? A: Generalized Anxiety Disorder most commonly affects Adults 18-44.
symptoms: Excessive worry; restlessness; fatigue; difficulty concentrating; irritability; muscle tension; sleep disturbances
treatments: Cognitive behavioral therapy is highly effective; medications such as SSRIs, SNRIs, or buspirone may be used. Relaxation techniques and lifestyle changes support management.
risk_factors: Family history of anxiety disorders; history of trauma; chronic health conditions; personality traits; substance use
prevention: Stress management techniques, regular physical activity, limiting caffeine and alcohol, and adequate sleep hygiene.

title: Is Epilepsy contagious?
content_text: Q: Is Epilepsy contagious? A: Based on current medical guidance, Epilepsy is not contagious and does not spread between people.
symptoms: Recurrent seizures (varying from brief staring spells to convulsions); temporary confusion; uncontrollable jerking movements; loss of consciousness; psychic symptoms such as fear or anxiety before a seizure
treatments: Antiepileptic drugs (e.g., levetiracetam, valproate, lamotrigine) are the mainstay of treatment, with surgery, dietary therapy (ketogenic diet), or neurostimulation devices considered for drug-resistant cases.
risk_factors: Family history of epilepsy; brain injury or trauma; stroke; brain infections; developmental disorders; prenatal injury
prevention: Preventing head injuries, managing cardiovascular risk factors to reduce stroke risk, and prompt treatment of infections that can affect the brain.
common_age_group: All ages, often diagnosed in childhood or after age 60
